infobox.key: [Coat of arms](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_the_United_Kingdom "Coat of arms of the United Kingdom"):
infobox.value: Used in relation to [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland") (right) and elsewhere (left) | |

(**UK**) or **Britain**,[[m]](#cite_note-36) is a country in [northwestern Europe](/wiki/Northwestern_Europe "Northwestern Europe"), off the coast of [the continental mainland](/wiki/European_mainland "European mainland"). It comprises [England](/wiki/England "England"), [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland"), [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales") and [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland"),[[n]](#cite_note-37) with a population of over 69 million in 2024. The UK includes the island of [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain"), the north-eastern part of the island of [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland"), and most of [the smaller islands](/wiki/List_of_islands_of_the_United_Kingdom "List of islands of the United Kingdom") within the [British Isles](/wiki/British_Isles "British Isles"), covering 94,354 square miles (244,376 km2).[[f]](#cite_note-ONSArea-17) It shares [a land border](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland%E2%80%93United_Kingdom_border "Republic of Ireland–United Kingdom border") with the [Republic of Ireland](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland "Republic of Ireland") and is surrounded by the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean"), the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea"), the [English Channel](/wiki/English_Channel "English Channel"), the [Celtic Sea](/wiki/Celtic_Sea "Celtic Sea") and the [Irish Sea](/wiki/Irish_Sea "Irish Sea"), while maintaining sovereignty over the [Crown Dependencies](/wiki/Crown_Dependencies "Crown Dependencies") and the [British Overseas Territories](/wiki/British_Overseas_Territories "British Overseas Territories"). The capital and largest city of England and the UK is [London](/wiki/London "London"); [Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh "Edinburgh"), [Cardiff](/wiki/Cardiff "Cardiff") and [Belfast](/wiki/Belfast "Belfast") are the national capitals of Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland.

For other uses, see [UK (disambiguation)](/wiki/UK_(disambiguation) "UK (disambiguation)") and [United Kingdom (disambiguation)](/wiki/United_Kingdom_(disambiguation) "United Kingdom (disambiguation)"). The **United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland**, commonly known as the **United Kingdom** (**UK**) or **Britain**,[[m]](#cite_note-36) is a country in [northwestern Europe](/wiki/Northwestern_Europe "Northwestern Europe"), off the coast of [the continental mainland](/wiki/European_mainland "European mainland"). It comprises [England](/wiki/England "England"), [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland"), [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales") and [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland"),[[n]](#cite_note-37) with a population of over 69 million in 2024. The UK includes the island of [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain"), the north-eastern part of the island of [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland"), and most of [the smaller islands](/wiki/List_of_islands_of_the_United_Kingdom "List of islands of the United Kingdom") within the [British Isles](/wiki/British_Isles "British Isles"), covering 94,354 square miles (244,376 km2).[[f]](#cite_note-ONSArea-17) It shares [a land border](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland%E2%80%93United_Kingdom_border "Republic of Ireland–United Kingdom border") with the [Republic of Ireland](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland "Republic of Ireland") and is surrounded by the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean"), the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea"), the [English Channel](/wiki/English_Channel "English Channel"), the [Celtic Sea](/wiki/Celtic_Sea "Celtic Sea") and the [Irish Sea](/wiki/Irish_Sea "Irish Sea"), while maintaining sovereignty over the [Crown Dependencies](/wiki/Crown_Dependencies "Crown Dependencies") and the [British Overseas Territories](/wiki/British_Overseas_Territories "British Overseas Territories"). The capital and largest city of England and the UK is [London](/wiki/London "London"); [Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh "Edinburgh"), [Cardiff](/wiki/Cardiff "Cardiff") and [Belfast](/wiki/Belfast "Belfast") are the national capitals of Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland.

Britain has been continuously inhabited since the [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic"). In AD 43 the [Roman conquest of Britain](/wiki/Roman_conquest_of_Britain "Roman conquest of Britain") began. The [Roman departure](/wiki/End_of_Roman_rule_in_Britain "End of Roman rule in Britain") between 383 and 410 was followed by [Anglo-Saxon settlement](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_settlement_of_Britain "Anglo-Saxon settlement of Britain") beginning around 450. In 1066 the [Normans conquered England](/wiki/Norman_Conquest "Norman Conquest"). Over the 17th century the role of the [British monarchy](/wiki/British_monarchy "British monarchy") was reduced, particularly as a result of the [English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War"). In 1707 the Kingdom of England and the [Kingdom of Scotland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Scotland "Kingdom of Scotland") united under the [Treaty of Union](/wiki/Treaty_of_Union "Treaty of Union") to create the [Kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain"). The [Acts of Union 1800](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1800 "Acts of Union 1800") incorporated the [Kingdom of Ireland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Ireland "Kingdom of Ireland") to create the [United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland") in 1801. Most of Ireland [seceded](/wiki/Partition_of_Ireland "Partition of Ireland") from the UK in 1922 as the [Irish Free State](/wiki/Irish_Free_State "Irish Free State"), and the [Royal and Parliamentary Titles Act 1927](/wiki/Royal_and_Parliamentary_Titles_Act_1927 "Royal and Parliamentary Titles Act 1927") created the present United Kingdom.

The UK [became the first industrialised country](/wiki/History_of_industrialisation#Industrial_Revolution_in_Europe "History of industrialisation") and was the world's [foremost power](/wiki/Foremost_power "Foremost power") for the majority of the 19th and early 20th centuries, particularly during the *[Pax Britannica](/wiki/Pax_Britannica "Pax Britannica")* between 1815 and 1914. The British Empire was the leading [economic power](/wiki/Economic_power "Economic power") for most of the 19th century, a position supported by [its agricultural prosperity](/wiki/British_Agricultural_Revolution "British Agricultural Revolution"), its role as a dominant [trading nation](/wiki/Trading_nation "Trading nation"), a massive industrial capacity, [significant technological achievements](/wiki/List_of_British_innovations_and_discoveries_in_the_19th_century "List of British innovations and discoveries in the 19th century"), and the rise of [19th-century London](/wiki/19th-century_London "19th-century London") as the world's principal financial centre. At its height in the 1920s, the empire encompassed around a quarter of the world's landmass and population, and was the [largest in history](/wiki/List_of_largest_empires#Empires_at_their_greatest_extent "List of largest empires"). However, [its involvement in the First World War](/wiki/History_of_the_United_Kingdom_during_the_First_World_War "History of the United Kingdom during the First World War") and [in the Second World War](/wiki/Military_history_of_the_United_Kingdom_during_World_War_II "Military history of the United Kingdom during World War II") damaged [Britain's economic power](/wiki/Economy_of_the_British_Empire "Economy of the British Empire"), and a global wave of [decolonisation](/wiki/Decolonisation "Decolonisation") led to the independence of most British colonies.

The UK is a [constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy") and [parliamentary democracy](/wiki/Parliamentary_democracy "Parliamentary democracy")[[o]](#cite_note-39) with three distinct jurisdictions: [England and Wales](/wiki/English_law "English law"), [Scotland](/wiki/Scots_law "Scots law"), and [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Law_of_Northern_Ireland "Law of Northern Ireland"). Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland have their own governments and parliaments which control various [devolved](/wiki/Devolution_in_the_United_Kingdom "Devolution in the United Kingdom") matters. A [developed country](/wiki/Developed_country "Developed country") with [an advanced economy](/wiki/Economy_of_the_United_Kingdom "Economy of the United Kingdom"), the UK ranks amongst the [largest economies by nominal GDP](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(nominal) "List of countries by GDP (nominal)") and is one of the world's [largest exporters](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_exports "List of countries by exports") and [importers](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_imports "List of countries by imports"). As a [nuclear state](/wiki/Nuclear_state "Nuclear state") with one of the [highest defence budgets](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_military_expenditures "List of countries by military expenditures"), the UK maintains [one of the strongest militaries in Europe](/wiki/British_Armed_Forces "British Armed Forces"). [British culture](/wiki/Culture_of_the_United_Kingdom "Culture of the United Kingdom") is globally influential, notably in the [Anglosphere](/wiki/Anglosphere "Anglosphere") and [the Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations"); [its soft power influence](/wiki/Anglicisation "Anglicisation") is observable in the legal and political systems of many [former colonies](/wiki/List_of_countries_that_have_gained_independence_from_the_United_Kingdom "List of countries that have gained independence from the United Kingdom"), and in its exports [of language](/wiki/English-speaking_world "English-speaking world"), [literature](/wiki/British_literature "British literature"), [theatre](/wiki/Theatre_of_the_United_Kingdom "Theatre of the United Kingdom"), [cinema](/wiki/Cinema_of_the_United_Kingdom "Cinema of the United Kingdom"), [music](/wiki/Music_of_the_United_Kingdom "Music of the United Kingdom") and [sport](/wiki/Sport_in_the_United_Kingdom "Sport in the United Kingdom"). A [great power](/wiki/Great_power "Great power"), the UK is part of [numerous international organisations](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_the_United_Kingdom#International_organisations "Foreign relations of the United Kingdom").

## Etymology and terminology

See also: [Britain (place name)](/wiki/Britain_(place_name) "Britain (place name)") and [Terminology of the British Isles](/wiki/Terminology_of_the_British_Isles "Terminology of the British Isles")

The [Acts of Union 1707](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1707 "Acts of Union 1707") declared that the [Kingdom of England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England") and the [Kingdom of Scotland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Scotland "Kingdom of Scotland") were "United into One Kingdom by the Name of Great Britain".[[p]](#cite_note-40)[[25]](#cite_note-Acts_of_Union_1707-41) The term "United Kingdom" has occasionally been used for the former [Kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain"), although its official name from 1707 to 1800 was simply "Great Britain".[[26]](#cite_note-42) The [Acts of Union 1800](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1800 "Acts of Union 1800") formed the [United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland"). Following the [partition of Ireland](/wiki/Partition_of_Ireland "Partition of Ireland") and the independence of the [Irish Free State](/wiki/Irish_Free_State "Irish Free State") in 1922, which left [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland") as the only part of the island of Ireland within the United Kingdom, the name was changed in 1927 to the "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland".[[27]](#cite_note-43)

Although the United Kingdom is a sovereign country, England, Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland are widely referred to as [countries](/wiki/Countries_of_the_United_Kingdom "Countries of the United Kingdom").[[28]](#cite_note-44) The UK Prime Minister's website has used the phrase "countries within a country" to describe it.[[29]](#cite_note-page823-45) Some statistical summaries, such as those for the twelve [NUTS 1 regions](/wiki/NUTS_of_the_United_Kingdom "NUTS of the United Kingdom"), refer to Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland as "regions".[[30]](#cite_note-46) Northern Ireland is also referred to as a "province".[[31]](#cite_note-47) With regard to Northern Ireland, the descriptive name used "can be controversial, with the choice often revealing one's political preferences".[[32]](#cite_note-48)

The term "Great Britain" conventionally refers to the island of Great Britain, or politically to England, Scotland and Wales in combination.[[33]](#cite_note-49) It is sometimes used as a loose synonym for the United Kingdom as a whole.[[34]](#cite_note-50) The word *England* is occasionally used incorrectly to refer to the United Kingdom as a whole, a mistake principally made by people from outside the UK.[[35]](#cite_note-51)

The term "Britain" is used as a synonym for [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain"),[[36]](#cite_note-52)[[37]](#cite_note-Britain-Col-53) but also sometimes for the United Kingdom.[[38]](#cite_note-54)[[37]](#cite_note-Britain-Col-53) Usage is mixed: the [style guide](/wiki/Style_guide "Style guide") of the [UK Government](/wiki/Government_of_the_United_Kingdom "Government of the United Kingdom") prefers the term "UK" rather than "Britain" or "British" (except when referring to embassies[[q]](#cite_note-56)),[[40]](#cite_note-57) while other government documents acknowledge that both terms refer to the United Kingdom and that elsewhere "British government" is used at least as frequently as "United Kingdom government".[[41]](#cite_note-PCGN-uk-guide-58) The UK [Permanent Committee on Geographical Names](/wiki/Permanent_Committee_on_Geographical_Names_for_British_Official_Use "Permanent Committee on Geographical Names for British Official Use") recognises "United Kingdom", "UK" and "U.K." as shortened and abbreviated geopolitical terms for the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland in its toponymic guidelines; it does not list "Britain" but notes that "it is only the one specific nominal term 'Great Britain' which invariably excludes Northern Ireland".[[41]](#cite_note-PCGN-uk-guide-58) The [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") historically preferred to use "Britain" as shorthand only for Great Britain, though the present style guide does not take a position except that "Great Britain" excludes Northern Ireland.[[42]](#cite_note-59)

The adjective "British" is commonly used to refer to matters relating to the United Kingdom and is used in law to refer to United Kingdom citizenship and [nationality](/wiki/British_nationality_law "British nationality law").[[43]](#cite_note-60)[[r]](#cite_note-63) People of the United Kingdom use several different terms to describe their national identity and may identify themselves as being [British](/wiki/British_people "British people"), [English](/wiki/English_people "English people"), [Scottish](/wiki/Scottish_people "Scottish people"), [Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_people "Welsh people"), [Northern Irish](/wiki/People_of_Northern_Ireland "People of Northern Ireland") or [Irish](/wiki/Irish_people "Irish people");[[46]](#cite_note-64) or as having a combination of different national identities.[[47]](#cite_note-65)

### Prior to the Treaty of Union

Main articles: [History of England](/wiki/History_of_England "History of England"), [History of Wales](/wiki/History_of_Wales "History of Wales"), [History of Scotland](/wiki/History_of_Scotland "History of Scotland"), and [History of Ireland](/wiki/History_of_Ireland "History of Ireland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Stonehenge2007_07_30.jpg/250px-Stonehenge2007_07_30.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stonehenge2007_07_30.jpg)

[Stonehenge](/wiki/Stonehenge "Stonehenge") in [Wiltshire](/wiki/Wiltshire "Wiltshire") is a ring of stones, each about 4 m (13 ft) high, 2 m (7 ft) wide and 25 [tonnes](/wiki/Tonne "Tonne"), erected 2400–2200 BC.

Settlement by [Cro-Magnons](/wiki/Cro-Magnons "Cro-Magnons") of what was to become the United Kingdom occurred in waves beginning by about 30,000 years ago.[[48]](#cite_note-66) The island has been continuously inhabited since the last retreat of ice around 11,500 years ago.[[49]](#cite_note-67) By the end of the [region's prehistoric period](/wiki/Prehistoric_Britain "Prehistoric Britain"), the population is thought to have belonged largely to a culture termed [Insular Celtic](/wiki/Insular_Celts "Insular Celts"), comprising [Brittonic Britain](/wiki/Britons_(historical) "Britons (historical)") and [Gaelic Ireland](/wiki/Gaelic_Ireland "Gaelic Ireland").[[50]](#cite_note-68)

The [Roman conquest](/wiki/Roman_conquest_of_Britain "Roman conquest of Britain"), beginning in AD 43, and the 400-year [rule of southern Britain](/wiki/Roman_Britain "Roman Britain"), was followed by an invasion by [Germanic](/wiki/Germanic_peoples "Germanic peoples") [Anglo-Saxon](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon "Anglo-Saxon") settlers, reducing the Brittonic area mainly [to what was to become Wales](/wiki/Wales#Post-Roman_era "Wales"), [Cornwall](/wiki/Cornwall "Cornwall") and, until the latter stages of the Anglo-Saxon settlement, the [Hen Ogledd](/wiki/Hen_Ogledd "Hen Ogledd") (northern England and parts of southern Scotland).[[51]](#cite_note-69) Most of the [region settled by the Anglo-Saxons](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_England "Anglo-Saxon England") became unified as the Kingdom of England in the 10th century.[[52]](#cite_note-70) [Gaelic speakers in north-west Britain](/wiki/D%C3%A1l_Riata "Dál Riata") (with connections to the north-east of Ireland and traditionally supposed to have migrated from there in the 5th century)[[53]](#cite_note-71) united with the [Picts](/wiki/Picts "Picts") to create the [Kingdom of Scotland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Scotland "Kingdom of Scotland") in the 9th century.[[54]](#cite_note-72)

[![Photograph of the Baths showing a rectangular area of greenish water surrounded by yellow stone buildings with pillars. In the background is the tower of the abbey.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/Roman_Baths_in_Bath_Spa%2C_England_-_July_2006.jpg/250px-Roman_Baths_in_Bath_Spa%2C_England_-_July_2006.jpg)](/wiki/File:Roman_Baths_in_Bath_Spa,_England_-_July_2006.jpg)

The [Roman Baths](/wiki/Roman_Baths_(Bath) "Roman Baths (Bath)") in [Bath, Somerset](/wiki/Bath,_Somerset "Bath, Somerset"), are a well-preserved *[thermae](/wiki/Thermae "Thermae")* from [Roman Britain](/wiki/Roman_Britain "Roman Britain").

In 1066 the [Normans](/wiki/Normans "Normans") invaded England from northern France. After [conquering England](/wiki/Norman_conquest_of_England "Norman conquest of England") they seized [large parts of Wales](/wiki/Norman_invasion_of_Wales "Norman invasion of Wales"), [conquered much of Ireland](/wiki/Norman_invasion_of_Ireland "Norman invasion of Ireland") and were invited to settle in Scotland, bringing to each country [feudalism](/wiki/Feudalism "Feudalism") on the Northern French model and [Norman-French](/wiki/Norman-French "Norman-French") culture.[[55]](#cite_note-73) The [Anglo-Norman](/wiki/Anglo-Normans "Anglo-Normans") [ruling class](/wiki/Ruling_class "Ruling class") greatly influenced, but eventually assimilated with, the local cultures.[[56]](#cite_note-74) Subsequent [medieval English kings](/wiki/House_of_Plantagenet "House of Plantagenet") completed the [conquest of Wales](/wiki/Conquest_of_Wales "Conquest of Wales") and tried unsuccessfully [to annex Scotland](/wiki/Wars_of_Scottish_Independence "Wars of Scottish Independence"). Asserting its independence in the 1320 [Declaration of Arbroath](/wiki/Declaration_of_Arbroath "Declaration of Arbroath"), Scotland maintained its independence thereafter, albeit in [near-constant conflict with England](/wiki/Anglo-Scottish_Wars "Anglo-Scottish Wars").[[57]](#cite_note-75)

In 1215 [Magna Carta](/wiki/Magna_Carta "Magna Carta") was the first document to state that no government was above the law and that citizens have rights protecting them.[[58]](#cite_note-76)

The English monarchs, through inheritance of [substantial territories in France](/wiki/Angevin_Empire "Angevin Empire") and claims to the French crown, were also heavily involved in conflicts in France, most notably the [Hundred Years' War](/wiki/Hundred_Years%27_War "Hundred Years' War"), while the [Kings of Scots](/wiki/List_of_Scottish_monarchs "List of Scottish monarchs") were in [an alliance with the French](/wiki/Auld_Alliance "Auld Alliance") during this period.[[59]](#cite_note-77) [Early modern Britain](/wiki/Early_modern_Britain "Early modern Britain") saw religious conflict resulting from the [Reformation](/wiki/Reformation "Reformation") and the introduction of [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") state churches in each country.[[60]](#cite_note-78) The [English Reformation](/wiki/English_Reformation "English Reformation") ushered in political, constitutional, social and cultural change in the 16th century and [established](/wiki/State_religion "State religion") the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England"). It defined a national identity for England and slowly, but profoundly, changed people's religious beliefs.[[61]](#cite_note-79) Wales was [fully incorporated into the Kingdom of England](/wiki/Laws_in_Wales_Acts_1535_and_1542 "Laws in Wales Acts 1535 and 1542"),[[62]](#cite_note-80) and Ireland was constituted as a kingdom in personal union with the English crown.[[63]](#cite_note-81) In what was to become Northern Ireland, the lands of the independent Catholic Gaelic nobility were confiscated and [given to Protestant settlers](/wiki/Plantation_of_Ulster "Plantation of Ulster") from England and Scotland.[[64]](#cite_note-82)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Bayeux_Tapestry_WillelmDux.jpg/250px-Bayeux_Tapestry_WillelmDux.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bayeux_Tapestry_WillelmDux.jpg)

The [Bayeux Tapestry](/wiki/Bayeux_Tapestry "Bayeux Tapestry") depicts the [Battle of Hastings](/wiki/Battle_of_Hastings "Battle of Hastings"), 1066, and the events leading to it.

In 1603 the kingdoms of England, Scotland and Ireland were united in a [personal union](/wiki/Personal_union "Personal union") when [James VI of Scotland](/wiki/James_VI_of_Scotland "James VI of Scotland") inherited the crowns of England and Ireland and moved his court from Edinburgh to London; each country nevertheless remained a separate political entity and retained its separate political, legal and religious institutions.[[65]](#cite_note-83)

In the mid-17th century, all three kingdoms [were involved in a series of connected wars](/wiki/Wars_of_the_Three_Kingdoms "Wars of the Three Kingdoms") (including the [English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War")) which led to the temporary overthrow of the monarchy, with [the execution](/wiki/Execution_of_Charles_I "Execution of Charles I") of [King Charles I](/wiki/Charles_I_of_England "Charles I of England"), and the establishment of the short-lived [unitary republic](/wiki/Unitary_republic "Unitary republic") of the [Commonwealth of England](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_England "Commonwealth of England"), Scotland and Ireland.[[66]](#cite_note-84)

Although the [monarchy was restored](/wiki/Restoration_(1660) "Restoration (1660)"), the [Interregnum](/wiki/Interregnum_(1649%E2%80%931660) "Interregnum (1649–1660)") along with the [Glorious Revolution](/wiki/Glorious_Revolution "Glorious Revolution") of 1688 and the subsequent [Bill of Rights 1689](/wiki/Bill_of_Rights_1689 "Bill of Rights 1689") in England and [Claim of Right Act 1689](/wiki/Claim_of_Right_Act_1689 "Claim of Right Act 1689") in Scotland ensured that, unlike much of the rest of Europe, [royal absolutism](/wiki/Royal_absolutism "Royal absolutism") would not prevail, and a professed Catholic could never accede to the throne. The [British constitution](/wiki/British_constitution "British constitution") would develop on the basis of [constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy") and the [parliamentary system](/wiki/Parliamentary_system "Parliamentary system").[[67]](#cite_note-85) With the founding of the [Royal Society](/wiki/Royal_Society "Royal Society") in 1660, science was greatly encouraged. During this period, particularly in England, the development of [naval power](/wiki/English_navy "English navy") and the interest in [voyages of discovery](/wiki/Age_of_Discovery "Age of Discovery") led to the acquisition and settlement of overseas colonies, particularly in North America and the Caribbean.[[68]](#cite_note-86)

Though previous attempts at uniting the two kingdoms within Great Britain in 1606, 1667 and 1689 had proved unsuccessful, the attempt initiated in 1705 led to the [Treaty of Union](/wiki/Treaty_of_Union "Treaty of Union") of 1706 being agreed and ratified by both parliaments.

### Union of England and Scotland

Main articles: [Treaty of Union](/wiki/Treaty_of_Union "Treaty of Union") and [Kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Articles_of_Union_between_England_and_Scotland_28_Jan_1707.png/250px-Articles_of_Union_between_England_and_Scotland_28_Jan_1707.png)](/wiki/File:Articles_of_Union_between_England_and_Scotland_28_Jan_1707.png)

The [Treaty of Union](/wiki/Treaty_of_Union "Treaty of Union") which unified the [Kingdom of England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England") and [Kingdom of Scotland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Scotland "Kingdom of Scotland") on 1 May 1707

On 1 May 1707 the Kingdom of Great Britain was formed, the result of the [Acts of Union 1707](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1707 "Acts of Union 1707") between the [Kingdom of England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England") and [Kingdom of Scotland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Scotland "Kingdom of Scotland").[[69]](#cite_note-87) In the 18th century cabinet government developed under [Robert Walpole](/wiki/Robert_Walpole "Robert Walpole"), who is considered the *[de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto")* first [prime minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_the_United_Kingdom "Prime Minister of the United Kingdom") from 1721 to 1742. A series of [Jacobite uprisings](/wiki/Jacobite_uprisings "Jacobite uprisings") sought to remove the Protestant [House of Hanover](/wiki/House_of_Hanover "House of Hanover") from the throne and restore the Catholic [House of Stuart](/wiki/House_of_Stuart "House of Stuart"). The Jacobites were finally defeated at the [Battle of Culloden](/wiki/Battle_of_Culloden "Battle of Culloden") in 1746, after which the [Scottish Highlanders](/wiki/Scottish_Highlanders "Scottish Highlanders") were forcibly assimilated into Scotland by revoking the feudal independence of [clan chiefs](/wiki/Scottish_clan_chief "Scottish clan chief"). The British colonies in North America that broke away in the [American War of Independence](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War") became the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"). British imperial ambition turned towards Asia, particularly to [India](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj").[[70]](#cite_note-88)

British merchants played a leading part in the [Atlantic slave trade](/wiki/Atlantic_slave_trade "Atlantic slave trade"), mainly between 1662 and 1807 when British or British-colonial [slave ships](/wiki/Slave_ships "Slave ships") transported nearly 3.3 million slaves from Africa.[[71]](#cite_note-89) The slaves were taken to [work on plantations](/wiki/Plantation_economy "Plantation economy"), principally [in the Caribbean](/wiki/History_of_the_British_West_Indies "History of the British West Indies") but also [in North America](/wiki/British_America "British America").[[72]](#cite_note-90) However, with pressure from the [abolitionist movement](/wiki/Abolitionism_in_the_United_Kingdom "Abolitionism in the United Kingdom"), Parliament banned the trade in 1807, banned slavery in the British Empire in 1833, and Britain took a leading role in the movement to abolish slavery worldwide through the [blockade of Africa](/wiki/Blockade_of_Africa "Blockade of Africa") and pressing other nations to end their trade with a series of treaties.[[73]](#cite_note-91)

### United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland

Main articles: [History of the United Kingdom](/wiki/History_of_the_United_Kingdom "History of the United Kingdom"), [Acts of Union 1800](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1800 "Acts of Union 1800"), and [United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/Victoria_in_her_Coronation_robes.jpg/250px-Victoria_in_her_Coronation_robes.jpg)](/wiki/File:Victoria_in_her_Coronation_robes.jpg)

[Victoria](/wiki/Queen_Victoria "Queen Victoria") reigned as [Queen of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Monarchy_of_the_United_Kingdom "Monarchy of the United Kingdom") and [Empress of India](/wiki/Empress_of_India "Empress of India") during the 19th century.

In 1800 the parliaments of Great Britain and Ireland each passed an Act of Union, uniting the two kingdoms and creating the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland on 1 January 1801.[[74]](#cite_note-92)

After the defeat of France at the end of the [French Revolutionary Wars](/wiki/French_Revolutionary_Wars "French Revolutionary Wars") and [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars") (1792–1815) the United Kingdom emerged as the principal naval and imperial power (with London the [largest city in the world from about 1830](/wiki/List_of_largest_cities_throughout_history "List of largest cities throughout history")).[[75]](#cite_note-93) [Unchallenged at sea](/wiki/History_of_the_Royal_Navy_(after_1707)#Pax_Britannica,_1815–1914 "History of the Royal Navy (after 1707)"), British dominance was later described as the *[Pax Britannica](/wiki/Pax_Britannica "Pax Britannica")* ("British Peace"), a period of [relative peace amongst the great powers](/wiki/International_relations_of_the_Great_Powers_(1814%E2%80%931919) "International relations of the Great Powers (1814–1919)") (1815–1914) during which the British Empire became the global [hegemon](/wiki/Hegemon "Hegemon")[[76]](#cite_note-94)[[77]](#cite_note-95) and adopted the role of [global policeman](/wiki/Global_policeman "Global policeman").[[78]](#cite_note-96)[[79]](#cite_note-97) From 1853 to 1856 Britain took part in the [Crimean War](/wiki/Crimean_War "Crimean War"), allied with the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") against [Tsarist Russia](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire").[[80]](#cite_note-98) Following the [Indian Rebellion of 1857](/wiki/Indian_Rebellion_of_1857 "Indian Rebellion of 1857") the British government led by [Lord Palmerston](/wiki/Henry_John_Temple,_3rd_Viscount_Palmerston "Henry John Temple, 3rd Viscount Palmerston") assumed [direct rule](/wiki/Direct_rule "Direct rule") over [India](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj"). Alongside the formal control it exerted over its own colonies, British dominance of much of world trade meant that it [effectively controlled](/wiki/Informal_empire "Informal empire") the economies of regions such as [East Asia](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia") and [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America").[[81]](#cite_note-99)

Throughout the [Victorian era](/wiki/Victorian_era "Victorian era") (1837–1901) political attitudes favoured [free trade](/wiki/Free_trade "Free trade") and *[laissez-faire](/wiki/Laissez-faire "Laissez-faire")* policies. Beginning with the [Great Reform Act](/wiki/Great_Reform_Act "Great Reform Act") in 1832, Parliament gradually [widened the voting franchise](/wiki/Reform_Acts "Reform Acts"), with the [1884 Reform Act](/wiki/Representation_of_the_People_Act_1884 "Representation of the People Act 1884") championed by [William Gladstone](/wiki/William_Ewart_Gladstone "William Ewart Gladstone") granting [suffrage](/wiki/Suffrage "Suffrage") to a majority of males for the first time. The British population increased at a dramatic rate, accompanied by rapid [urbanisation](/wiki/Urbanisation "Urbanisation"), causing significant social and economic stresses.[[82]](#cite_note-100) By the late 19th century the [Conservative Party](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)") under [Benjamin Disraeli](/wiki/Benjamin_Disraeli "Benjamin Disraeli") and [Lord Salisbury](/wiki/Robert_Gascoyne-Cecil,_3rd_Marquess_of_Salisbury "Robert Gascoyne-Cecil, 3rd Marquess of Salisbury") initiated a period of [imperial expansion in Africa](/wiki/Scramble_for_Africa "Scramble for Africa"), maintained a policy of "[splendid isolation](/wiki/Splendid_isolation "Splendid isolation")" in Europe, and attempted to contain the influence of the [Russian Empire](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire") in [Afghanistan](/wiki/Emirate_of_Afghanistan "Emirate of Afghanistan") and [Persia](/wiki/Qajar_Iran "Qajar Iran"), in what came to be known as the [Great Game](/wiki/Great_Game "Great Game").[[83]](#cite_note-101) During this time [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia") and [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand") were granted self-governing [dominion](/wiki/Dominion "Dominion") status.[[84]](#cite_note-102) At the turn of the century, Britain's industrial dominance became challenged by the [German Empire](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire") and the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States").[[85]](#cite_note-103) The [Edwardian era](/wiki/Edwardian_era "Edwardian era") (1901–1910) saw [social reform](/wiki/Liberal_welfare_reforms "Liberal welfare reforms") and [home rule for Ireland](/wiki/Irish_Home_Rule_movement "Irish Home Rule movement") become important domestic issues, while the [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") emerged from an alliance of [trade unions](/wiki/Trade_unions_in_the_United_Kingdom "Trade unions in the United Kingdom") and small socialist groups in 1900, and [suffragettes](/wiki/Suffragettes "Suffragettes") campaigned for women's right to vote.[[86]](#cite_note-104)

### World wars and partition of Ireland

Main articles: [History of the United Kingdom during the First World War](/wiki/History_of_the_United_Kingdom_during_the_First_World_War "History of the United Kingdom during the First World War"), [Partition of Ireland](/wiki/Partition_of_Ireland "Partition of Ireland"), [Interwar Britain](/wiki/Interwar_Britain "Interwar Britain"), [United Kingdom home front during the Second World War](/wiki/United_Kingdom_home_front_during_the_Second_World_War "United Kingdom home front during the Second World War"), and [Military history of the United Kingdom during World War II](/wiki/Military_history_of_the_United_Kingdom_during_World_War_II "Military history of the United Kingdom during World War II")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Wreaths_Are_Laid_at_the_Cenotaph%2C_London_During_Remembrance_Sunday_Service_MOD_45152052.jpg/250px-Wreaths_Are_Laid_at_the_Cenotaph%2C_London_During_Remembrance_Sunday_Service_MOD_45152052.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wreaths_Are_Laid_at_the_Cenotaph,_London_During_Remembrance_Sunday_Service_MOD_45152052.jpg)

Wreaths being laid during the [Remembrance Sunday](/wiki/Remembrance_Sunday "Remembrance Sunday") service at the [Cenotaph](/wiki/The_Cenotaph "The Cenotaph") in [Whitehall](/wiki/Whitehall "Whitehall"), London

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/Spitfire_and_Hurricane_in_the_Battle_of_Britain_Memorial_Flight.jpg/250px-Spitfire_and_Hurricane_in_the_Battle_of_Britain_Memorial_Flight.jpg)](/wiki/File:Spitfire_and_Hurricane_in_the_Battle_of_Britain_Memorial_Flight.jpg)

A [Spitfire](/wiki/Supermarine_Spitfire "Supermarine Spitfire") and a [Hurricane](/wiki/Hawker_Hurricane "Hawker Hurricane") as flown in the [Battle of Britain](/wiki/Battle_of_Britain "Battle of Britain") during the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War")

Britain was one of the principal [Allies](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_I "Allies of World War I") that defeated the [Central Powers](/wiki/Central_Powers "Central Powers") in the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War") (1914–1918). Alongside their French, Russian and (after 1917) American counterparts,[[87]](#cite_note-105) British armed forces were engaged across much of the British Empire and in several regions of Europe, particularly on the [Western Front](/wiki/Western_Front_(World_War_I) "Western Front (World War I)").[[88]](#cite_note-Westwell&Cove-106) The high fatalities of [trench warfare](/wiki/Trench_warfare "Trench warfare") caused the loss of much of a generation of men, with lasting social effects in the nation and a great disruption in the social order. Britain had suffered 2.5 million casualties and finished the war with a huge national debt.[[88]](#cite_note-Westwell&Cove-106)

The consequences of the war persuaded the government to expand the right to vote in national and local elections to all adult men and most adult women with the [Representation of the People Act 1918](/wiki/Representation_of_the_People_Act_1918 "Representation of the People Act 1918").[[88]](#cite_note-Westwell&Cove-106) After the war Britain became a permanent member of the Executive Council of the [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations") and received [a mandate](/wiki/League_of_Nations_mandate "League of Nations mandate") over a number of former German and [Ottoman](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") colonies. Under the leadership of [David Lloyd George](/wiki/David_Lloyd_George "David Lloyd George"), the British Empire reached its greatest extent, covering a fifth of the world's land surface and a quarter of its population.[[89]](#cite_note-107)

By the mid-1920s most of the British population could listen to [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") radio programmes.[[90]](#cite_note-108)[[91]](#cite_note-109) Experimental [television](/wiki/Television_in_the_United_Kingdom "Television in the United Kingdom") broadcasts [began in 1929](/wiki/History_of_television#United_Kingdom "History of television") and the [first scheduled BBC Television Service](/wiki/First_day_of_BBC_television "First day of BBC television") commenced in 1936.[[92]](#cite_note-110) The rise of [Irish nationalism](/wiki/Irish_nationalism "Irish nationalism"), and disputes within Ireland over the terms of [Irish Home Rule](/wiki/Irish_Home_Rule "Irish Home Rule"), led eventually to the [partition of the island](/wiki/Partition_of_Ireland "Partition of Ireland") in 1921.[[93]](#cite_note-111) [A period of conflict in what is now Northern Ireland](/wiki/The_Troubles_in_Ulster_(1920%E2%80%931922) "The Troubles in Ulster (1920–1922)") occurred from June 1920 until June 1922. The [Irish Free State](/wiki/Irish_Free_State "Irish Free State") became independent, initially with [Dominion](/wiki/Dominion "Dominion") status in 1922, and [unambiguously independent in 1931](/wiki/Statute_of_Westminster_1931#Irish_Free_State "Statute of Westminster 1931"). Northern Ireland remained part of the United Kingdom.[[94]](#cite_note-112) The [1928 Equal Franchise Act](/wiki/1928_Equal_Franchise_Act "1928 Equal Franchise Act") gave women electoral equality with men in national elections. Strikes in the mid-1920s culminated in the [General Strike of 1926](/wiki/General_Strike_of_1926 "General Strike of 1926"). Britain had still not recovered from the effects of the First World War when the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression_in_the_United_Kingdom "Great Depression in the United Kingdom") (1929–1932) led to considerable unemployment and hardship in the old industrial areas, as well as political and social unrest with rising membership in communist and socialist parties. [A coalition government](/wiki/National_Government_(1931) "National Government (1931)") was formed in 1931.[[95]](#cite_note-113)

Nonetheless, Britain was described as "a very wealthy country, formidable in arms, ruthless in pursuit of its interests and sitting at the heart of a global production system."[[96]](#cite_note-Edgerton2012-114) After [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany")'s [invasion of Poland](/wiki/Invasion_of_Poland "Invasion of Poland") in 1939, Britain entered the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"). Despite the defeat of its European allies in the first year, Britain and its empire continued the war against Germany.[[96]](#cite_note-Edgerton2012-114)

In 1940 the [Royal Air Force](/wiki/Royal_Air_Force "Royal Air Force") defeated the German *[Luftwaffe](/wiki/Luftwaffe "Luftwaffe")* in the [Battle of Britain](/wiki/Battle_of_Britain "Battle of Britain"). Urban areas suffered heavy bombing during [the Blitz](/wiki/The_Blitz "The Blitz"). The [Grand Alliance](/wiki/Grand_Alliance_(World_War_II) "Grand Alliance (World War II)") of Britain, the United States and the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") formed in 1941, leading the [Allies](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II") against the [Axis powers](/wiki/Axis_powers "Axis powers"). There were eventual hard-fought victories in the [Battle of the Atlantic](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Atlantic "Battle of the Atlantic"), the [North Africa campaign](/wiki/North_Africa_campaign "North Africa campaign") and the [Italian campaign](/wiki/Italian_campaign_(World_War_II) "Italian campaign (World War II)"). British forces played important roles in the [Normandy landings](/wiki/Normandy_landings "Normandy landings") of 1944 and the [liberation of Europe](/wiki/Liberation_of_Europe "Liberation of Europe"). The British Army led the [Burma campaign](/wiki/Burma_campaign "Burma campaign") against Japan, and the [British Pacific Fleet](/wiki/British_Pacific_Fleet "British Pacific Fleet") fought Japan at sea. [British scientists contributed](/wiki/British_contribution_to_the_Manhattan_Project "British contribution to the Manhattan Project") to the American [Manhattan Project](/wiki/Manhattan_Project "Manhattan Project"), whose task was to build a [nuclear weapon](/wiki/Nuclear_weapon "Nuclear weapon").[[97]](#cite_note-115)

### Post-war 20th century

Main articles: [Post-war Britain (1945–1979)](/wiki/Post-war_Britain_(1945%E2%80%931979) "Post-war Britain (1945–1979)") and [Social history of post-war Britain (1945–1979)](/wiki/Social_history_of_post-war_Britain_(1945%E2%80%931979) "Social history of post-war Britain (1945–1979)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/British_Empire_1921.png/500px-British_Empire_1921.png)](/wiki/File:British_Empire_1921.png)

The [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") at its territorial peak in 1921

The United Kingdom was one of the [Big Three](/wiki/Big_Three_(World_War_II) "Big Three (World War II)") powers (with the United States and the Soviet Union) who met to plan the [post-war world](/wiki/Aftermath_of_World_War_II "Aftermath of World War II");[[98]](#cite_note-116) it drafted the [Declaration by United Nations](/wiki/Declaration_by_United_Nations "Declaration by United Nations") with the United States and became one of the five permanent members of the [United Nations Security Council](/wiki/United_Nations_Security_Council "United Nations Security Council"). It worked closely with the United States to establish the [International Monetary Fund](/wiki/International_Monetary_Fund "International Monetary Fund"), the [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank") and [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO").[[99]](#cite_note-117) The war left the UK severely weakened and financially dependent on the American [Marshall Plan](/wiki/Marshall_Plan "Marshall Plan"),[[100]](#cite_note-118) but it was spared the total war that devastated [eastern Europe](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe").[[101]](#cite_note-119)

In the immediate post-war years the [Labour government](/wiki/Labour_Government_1945%E2%80%931951 "Labour Government 1945–1951") under [Clement Attlee](/wiki/Clement_Attlee "Clement Attlee") initiated a radical programme of reforms, which significantly affected British society [in the following decades](/wiki/Post-war_consensus "Post-war consensus").[[102]](#cite_note-120) Major industries and public utilities were [nationalised](/wiki/Nationalised "Nationalised"), a [welfare state](/wiki/Welfare_state "Welfare state") was established, and a comprehensive, publicly funded healthcare system, the [National Health Service](/wiki/National_Health_Service "National Health Service"), was created.[[103]](#cite_note-121) The rise of nationalism in the colonies coincided with Britain's much-diminished economic position after [its involvement in the First World War](/wiki/History_of_the_United_Kingdom_during_the_First_World_War "History of the United Kingdom during the First World War") and [the Second World War](/wiki/Military_history_of_the_United_Kingdom_during_World_War_II "Military history of the United Kingdom during World War II"), so that a policy of [decolonisation](/wiki/Decolonisation "Decolonisation") was unavoidable.[[104]](#cite_note-122)[[105]](#cite_note-123)[[106]](#cite_note-124) Independence was granted to India and Pakistan in 1947.[[107]](#cite_note-125) Over the next three decades, most colonies of the British Empire gained their independence, and many became members of the [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations").[[108]](#cite_note-126)

The UK was the third country to develop [a nuclear weapons arsenal](/wiki/Nuclear_weapons_and_the_United_Kingdom "Nuclear weapons and the United Kingdom"), with its first atomic bomb test, [Operation Hurricane](/wiki/Operation_Hurricane "Operation Hurricane"), in 1952, but the post-war limitations of Britain's international role were illustrated by the [Suez Crisis](/wiki/Suez_Crisis "Suez Crisis") of 1956. As a result of a shortage of workers in the 1950s, the government [encouraged immigration](/wiki/Modern_immigration_to_the_United_Kingdom "Modern immigration to the United Kingdom") from [Commonwealth countries](/wiki/Commonwealth_countries "Commonwealth countries"). In the following decades the UK became a more [multiracial](/wiki/Multiracialism "Multiracialism") and [multicultural](/wiki/Multiculturalism "Multiculturalism") society.[[109]](#cite_note-127) Despite rising living standards in the late 1950s and 1960s, the United Kingdom's economic performance was less successful than many of its main competitors such as France, [West Germany](/wiki/West_Germany "West Germany") and Japan. The United Kingdom was the first democratic nation to [lower its voting age to 18](/wiki/Representation_of_the_People_Act_1969 "Representation of the People Act 1969") in 1969.[[110]](#cite_note-128)

In the decades-long process of [European integration](/wiki/European_integration "European integration") the UK was a founding member of the [Western European Union](/wiki/Western_European_Union "Western European Union"), established with the [London and Paris Conferences](/wiki/London_and_Paris_Conferences "London and Paris Conferences") in 1954. In 1960 the UK was one of the seven founding members of the [European Free Trade Association](/wiki/European_Free_Trade_Association "European Free Trade Association") (EFTA), but in 1973 it left to join the [European Communities](/wiki/European_Communities "European Communities") (EC). In a [1975 referendum](/wiki/1975_United_Kingdom_European_Communities_membership_referendum "1975 United Kingdom European Communities membership referendum") 67 per cent voted to stay in it.[[111]](#cite_note-129) When the EC became the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") (EU) in 1992, the UK was one of the 12 founding member states.

From the late 1960s, Northern Ireland experienced communal and [paramilitary](/wiki/Paramilitary "Paramilitary") violence, sometimes affecting other parts of the UK, known as [the Troubles](/wiki/The_Troubles "The Troubles"). It is usually considered to have ended with the 1998 [Belfast "Good Friday" Agreement](/wiki/Belfast_Agreement "Belfast Agreement").[[112]](#cite_note-130) Following a period of widespread economic slowdown and industrial strife in the 1970s, the [Conservative](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)") government of the 1980s led by [Margaret Thatcher](/wiki/Margaret_Thatcher "Margaret Thatcher") initiated a radical policy of [monetarism](/wiki/Monetarism "Monetarism"), [deregulation](/wiki/Deregulation "Deregulation"), particularly of the financial sector (for example, the [Big Bang](/wiki/Big_Bang_(financial_markets) "Big Bang (financial markets)") in 1986) and labour markets, the sale of state-owned companies (privatisation), and the withdrawal of subsidies to others.[[113]](#cite_note-131)

In 1982, [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina") invaded the British territories of [South Georgia](/wiki/South_Georgia_and_the_South_Sandwich_Islands "South Georgia and the South Sandwich Islands") and the [Falkland Islands](/wiki/Falkland_Islands "Falkland Islands"), leading to the 10-week [Falklands War](/wiki/Falklands_War "Falklands War") in which Argentine forces were defeated. The inhabitants of the islands strongly favour British sovereignty, expressed in a [2013 referendum](/wiki/2013_Falkland_Islands_sovereignty_referendum "2013 Falkland Islands sovereignty referendum"). From 1984 the British economy was helped by the inflow of substantial [North Sea oil](/wiki/North_Sea_oil "North Sea oil") revenues.[[114]](#cite_note-132) Another British Overseas Territory, [Gibraltar](/wiki/Gibraltar "Gibraltar"),[[115]](#cite_note-133) is [a key military base](/wiki/Port_of_Gibraltar "Port of Gibraltar"); a [referendum in 2002](/wiki/2002_Gibraltar_sovereignty_referendum "2002 Gibraltar sovereignty referendum") on shared sovereignty with Spain was rejected.

Around the end of the 20th century, there were major changes to the governance of the UK with the establishment of [devolved](/wiki/Devolution "Devolution") administrations for Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland.[[116]](#cite_note-134) [The statutory incorporation](/wiki/Human_Rights_Act_1998 "Human Rights Act 1998") followed acceptance of the [European Convention on Human Rights](/wiki/European_Convention_on_Human_Rights "European Convention on Human Rights"). The UK remained a [great power](/wiki/List_of_modern_great_powers#United_Kingdom "List of modern great powers") with global diplomatic and military influence and a leading role in the United Nations and NATO.[[117]](#cite_note-David_M._McCourt-135)

### 21st century

Main articles: [Political history of the United Kingdom (1979–present)](/wiki/Political_history_of_the_United_Kingdom_(1979%E2%80%93present) "Political history of the United Kingdom (1979–present)") and [Social history of the United Kingdom (1979–present)](/wiki/Social_history_of_the_United_Kingdom_(1979%E2%80%93present) "Social history of the United Kingdom (1979–present)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Johnson_signed_Brexit_Withdrawal_Agreement.jpg/250px-Johnson_signed_Brexit_Withdrawal_Agreement.jpg)](/wiki/File:Johnson_signed_Brexit_Withdrawal_Agreement.jpg)

Prime Minister [Boris Johnson](/wiki/Boris_Johnson "Boris Johnson") signs the [Brexit withdrawal agreement](/wiki/Brexit_withdrawal_agreement "Brexit withdrawal agreement") in 2020, formally [withdrawing the UK](/wiki/Brexit "Brexit") from the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") (EU).

The United Kingdom broadly supported the United States' approach to the "[war on terror](/wiki/War_on_terror "War on terror")" in the early 21st century.[[118]](#cite_note-136) British troops fought in the [war in Afghanistan](/wiki/War_in_Afghanistan_(2001%E2%80%932021) "War in Afghanistan (2001–2021)"), but controversy surrounded Britain's [military deployment in Iraq](/wiki/Iraq_War "Iraq War"), which saw the [largest protest in British history](/wiki/15_February_2003_anti-war_protests "15 February 2003 anti-war protests") in opposition to the government led by [Tony Blair](/wiki/Tony_Blair "Tony Blair").[[119]](#cite_note-137)

The [Great Recession](/wiki/Great_Recession "Great Recession") (2007–2010) severely affected the British economy,[[120]](#cite_note-Latest_UK_GDP_data-138) and was followed by a period of weak growth and stagnation.[[121]](#cite_note-139)[[122]](#cite_note-140) The [Cameron–Clegg coalition](/wiki/Cameron%E2%80%93Clegg_coalition "Cameron–Clegg coalition") government of 2010 introduced [austerity measures](/wiki/United_Kingdom_government_austerity_programme "United Kingdom government austerity programme") intended to tackle the substantial public deficits.[[123]](#cite_note-141) [A referendum](/wiki/2014_Scottish_independence_referendum "2014 Scottish independence referendum") on [Scottish independence](/wiki/Scottish_independence "Scottish independence") in 2014 resulted in the Scottish electorate voting by 55.3 to 44.7 per cent to remain part of the United Kingdom.[[124]](#cite_note-142)

In 2016, 51.9 per cent of voters in the UK [voted to leave the European Union](/wiki/2016_United_Kingdom_European_Union_membership_referendum "2016 United Kingdom European Union membership referendum") (EU).[[125]](#cite_note-143) The [UK left the EU](/wiki/Brexit "Brexit") in 2020.[[126]](#cite_note-144) On 1 May 2021 the [EU–UK Trade and Cooperation Agreement](/wiki/EU%E2%80%93UK_Trade_and_Cooperation_Agreement "EU–UK Trade and Cooperation Agreement"), a free trade agreement between the UK and the EU, came into force.[[127]](#cite_note-commission.europa.eu-145)[[128]](#cite_note-146)

The [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_the_United_Kingdom "COVID-19 pandemic in the United Kingdom") had a severe [impact on the British economy](/wiki/Economic_impact_of_the_COVID-19_pandemic_in_the_United_Kingdom "Economic impact of the COVID-19 pandemic in the United Kingdom"), caused major [disruptions to education](/wiki/Impact_of_the_COVID-19_pandemic_on_education_in_the_United_Kingdom "Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on education in the United Kingdom") and had [far-reaching impacts on society and politics](/wiki/Social_impact_of_the_COVID-19_pandemic_in_the_United_Kingdom "Social impact of the COVID-19 pandemic in the United Kingdom") in 2020 and 2021.[[129]](#cite_note-GOVUK-147)[[130]](#cite_note-148)[[131]](#cite_note-149) The United Kingdom was the first country in the world to use an approved [COVID-19 vaccine](/wiki/COVID-19_vaccine "COVID-19 vaccine"). Developing a  [vaccine in the UK](/wiki/Oxford%E2%80%93AstraZeneca_COVID-19_vaccine "Oxford–AstraZeneca COVID-19 vaccine") allowed the rollout to be amongst the fastest in the world.[[132]](#cite_note-150)[[133]](#cite_note-151)

## Geography

Main articles: [Geography of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Geography_of_the_United_Kingdom "Geography of the United Kingdom"), [Fauna of Great Britain](/wiki/Fauna_of_Great_Britain "Fauna of Great Britain"), and [Flora of Great Britain and Ireland](/wiki/Flora_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "Flora of Great Britain and Ireland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/MODIS_-_Great_Britain_and_Northern_Ireland_-_2012-06-04_during_heat_wave_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-MODIS_-_Great_Britain_and_Northern_Ireland_-_2012-06-04_during_heat_wave_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:MODIS_-_Great_Britain_and_Northern_Ireland_-_2012-06-04_during_heat_wave_(cropped).jpg)

A satellite image of the United Kingdom excluding [Shetland](/wiki/Shetland "Shetland")

The total area of the United Kingdom is approximately 94,354 square miles (244,376 km2),[[f]](#cite_note-ONSArea-17)[[12]](#cite_note-ONS_Standard_Area_Measurement-18) with a land area of 93,723 square miles (242,741 km2).[[12]](#cite_note-ONS_Standard_Area_Measurement-18) It occupies the major part of the [British Isles](/wiki/British_Isles "British Isles")[[134]](#cite_note-152) and includes the island of Great Britain, the north-eastern one-sixth of the island of Ireland and [some smaller surrounding islands](/wiki/List_of_islands_of_the_United_Kingdom "List of islands of the United Kingdom"), meaning it comprises [England](/wiki/England "England"), [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland"), [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales") and [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland").[[135]](#cite_note-ONS_Geography_Guide-153)

Geographically, the United Kingdom lies between the [North Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/North_Atlantic_Ocean "North Atlantic Ocean") and the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea") with the southeast coast coming within 22 miles (35 km) of the coast of northern France, from which it is separated by the [English Channel](/wiki/English_Channel "English Channel").[[136]](#cite_note-factbook-154)

The nearby island polities of the [Isle of Man](/wiki/Isle_of_Man "Isle of Man"), [Jersey](/wiki/Jersey "Jersey") and [Guernsey](/wiki/Guernsey "Guernsey") are [Crown Dependencies](/wiki/Crown_Dependencies "Crown Dependencies"), in union with the [British monarch](/wiki/Monarchy_of_the_United_Kingdom "Monarchy of the United Kingdom"), but do not strictly form part of the United Kingdom or any of its three jurisdictions or four countries, although the British government retains responsibility for their external affairs. The Isle of Man lies roughly midway between Great Britain and the [island of Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland") in the [Irish Sea](/wiki/Irish_Sea "Irish Sea"), while the Channel Islands lie just off the northern French coast.

The [Royal Greenwich Observatory](/wiki/Royal_Observatory,_Greenwich "Royal Observatory, Greenwich") in London was chosen as the defining point of the [Prime Meridian](/wiki/Prime_Meridian "Prime Meridian")[[137]](#cite_note-155) at the [International Meridian Conference](/wiki/International_Meridian_Conference "International Meridian Conference") in 1884.[[138]](#cite_note-156)

The UK lies between latitudes [49°](/wiki/49th_parallel_north "49th parallel north") and [61° N](/wiki/61st_parallel_north "61st parallel north"), and longitudes [9° W](/wiki/9th_meridian_west "9th meridian west") and [2° E](/wiki/2nd_meridian_east "2nd meridian east"). Northern Ireland shares a 310-mile (499 km) land boundary with the Republic of Ireland[[136]](#cite_note-factbook-154) and has a 404-mile (650 km) coastline.[[139]](#cite_note-NI_coastline-157) The length of coastline of Great Britain plus its principal islands is about 19,491 miles (31,368 km) long, with the coastline of the main island Great Britain being 11,073 miles (17,820 km) of that,[[140]](#cite_note-UK_coastline-158) though measurements can vary greatly due to the [coastline paradox](/wiki/Coastline_paradox "Coastline paradox").[[141]](#cite_note-159) It is connected to continental Europe by the [Channel Tunnel](/wiki/Channel_Tunnel "Channel Tunnel"), which at 31 miles (50 km) (24 miles (38 km) underwater) is the longest underwater tunnel in the world.[[142]](#cite_note-160)

The UK contains four terrestrial [ecoregions](/wiki/Ecoregion "Ecoregion"): [Celtic broadleaf forests](/wiki/Celtic_broadleaf_forests "Celtic broadleaf forests"), [English Lowlands beech forests](/wiki/English_Lowlands_beech_forests "English Lowlands beech forests"), [North Atlantic moist mixed forests](/wiki/North_Atlantic_moist_mixed_forests "North Atlantic moist mixed forests"), and [Caledonian conifer forests](/wiki/Caledonian_Forest "Caledonian Forest").[[143]](#cite_note-161) The area of woodland in the UK was estimated to be 3.25 million hectares in 2023, which represents 13 per cent of its land area.[[144]](#cite_note-162)

### Climate

Main articles: [Climate of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Climate_of_the_United_Kingdom "Climate of the United Kingdom") and [Climate change in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Climate_change_in_the_United_Kingdom "Climate change in the United Kingdom")

Further information: [Climate of England](/wiki/Climate_of_England "Climate of England"), [Climate of Scotland](/wiki/Climate_of_Scotland "Climate of Scotland"), [Climate of Wales](/wiki/Climate_of_Wales "Climate of Wales"), and [Climate of Northern Ireland](/wiki/Climate_of_Northern_Ireland "Climate of Northern Ireland")

Most of the United Kingdom has a temperate climate, with generally cool temperatures and plentiful rainfall all year round.[[136]](#cite_note-factbook-154) The temperature varies with the seasons seldom dropping below 0 [°C](/wiki/Celsius "Celsius") (32 [°F](/wiki/Fahrenheit "Fahrenheit")) or rising above 30 °C (86 °F).[[145]](#cite_note-163) Some parts, away from the coast, of upland England, Wales, Northern Ireland and most of Scotland, experience a [subpolar oceanic climate](/wiki/Subpolar_oceanic_climate "Subpolar oceanic climate"). Higher elevations in Scotland experience a [continental subarctic climate](/wiki/Subarctic_climate "Subarctic climate") and the mountains experience a [tundra climate](/wiki/Tundra_climate "Tundra climate").[[146]](#cite_note-164)

The prevailing wind is from the southwest and bears frequent spells of mild and wet weather from the Atlantic Ocean,[[136]](#cite_note-factbook-154) although the eastern parts are mostly sheltered from this wind. Since the majority of the rain falls over the western regions, the eastern parts are the driest. Atlantic currents, warmed by the [Gulf Stream](/wiki/Gulf_Stream "Gulf Stream"), bring mild winters, especially in the west where winters are wet and even more so over high ground. Summers are warmest in the southeast of England and coolest in the north. Heavy snowfall can occur in winter and early spring on high ground, and occasionally settles to great depth away from the hills.[[147]](#cite_note-165)

The average total annual sunshine in the United Kingdom was 1,339.7 hours between 1971 and 2000,[[148]](#cite_note-ukaverages-166) which is just under 30% of the maximum possible.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The hours of sunshine vary from 1,200 to about 1,580 hours per year.[[149]](#cite_note-met_off_climate_series-167)

Climate change has serious impacts on the country. A third of food price rise in 2023 was attributed to it.[[150]](#cite_note-168) In 2024 the United Kingdom ranked 5th out of 180 countries in the [Environmental Performance Index](/wiki/Environmental_Performance_Index "Environmental Performance Index").[[151]](#cite_note-169) A law has been passed that [UK greenhouse gas emissions](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas_emissions_by_the_United_Kingdom "Greenhouse gas emissions by the United Kingdom") will be [net zero](/wiki/Net_zero "Net zero") by 2050.[[152]](#cite_note-170)

### Topography

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/Uk_topo_en.jpg/250px-Uk_topo_en.jpg)](/wiki/File:Uk_topo_en.jpg)

The United Kingdom's topography

[England](/wiki/Geography_of_England "Geography of England") accounts for 53 per cent of the UK, covering 50,350 square miles (130,395 km2).[[153]](#cite_note-171) Most of the country consists of lowland terrain,[[154]](#cite_note-Atlapedia-172) with upland and mountainous terrain northwest of the [Tees–Exe line](/wiki/Tees%E2%80%93Exe_line "Tees–Exe line") which roughly divides the UK into [lowland and upland](/wiki/Lowland_and_upland "Lowland and upland") areas. Lowland areas include [Cornwall](/wiki/Cornwall "Cornwall"), the [New Forest](/wiki/New_Forest "New Forest"), the [South Downs](/wiki/South_Downs "South Downs") and the [Norfolk Broads](/wiki/The_Broads "The Broads"). Upland areas include the [Lake District](/wiki/Lake_District "Lake District"), the [Pennines](/wiki/Pennines "Pennines"), the [Yorkshire Dales](/wiki/Yorkshire_Dales "Yorkshire Dales"), [Exmoor](/wiki/Exmoor "Exmoor") and [Dartmoor](/wiki/Dartmoor "Dartmoor"). The main rivers and estuaries are the [Thames](/wiki/River_Thames "River Thames"), [Severn](/wiki/River_Severn "River Severn"), and the [Humber](/wiki/Humber "Humber"). England's highest mountain is [Scafell Pike](/wiki/Scafell_Pike "Scafell Pike"), at 978 metres (3,209 ft) in the Lake District; its largest island is the [Isle of Wight](/wiki/Isle_of_Wight "Isle of Wight").

[Scotland](/wiki/Geography_of_Scotland "Geography of Scotland") accounts for 32 per cent of the UK, covering 30,410 square miles (78,772 km2).[[155]](#cite_note-173) This includes [nearly 800 islands](/wiki/List_of_islands_of_Scotland "List of islands of Scotland"),[[156]](#cite_note-174) notably the [Hebrides](/wiki/Hebrides "Hebrides"), [Orkney](/wiki/Orkney "Orkney") Islands and [Shetland Islands](/wiki/Shetland_Islands "Shetland Islands"). Scotland is the most mountainous constituent country of the UK. The [Highlands](/wiki/Scottish_Highlands "Scottish Highlands") to the north and west are the more rugged region containing the majority of Scotland's mountainous land, including the [Cairngorms](/wiki/Cairngorms "Cairngorms"), [Loch Lomond and The Trossachs](/wiki/Loch_Lomond_and_The_Trossachs "Loch Lomond and The Trossachs") and [Ben Nevis](/wiki/Ben_Nevis "Ben Nevis") which at 1,345 metres (4,413 ft)[[157]](#cite_note-175) is the highest point in the British Isles.[[158]](#cite_note-176)

[Wales](/wiki/Geography_of_Wales "Geography of Wales") accounts for less than 9 per cent of the UK, covering 8,020 square miles (20,779 km2).[[159]](#cite_note-177) It is mostly mountainous, though [South Wales](/wiki/South_Wales "South Wales") is less mountainous than [North](/wiki/North_Wales "North Wales") and [Mid Wales](/wiki/Mid_Wales "Mid Wales"). The highest mountains in Wales are in [Snowdonia](/wiki/Snowdonia "Snowdonia") and include [Snowdon](/wiki/Snowdon "Snowdon") ([Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_language "Welsh language"): *Yr Wyddfa*) which, at 1,085 metres (3,560 ft), is the highest peak in Wales.[[154]](#cite_note-Atlapedia-172) Wales has over 1,680 miles (2,704 kilometres) of coastline including the [Pembrokeshire Coast](/wiki/Pembrokeshire_Coast "Pembrokeshire Coast").[[140]](#cite_note-UK_coastline-158) Several islands lie off the Welsh mainland, the largest of which is [Anglesey](/wiki/Anglesey "Anglesey") (*Ynys Môn*).

[Northern Ireland](/wiki/Geography_of_Ireland "Geography of Ireland"), separated from Great Britain by the [Irish Sea](/wiki/Irish_Sea "Irish Sea") and [North Channel](/wiki/North_Channel_(Great_Britain_and_Ireland) "North Channel (Great Britain and Ireland)"), has an area of 5,470 square miles (14,160 km2) and is mostly hilly. It includes [Lough Neagh](/wiki/Lough_Neagh "Lough Neagh") which, at 150 square miles (388 km2), is the largest lake in the British Isles by area,[[160]](#cite_note-178) [Lough Erne](/wiki/Lough_Erne "Lough Erne"), which has over 150 islands, and the [Giant's Causeway](/wiki/Giant%27s_Causeway "Giant's Causeway"), which is listed by [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") as a [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site"). The highest peak in Northern Ireland is [Slieve Donard](/wiki/Slieve_Donard "Slieve Donard") in the [Mourne Mountains](/wiki/Mourne_Mountains "Mourne Mountains") at 852 metres (2,795 ft).[[154]](#cite_note-Atlapedia-172)

## Government and politics

Main articles: [Politics of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Politics_of_the_United_Kingdom "Politics of the United Kingdom"), [Government of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Government_of_the_United_Kingdom "Government of the United Kingdom"), and [Monarchy of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Monarchy_of_the_United_Kingdom "Monarchy of the United Kingdom")

Further information: [Constitution of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_United_Kingdom "Constitution of the United Kingdom")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/King_Charles_III_%28July_2023%29.jpg/250px-King_Charles_III_%28July_2023%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:King_Charles_III_(July_2023).jpg)

[Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III")  
[King](/wiki/Monarch_of_the_United_Kingdom "Monarch of the United Kingdom")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/Prime_Minister_Sir_Keir_Starmer_Official_Portrait_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Prime_Minister_Sir_Keir_Starmer_Official_Portrait_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Prime_Minister_Sir_Keir_Starmer_Official_Portrait_(cropped).jpg)

[Keir Starmer](/wiki/Keir_Starmer "Keir Starmer")  
[Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_the_United_Kingdom "Prime Minister of the United Kingdom")

The United Kingdom is a [constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy") and a [parliamentary democracy](/wiki/Parliamentary_democracy "Parliamentary democracy")[[161]](#cite_note-179) operating under the [Westminster system](/wiki/Westminster_system "Westminster system"), otherwise known as a "democratic parliamentary monarchy".[[162]](#cite_note-180) It is a [centralised](/wiki/Centralized_government "Centralized government"), [unitary state](/wiki/Unitary_state "Unitary state")[[163]](#cite_note-181)[[164]](#cite_note-182) wherein the [Parliament of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom") is [sovereign](/wiki/Parliamentary_sovereignty_in_the_United_Kingdom "Parliamentary sovereignty in the United Kingdom").[[165]](#cite_note-183) Parliament is made up of the elected [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom"), the appointed [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords") and [the Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown") (as personified by [the monarch](/wiki/Monarchy_of_the_United_Kingdom "Monarchy of the United Kingdom")).[[s]](#cite_note-186)[[168]](#cite_note-parliament.uk-187) The main business of Parliament takes place in the two houses,[[168]](#cite_note-parliament.uk-187) but [royal assent](/wiki/Royal_assent "Royal assent") is required for a bill to become an [act of Parliament](/wiki/Act_of_Parliament_(UK) "Act of Parliament (UK)") (that is, [statute law](/wiki/Statute_law "Statute law")).[[169]](#cite_note-188) As a result of parliamentary sovereignty, the [British constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_United_Kingdom "Constitution of the United Kingdom") is [uncodified](/wiki/Uncodified_constitution "Uncodified constitution"), consisting mostly of disparate written sources, including parliamentary [statutes](/wiki/Statute "Statute"), judge-made [case law](/wiki/Case_law "Case law") and international treaties, together with [constitutional conventions](/wiki/British_Constitution#Conventions "British Constitution").[[170]](#cite_note-189) Nevertheless, the [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_Kingdom "Supreme Court of the United Kingdom") recognises a number of principles underlying the British constitution, such as parliamentary sovereignty, the [rule of law](/wiki/Rule_of_law "Rule of law"), [democracy](/wiki/Democracy "Democracy") and upholding [international law](/wiki/Internationalism_(politics) "Internationalism (politics)").[[171]](#cite_note-190)

[King Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III") is the [monarch](/wiki/Monarch "Monarch") and [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state") of the United Kingdom and 14 other independent [sovereign states](/wiki/Sovereign_state "Sovereign state"), referred to as "[Commonwealth realms](/wiki/Commonwealth_realm "Commonwealth realm")". The monarch is formally vested with all executive authority as the personal embodiment of [the Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown") and is "fundamental to the law and working of government in the UK".[[172]](#cite_note-191) The disposition of such powers however, including those belonging to the [royal prerogative](/wiki/Royal_prerogative_in_the_United_Kingdom "Royal prerogative in the United Kingdom"), is generally exercised only on the [advice](/wiki/Advice_(constitutional_law) "Advice (constitutional law)") of [ministers of the Crown](/wiki/Ministers_of_the_Crown "Ministers of the Crown") responsible to Parliament and thence to the electorate. Nevertheless, in the performance of official duties the monarch has "the right to be consulted, the right to encourage, and the right to warn".[[173]](#cite_note-192) In addition the monarch has a number of [reserve powers](/wiki/Reserve_power#United_Kingdom "Reserve power") at his disposal to uphold [responsible government](/wiki/Responsible_government "Responsible government") and prevent [constitutional crises](/wiki/Constitutional_crisis "Constitutional crisis").[[t]](#cite_note-194)

The prime minister is the [head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government") in the United Kingdom.[[175]](#cite_note-195) Acting under the direction and supervision of a [Cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_the_United_Kingdom "Cabinet of the United Kingdom") of senior ministers selected and led by the prime minister, [His Majesty's Government](/wiki/His_Majesty%27s_Government "His Majesty's Government") serves as the principal instrument for public policymaking, administers public services and, through the [Privy Council](/wiki/Privy_Council_of_the_United_Kingdom "Privy Council of the United Kingdom"), promulgates [statutory instruments](/wiki/Statutory_instrument_(UK) "Statutory instrument (UK)") and tenders advice to the monarch.[[176]](#cite_note-196)[[177]](#cite_note-197)[[178]](#cite_note-198) Nearly all prime ministers have served concurrently as [First Lord of the Treasury](/wiki/First_Lord_of_the_Treasury "First Lord of the Treasury")[[179]](#cite_note-199) and all prime ministers have continuously served as First Lord of the Treasury since 1905,[[180]](#cite_note-200) [Minister for the Civil Service](/wiki/Minister_for_the_Civil_Service "Minister for the Civil Service") since 1968,[[181]](#cite_note-201) and [Minister for the Union](/wiki/Minister_for_the_Union "Minister for the Union") since 2019.[[182]](#cite_note-202) While appointed by the monarch, in modern times the prime minister is, by [convention](/wiki/Constitutional_conventions_of_the_United_Kingdom "Constitutional conventions of the United Kingdom"), an MP, the leader of the political party with the most seats in the House of Commons, and holds office by virtue of their ability to [command the confidence](/wiki/Confidence_motions_in_the_United_Kingdom "Confidence motions in the United Kingdom") of the House of Commons.[[183]](#cite_note-203)[[184]](#cite_note-204) The prime minister as at 2025 is [Sir Keir Starmer](/wiki/Keir_Starmer "Keir Starmer"), the [leader of the Labour Party](/wiki/Leader_of_the_Labour_Party_(UK) "Leader of the Labour Party (UK)").

Although not part of the United Kingdom, the three [Crown Dependencies](/wiki/Crown_Dependencies "Crown Dependencies") of [Jersey](/wiki/Jersey "Jersey"), [Guernsey](/wiki/Guernsey "Guernsey") and the [Isle of Man](/wiki/Isle_of_Man "Isle of Man"), as well as the 14 [British Overseas Territories](/wiki/British_Overseas_Territories "British Overseas Territories"), are subject to the sovereignty of the British Crown.[[185]](#cite_note-205)

[Democratic backsliding](/wiki/Democratic_backsliding "Democratic backsliding") was found by the 2026 [V-Dem Democracy Report](/wiki/V-Dem_Institute#Democracy_Report "V-Dem Institute") for the United Kingdom.[[186]](#cite_note-j496-206)

### Elections

Main article: [Elections in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Elections_in_the_United_Kingdom "Elections in the United Kingdom")

[![Large sand-coloured building of Gothic design beside brown river. The building has several large towers, including large clock tower.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/Houses_of_Parliament_in_2022_%28cropped%29.jpg/330px-Houses_of_Parliament_in_2022_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Houses_of_Parliament_in_2022_(cropped).jpg)

The [Palace of Westminster](/wiki/Palace_of_Westminster "Palace of Westminster") in London is the seat of both houses of the Parliament of the United Kingdom.

For [general elections](/wiki/General_elections_in_the_United_Kingdom "General elections in the United Kingdom") (elections to the House of Commons), the United Kingdom is divided into 650 [constituencies](/wiki/United_Kingdom_constituencies "United Kingdom constituencies"), each of which is represented by one [member of Parliament](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament_(United_Kingdom) "Member of Parliament (United Kingdom)") (MP) elected by the [first-past-the-post](/wiki/First-past-the-post_voting "First-past-the-post voting") system.[[187]](#cite_note-parliament.uk-2-207) MPs hold office for up to five years and must then stand for re-election if they wish to continue to be an MP.[[187]](#cite_note-parliament.uk-2-207) The [Conservative Party](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)"), colloquially known as the Tory Party or the Tories, and the [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") have been the dominant political parties in the country since the 1920s, leading to the UK being described as a [two-party system](/wiki/Two-party_system "Two-party system"). However, since the 1920s other [political parties](/wiki/List_of_political_parties_in_the_United_Kingdom "List of political parties in the United Kingdom") have won seats in the House of Commons, although never more than the Conservatives or Labour.[[188]](#cite_note-208)

### Administrative divisions

Main articles: [Countries of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Countries_of_the_United_Kingdom "Countries of the United Kingdom"), [Administrative geography of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Administrative_geography_of_the_United_Kingdom "Administrative geography of the United Kingdom"), and [List of counties in the United Kingdom](/wiki/List_of_counties_in_the_United_Kingdom "List of counties in the United Kingdom")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/United_Kingdom_labelled_map7_vector.svg/250px-United_Kingdom_labelled_map7_vector.svg.png)](/wiki/File:United_Kingdom_labelled_map7_vector.svg)

The four countries of the United Kingdom

The geographical division of the United Kingdom into counties or [shires](/wiki/Shire "Shire") began in England and Scotland in the [early Middle Ages](/wiki/Early_Middle_Ages "Early Middle Ages"), and was completed throughout Great Britain and Ireland by the [early modern period](/wiki/Early_modern_period "Early modern period").[[189]](#cite_note-209) Modern [local government](/wiki/Local_government "Local government") by elected councils, partly based on the ancient counties, was established by separate Acts of Parliament: in England and Wales [in 1888](/wiki/Local_Government_Act_1888 "Local Government Act 1888"), Scotland [in 1889](/wiki/Local_Government_(Scotland)_Act_1889 "Local Government (Scotland) Act 1889") and Ireland [in 1898](/wiki/Local_Government_(Ireland)_Act_1898 "Local Government (Ireland) Act 1898"), meaning there is no consistent system of administrative or geographic demarcation across the UK,[[190]](#cite_note-210) and [England and Wales](/wiki/English_law "English law"), [Scotland](/wiki/Scots_law "Scots law") and [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Law_of_Northern_Ireland "Law of Northern Ireland") each have their own distinct jurisdictions.[[191]](#cite_note-211) Until the 19th century there was little change to those arrangements, but there has since been a constant evolution of role and function.[[192]](#cite_note-212)

[Local government in England](/wiki/Local_government_in_England "Local government in England") is complex, with the distribution of functions varying according to local arrangements.
