The upper-tier [subdivisions of England](/wiki/Subdivisions_of_England "Subdivisions of England") are the nine [regions](/wiki/Regions_of_England "Regions of England"), used primarily for statistical purposes.[[193]](#cite_note-213) One of the regions, [Greater London](/wiki/Greater_London_Authority "Greater London Authority"), has had a directly elected assembly and mayor since 2000 following popular support for the proposal in a [1998 referendum](/wiki/1998_Greater_London_Authority_referendum "1998 Greater London Authority referendum").[[194]](#cite_note-214)

[Local government in Scotland](/wiki/Local_government_in_Scotland "Local government in Scotland") is divided into [32 council areas](/wiki/Subdivisions_of_Scotland "Subdivisions of Scotland") with a wide variation in size and population. The cities of [Glasgow](/wiki/Glasgow "Glasgow"), Edinburgh, [Aberdeen](/wiki/Aberdeen "Aberdeen") and [Dundee](/wiki/Dundee "Dundee") are separate council areas, as is the [Highland Council](/wiki/Politics_of_the_Highland_council_area "Politics of the Highland council area"), which includes a third of Scotland's area but only just over 200,000 people. Local councils are made up of elected councillors, of whom there are 1,223.[[195]](#cite_note-political-studies-association-215)

[Local government in Wales](/wiki/Local_government_in_Wales "Local government in Wales") consists of 22 [unitary authorities](/wiki/Unitary_authority "Unitary authority"), each led by a leader and cabinet elected by the council itself. These include the cities of Cardiff, [Swansea](/wiki/Swansea "Swansea") and [Newport](/wiki/Newport,_Wales "Newport, Wales"), which are unitary authorities in their own right.[[196]](#cite_note-unitary-216) Elections are held every four years under the first-past-the-post system.[[196]](#cite_note-unitary-216)

[Local government in Northern Ireland](/wiki/Local_government_in_Northern_Ireland "Local government in Northern Ireland") since 1973 has been organised into 26 district councils, each elected by single transferable vote. Their powers are limited to services such as waste collection, dog control, and maintaining parks and cemeteries.[[197]](#cite_note-Devenport-217) In 2008 the executive agreed on proposals to create 11 new councils to replace the existing system.[[198]](#cite_note-218)

### Devolution

Main article: [Devolution in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Devolution_in_the_United_Kingdom "Devolution in the United Kingdom")

Further information: [Devolved, reserved and excepted matters](/wiki/Devolved,_reserved_and_excepted_matters "Devolved, reserved and excepted matters")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Council_of_Nations_and_Regions_%2854059219744%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Council_of_Nations_and_Regions_%2854059219744%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Council_of_Nations_and_Regions_(54059219744)_(cropped).jpg)

Prime Minister Keir Starmer meets with the first ministers of Scotland, Northern Ireland and Wales during the [Council of Nations and Regions](/wiki/Council_of_Nations_and_Regions "Council of Nations and Regions") summit.

In the United Kingdom a process of devolution has transferred various powers from the UK Government to three of the four UK countries—Scotland, Northern Ireland and Wales—as well as to the regions of England, which since 1999 have their own governments and parliaments that control various devolved matters.[[199]](#cite_note-219) These powers vary and have been moved to the [Scottish Government](/wiki/Scottish_Government "Scottish Government"), the [Welsh Government](/wiki/Welsh_Government "Welsh Government"), the [Northern Ireland Executive](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Executive "Northern Ireland Executive") and in England, the [Greater London Authority](/wiki/Greater_London_Authority "Greater London Authority") and [Combined Authorities](/wiki/Combined_authority "Combined authority").[[200]](#cite_note-220) Amongst the devolved parliaments across the United Kingdom, the [Scottish Parliament](/wiki/Scottish_Parliament "Scottish Parliament") has the most extensive responsibilities for [devolved powers](/wiki/Devolved,_reserved_and_excepted_matters "Devolved, reserved and excepted matters"), and has been described as "one of the most powerful devolved parliaments in the world".[[201]](#cite_note-221)[[202]](#cite_note-222)

The United Kingdom has an [uncodified constitution](/wiki/Uncodified_constitution "Uncodified constitution") and constitutional matters are not amongst the powers that have been devolved. Under the doctrine of [parliamentary sovereignty](/wiki/Parliamentary_sovereignty_in_the_United_Kingdom "Parliamentary sovereignty in the United Kingdom"), the UK Parliament could, in theory, therefore, abolish the Scottish Parliament, Senedd or Northern Ireland Assembly.[[203]](#cite_note-223) Though in the [Scotland Act 2016](/wiki/Scotland_Act_2016 "Scotland Act 2016") and the [Wales Act 2017](/wiki/Wales_Act_2017 "Wales Act 2017") it states that the Scottish Government and the Welsh Government "are a permanent part of the United Kingdom's constitutional arrangements".[[204]](#cite_note-224)[[205]](#cite_note-225)

In practice it would be politically difficult for the UK Parliament to abolish devolution to the Scottish Parliament and the Senedd because these institutions were created by referendums.[[206]](#cite_note-226) The political constraints placed upon the UK Parliament's power to interfere with devolution in Northern Ireland are greater still, because devolution in Northern Ireland rests upon an international agreement with the [Government of Ireland](/wiki/Government_of_Ireland "Government of Ireland").[[207]](#cite_note-227) The UK Parliament restricts the three devolved parliaments' legislative powers in economic policy matters through [an act](/wiki/United_Kingdom_Internal_Market_Act_2020 "United Kingdom Internal Market Act 2020") passed in 2020.[[208]](#cite_note-UKIM-228)

#### England

Unlike Scotland, Northern Ireland and Wales, England does not have a separate devolved government or national parliament,[[209]](#cite_note-229) rather a process of devolution of powers from the central government to local authorities has taken place, first in 1998.[[210]](#cite_note-230) The [Greater London Authority](/wiki/Greater_London_Authority "Greater London Authority") (GLA) was set up following a [referendum in 1998](/wiki/1998_Greater_London_Authority_referendum "1998 Greater London Authority referendum"). Colloquially known as City Hall, it is the devolved regional government body for Greater London. It consists of two political branches: an [Executive Mayor](/wiki/Mayor_of_London "Mayor of London") and the [London Assembly](/wiki/London_Assembly "London Assembly"), which serves as a check and balance on the Mayor.

A [Combined Authority](/wiki/Combined_Authority "Combined Authority") (CA) is a type of local government institution introduced in England outside Greater London by the [Local Democracy, Economic Development and Construction Act 2009](/wiki/Local_Democracy,_Economic_Development_and_Construction_Act_2009 "Local Democracy, Economic Development and Construction Act 2009"). CAs allow a group of local authorities to pool appropriate responsibility and receive certain devolved functions from central government in order to deliver transport and economic policy more effectively over a wider area.[[211]](#cite_note-231) A Combined County Authority (CCA) is a similar type of local-government institution introduced in England outside Greater London by the [Levelling-up and Regeneration Act 2023](/wiki/Levelling-up_and_Regeneration_Act_2023 "Levelling-up and Regeneration Act 2023"), but may only be formed by upper-tier authorities: [county councils](/wiki/County_council "County council") and [unitary authorities](/wiki/Unitary_authority "Unitary authority").[[212]](#cite_note-232)

#### Scotland

Main article: [Responsibilities of the Scottish Government](/wiki/Responsibilities_of_the_Scottish_Government "Responsibilities of the Scottish Government")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/First_Minister_meets_Prime_Minister_%2853840421342%29.jpg/250px-First_Minister_meets_Prime_Minister_%2853840421342%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:First_Minister_meets_Prime_Minister_(53840421342).jpg)

Prime Minister Keir Starmer meets with [First Minister of Scotland](/wiki/First_Minister_of_Scotland "First Minister of Scotland") [John Swinney](/wiki/John_Swinney "John Swinney"), the head of the [Scottish Government](/wiki/Scottish_Government "Scottish Government"), at [Bute House](/wiki/Bute_House "Bute House"), Edinburgh.

Since 1999 Scotland has had a devolved national government and parliament with wide-ranging powers over any matter that has not been specifically [reserved](/wiki/Reserved_and_excepted_matters "Reserved and excepted matters") to the UK Parliament.[[213]](#cite_note-Scottish_Parliament-233)[[214]](#cite_note-234) Scotland has the most devolved powers of any of the three devolved parliaments in the United Kingdom, with full legislative control over [education](/wiki/Education_in_Scotland "Education in Scotland"), [law and order](/wiki/Scots_law "Scots law"), [the economy](/wiki/Economy_of_Scotland "Economy of Scotland"), [healthcare](/wiki/Health_in_Scotland "Health in Scotland"), [elections](/wiki/Elections_in_Scotland "Elections in Scotland"), [Crown Estate Scotland](/wiki/Crown_Estate_Scotland "Crown Estate Scotland"), the [planning system](/wiki/Planning_system_in_Scotland "Planning system in Scotland") and [housing](/wiki/Housing_in_Scotland "Housing in Scotland").[[215]](#cite_note-235)

Additional powers were transferred to the Scottish Parliament via the [Scotland Act 2012](/wiki/Scotland_Act_2012 "Scotland Act 2012") and the [Scotland Act 2016](/wiki/Scotland_Act_2016 "Scotland Act 2016"), such as [some taxation powers](/wiki/Taxation_in_Scotland "Taxation in Scotland"), including full control of [income tax](/wiki/Income_tax_in_Scotland "Income tax in Scotland") on income earned through employment, [Land and Buildings Transaction Tax](/wiki/Land_and_Buildings_Transaction_Tax "Land and Buildings Transaction Tax"), [Landfill Tax](/wiki/Scottish_Landfill_Tax "Scottish Landfill Tax"), Aggregates Levy, [Air Departure Tax](/wiki/Air_Departure_Tax "Air Departure Tax") and [Revenue Scotland](/wiki/Revenue_Scotland "Revenue Scotland"), as well as aspects of the [energy network](/wiki/Energy_in_Scotland "Energy in Scotland"), including [renewable energy](/wiki/Renewable_energy_in_Scotland "Renewable energy in Scotland"), energy efficiency and onshore [oil and gas licensing](/wiki/Oil_industry_in_Scotland "Oil industry in Scotland").[[216]](#cite_note-Devolved_Matters_in_Scotland-236)[[213]](#cite_note-Scottish_Parliament-233) Their power over economic issues is significantly constrained by an [act of the UK Parliament](/wiki/United_Kingdom_Internal_Market_Act_2020 "United Kingdom Internal Market Act 2020") passed in 2020.[[208]](#cite_note-UKIM-228)

The [Scottish Government](/wiki/Scottish_Government "Scottish Government") is a [Scottish National Party](/wiki/Scottish_National_Party "Scottish National Party") (SNP) [minority government](/wiki/Minority_government "Minority government"),[[224]](#cite_note-244) led by the [first minister](/wiki/First_Minister_of_Scotland "First Minister of Scotland"), currently [John Swinney](/wiki/John_Swinney "John Swinney"), the [leader of the SNP](/wiki/Leader_of_the_SNP "Leader of the SNP"). In 2014 the [Scottish independence referendum](/wiki/2014_Scottish_independence_referendum "2014 Scottish independence referendum") was held, with 55.3 per cent voting against independence from the United Kingdom and 44.7 per cent voting in favour, resulting in Scotland staying within the United Kingdom. [Local government in Scotland](/wiki/Local_government_in_Scotland "Local government in Scotland") is divided into [32 council areas](/wiki/Subdivisions_of_Scotland "Subdivisions of Scotland") with a wide variation in size and population. Local councils are made up of elected councillors, of whom there are 1,223.[[195]](#cite_note-political-studies-association-215)

The Scottish Parliament is separate from the Scottish Government. It is made up of 129 elected [members of the Scottish Parliament](/wiki/Members_of_the_Scottish_Parliament "Members of the Scottish Parliament") (MSPs). It is the law-making body of Scotland, and thus it scrutinises the work of the incumbent Scottish Government and considers any piece of proposed legislation through parliamentary debates, committees and parliamentary questions.[[216]](#cite_note-Devolved_Matters_in_Scotland-236)

#### Wales

Since 1999 Wales has had a devolved national government and legislature, known as the Senedd. Elections to the Senedd use the [additional member system](/wiki/Additional_member_system "Additional member system"). It has more limited powers than those devolved to Scotland.[[225]](#cite_note-245) The Senedd can legislate on any matter not specifically reserved to the UK Parliament by [Acts of Senedd Cymru](/wiki/Acts_of_Senedd_Cymru "Acts of Senedd Cymru"). The [Welsh Government](/wiki/Welsh_Government "Welsh Government") is currently a [Welsh Labour](/wiki/Welsh_Labour "Welsh Labour") minority government led by the [first minister](/wiki/First_Minister_of_Wales "First Minister of Wales"), [Eluned Morgan](/wiki/Eluned_Morgan "Eluned Morgan"). [Local government in Wales](/wiki/Local_government_in_Wales "Local government in Wales") consists of 22 unitary authorities, each led by a leader and cabinet elected by the council itself.

#### Northern Ireland

The devolved form of government in Northern Ireland is based on the 1998 [Good Friday Agreement](/wiki/Good_Friday_Agreement "Good Friday Agreement"), which brought to an end a 30-year period of [unionist](/wiki/Unionism_in_Ireland "Unionism in Ireland")-[nationalist](/wiki/Irish_nationalism "Irish nationalism") communal conflict known as [the Troubles](/wiki/The_Troubles "The Troubles"). The Agreement was [confirmed by referendum](/wiki/1998_Northern_Ireland_Good_Friday_Agreement_referendum "1998 Northern Ireland Good Friday Agreement referendum") and implemented later that year. It established [power sharing](/wiki/Power_sharing "Power sharing") arrangements for a devolved government and legislature, referred to as the [Northern Ireland Executive](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Executive "Northern Ireland Executive") and the [Northern Ireland Assembly](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Assembly "Northern Ireland Assembly") respectively.[[226]](#cite_note-246) Elections to the Assembly use the [single transferable vote](/wiki/Single_transferable_vote "Single transferable vote") system. The Executive and Assembly have powers similar to those devolved to Scotland.[[227]](#cite_note-247) The Executive is led by a [diarchy](/wiki/Diarchy "Diarchy") representing [unionist](/wiki/Designated_Unionist "Designated Unionist") and [nationalist](/wiki/Designated_Nationalist "Designated Nationalist") members of the Assembly.[[228]](#cite_note-northernireland1-248) The [first minister and deputy first minister of Northern Ireland](/wiki/First_Minister_and_Deputy_First_Minister_of_Northern_Ireland "First Minister and Deputy First Minister of Northern Ireland") are the joint heads of government of Northern Ireland.[[229]](#cite_note-249)[[230]](#cite_note-250) [Local government in Northern Ireland](/wiki/Local_government_in_Northern_Ireland "Local government in Northern Ireland") since 2015 has been divided between 11 councils with limited responsibilities.[[197]](#cite_note-Devenport-217)

### Foreign relations

Main article: [Foreign relations of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_the_United_Kingdom "Foreign relations of the United Kingdom")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/Trump-Starmer_bilateral_2025-02-27-16-07.jpg/250px-Trump-Starmer_bilateral_2025-02-27-16-07.jpg)](/wiki/File:Trump-Starmer_bilateral_2025-02-27-16-07.jpg)

UK prime minister Keir Starmer and US president [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") shaking hands in a joint press conference in 2025. The UK and the US share a "[Special Relationship](/wiki/Special_Relationship "Special Relationship")".

The United Kingdom is [a permanent member](/wiki/Big_Five_(United_Nations) "Big Five (United Nations)") of the [United Nations Security Council](/wiki/United_Nations_Security_Council "United Nations Security Council") and a member of [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO"), [AUKUS](/wiki/AUKUS "AUKUS"), the Commonwealth of Nations, the [G7](/wiki/G7 "G7"), the [G20](/wiki/G-20_major_economies "G-20 major economies"), the [OECD](/wiki/Organisation_for_Economic_Co-operation_and_Development "Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development"), the [WTO](/wiki/World_Trade_Organization "World Trade Organization"), the [Council of Europe](/wiki/Council_of_Europe "Council of Europe") and the [OSCE](/wiki/Organization_for_Security_and_Co-operation_in_Europe "Organization for Security and Co-operation in Europe").[[231]](#cite_note-251) It maintains the [British Council](/wiki/British_Council "British Council"), an organisation specialising in international cultural and educational opportunities in over 100 countries. The UK remains a [great power](/wiki/Great_power "Great power") with considerable political, cultural, economic and military influence.[[232]](#cite_note-PaulWirtz2004-252)[[233]](#cite_note-253)

The United Kingdom is said to have a "[Special Relationship](/wiki/Special_Relationship "Special Relationship")" with the United States and a close partnership with France – the "[Entente cordiale](/wiki/Entente_cordiale "Entente cordiale")" – and shares nuclear weapons technology with both countries;[[234]](#cite_note-254)[[235]](#cite_note-255) the [Anglo-Portuguese Alliance](/wiki/Anglo-Portuguese_Alliance "Anglo-Portuguese Alliance") is considered to be the oldest binding military alliance in the world. The UK is also closely linked with the Republic of Ireland; the two countries share a [Common Travel Area](/wiki/Common_Travel_Area "Common Travel Area") and co-operate through the [British-Irish Intergovernmental Conference](/wiki/British-Irish_Intergovernmental_Conference "British-Irish Intergovernmental Conference") and the [British-Irish Council](/wiki/British-Irish_Council "British-Irish Council"). Britain's global presence and influence is further amplified through its trading relations, foreign investments, [official development assistance](/wiki/Official_development_assistance "Official development assistance") and military engagements.[[236]](#cite_note-256)

### Military

Main articles: [British Armed Forces](/wiki/British_Armed_Forces "British Armed Forces") and [Military history of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Military_history_of_the_United_Kingdom "Military history of the United Kingdom")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/United_Kingdom_overseas_military_installations_and_operations.png/500px-United_Kingdom_overseas_military_installations_and_operations.png)](/wiki/File:United_Kingdom_overseas_military_installations_and_operations.png)

Overseas military installations of the United Kingdom, and locally raised units of the [British Overseas Territories](/wiki/British_Overseas_Territories "British Overseas Territories")

Military interventions since 2000: [Palliser](/wiki/British_military_intervention_in_the_Sierra_Leone_Civil_War "British military intervention in the Sierra Leone Civil War") (Sierra Leone); [Herrick](/wiki/Operation_Herrick "Operation Herrick") (Afghanistan); [Enduring Freedom](/wiki/Operation_Enduring_Freedom_%E2%80%93_Horn_of_Africa "Operation Enduring Freedom – Horn of Africa") (Horn of Africa); [Telic](/wiki/Operation_Telic "Operation Telic") (Iraq); [Ellamy](/wiki/Operation_Ellamy "Operation Ellamy") (Libya); and [Shader](/wiki/Operation_Shader "Operation Shader") (Islamic State of Iraq and the Levant).

The British Armed Forces consist of three professional service branches: the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy") and [Royal Marines](/wiki/Royal_Marines "Royal Marines") (forming the [Naval Service](/wiki/Naval_Service_(United_Kingdom) "Naval Service (United Kingdom)")), the [British Army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army") and the [Royal Air Force](/wiki/Royal_Air_Force "Royal Air Force").[[237]](#cite_note-257) The armed forces of the United Kingdom are managed by the [Ministry of Defence](/wiki/Ministry_of_Defence_(United_Kingdom) "Ministry of Defence (United Kingdom)") and controlled by the [Defence Council](/wiki/Defence_Council_of_the_United_Kingdom "Defence Council of the United Kingdom"), chaired by the [Secretary of State for Defence](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_Defence "Secretary of State for Defence"). The [Commander-in-Chief](/wiki/Commander-in-Chief "Commander-in-Chief") is the [British monarch](/wiki/Monarchy_of_the_United_Kingdom "Monarchy of the United Kingdom"), to whom members of the forces swear an oath of allegiance.[[238]](#cite_note-258)

The armed forces are charged with protecting the United Kingdom and its overseas territories, promoting the UK's global security interests and supporting international peacekeeping efforts. They are active and regular participants in [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") (including the [Allied Rapid Reaction Corps](/wiki/Allied_Rapid_Reaction_Corps "Allied Rapid Reaction Corps")), the [Five Power Defence Arrangements](/wiki/Five_Power_Defence_Arrangements "Five Power Defence Arrangements"), [RIMPAC](/wiki/RIMPAC "RIMPAC") and other worldwide coalition operations. [Overseas garrisons](/wiki/Overseas_military_bases_of_the_United_Kingdom "Overseas military bases of the United Kingdom") and facilities are maintained in [Ascension Island](/wiki/RAF_Ascension_Island "RAF Ascension Island"), [Bahrain](/wiki/Mina_Salman "Mina Salman"), [Belize](/wiki/Military_of_Belize "Military of Belize"), [Brunei](/wiki/Military_Forces_based_in_Brunei "Military Forces based in Brunei"), [Canada](/wiki/British_Army_Training_Unit_Suffield "British Army Training Unit Suffield"), [Cyprus](/wiki/British_Forces_Cyprus "British Forces Cyprus"), [Diego Garcia](/wiki/Diego_Garcia "Diego Garcia"), the [Falkland Islands](/wiki/Military_of_the_Falkland_Islands "Military of the Falkland Islands"), [Germany](/wiki/British_Forces_Germany "British Forces Germany"), [Gibraltar](/wiki/British_Forces_Gibraltar "British Forces Gibraltar"), [Kenya](/wiki/British_Army_Training_Unit_Kenya "British Army Training Unit Kenya"), [Oman](/wiki/Oman "Oman"), [Qatar](/wiki/Al_Udeid_Air_Base "Al Udeid Air Base") and [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore").[[239]](#cite_note-259)

According to the [Stockholm International Peace Research Institute](/wiki/Stockholm_International_Peace_Research_Institute "Stockholm International Peace Research Institute")[[240]](#cite_note-SIPRI-2020-260) and the [International Institute for Strategic Studies](/wiki/International_Institute_for_Strategic_Studies "International Institute for Strategic Studies"),[[241]](#cite_note-261) the UK had the world's [sixth- or fifth-highest military expenditures](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_military_expenditures "List of countries by military expenditures") in 2024. Total defence spending in 2024 was estimated at 2.3 per cent of [gross domestic product](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product").[[242]](#cite_note-262) Following the end of the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War"), defence policy has a stated assumption that "the most demanding operations" will be undertaken as part of a coalition.[[243]](#cite_note-263)

### Law and criminal justice

Main articles: [Law of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Law_of_the_United_Kingdom "Law of the United Kingdom") and [Crime in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Crime_in_the_United_Kingdom "Crime in the United Kingdom")

Further information: [English law](/wiki/English_law "English law"), [Northern Ireland law](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_law "Northern Ireland law"), and [Scots law](/wiki/Scots_law "Scots law")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Middlesex_Guildhall_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Middlesex_Guildhall_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Middlesex_Guildhall_(cropped).jpg)

The [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_Kingdom "Supreme Court of the United Kingdom") is the final court of appeal for England, Wales and Northern Ireland, and for Scottish civil cases.

The United Kingdom does not have a single legal system as the [1706 Treaty of Union](/wiki/Treaty_of_Union "Treaty of Union") provided for the continuation of Scotland's separate legal system.[[25]](#cite_note-Acts_of_Union_1707-41) The UK has three distinct [systems of law](/wiki/Legal_systems_of_the_world "Legal systems of the world"): English law, Northern Ireland law and Scots law. A new [Supreme Court of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_Kingdom "Supreme Court of the United Kingdom") came into being in October 2009 to replace the [Appellate Committee of the House of Lords](/wiki/Judicial_functions_of_the_House_of_Lords "Judicial functions of the House of Lords").[[244]](#cite_note-264) The [Judicial Committee of the Privy Council](/wiki/Judicial_Committee_of_the_Privy_Council "Judicial Committee of the Privy Council"), including the same members as the Supreme Court, is the highest court of appeal for several independent Commonwealth countries, the [British Overseas Territories](/wiki/British_Overseas_Territories "British Overseas Territories") and the [Crown Dependencies](/wiki/Crown_Dependencies "Crown Dependencies").[[245]](#cite_note-265)

Both English law, which applies in England and Wales, and Northern Ireland law are based on [common law](/wiki/Common_law "Common law") (or [case law](/wiki/Case_law "Case law")) principles.[[246]](#cite_note-266) It originated in England in the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") and is the basis for many legal systems around the world.[[247]](#cite_note-267) The [courts of England and Wales](/wiki/Courts_of_England_and_Wales "Courts of England and Wales") are headed by the [Senior Courts of England and Wales](/wiki/Senior_Courts_of_England_and_Wales "Senior Courts of England and Wales"), consisting of the [Court of Appeal](/wiki/Court_of_Appeal_of_England_and_Wales "Court of Appeal of England and Wales"), the [High Court of Justice](/wiki/High_Court_of_Justice "High Court of Justice") for civil cases and the [Crown Court](/wiki/Crown_Court "Crown Court") for criminal cases.[[248]](#cite_note-268) Scots law is a hybrid system based on common-law and [civil-law](/wiki/Civil_law_(legal_system) "Civil law (legal system)") principles. The chief courts are the [Court of Session](/wiki/Court_of_Session "Court of Session"), for civil cases,[[249]](#cite_note-269) and the [High Court of Justiciary](/wiki/High_Court_of_Justiciary "High Court of Justiciary"), for criminal cases.[[250]](#cite_note-270) The Supreme Court of the United Kingdom serves as the highest court of appeal for civil cases under Scots law.[[251]](#cite_note-271)

Crime in England and Wales increased between 1981 and 1995. Since that peak there has been an overall fall of 66% in recorded crime from 1995 to 2015,[[252]](#cite_note-272) according to [UK crime statistics](/wiki/Crime_statistics_in_the_United_Kingdom "Crime statistics in the United Kingdom"). As of June 2023, the United Kingdom has the highest per-capita incarceration rate in Western Europe.[[253]](#cite_note-273)[[254]](#cite_note-274)[[255]](#cite_note-275)

[UK labour laws](/wiki/United_Kingdom_labour_law "United Kingdom labour law") establish employment rights including [a minimum wage](/wiki/National_Minimum_Wage_Act_1998 "National Minimum Wage Act 1998"), a minimum of 28 days annual holiday, parental leave, statutory sick pay and [a pension](/wiki/Pensions_Act_2008 "Pensions Act 2008"). [Same-sex marriage](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage "Same-sex marriage") has been legal in England, Scotland, and Wales since 2014, and in [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland") since 2020.[[256]](#cite_note-276) [LGBT equality](/wiki/LGBT_rights_in_the_United_Kingdom "LGBT rights in the United Kingdom") in the United Kingdom is considered advanced by modern standards.[[257]](#cite_note-277)[[258]](#cite_note-278)

Since leaving the [EU](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") most disputes under UK-EU agreements are addressed through consultation between the parties. If consultation fails to resolve the issue, either party can request [arbitration](/wiki/Arbitration "Arbitration"), typically at the [PCA](/wiki/Permanent_Court_of_Arbitration "Permanent Court of Arbitration") in [The Hague](/wiki/Peace_Palace "Peace Palace").[[127]](#cite_note-commission.europa.eu-145)[[259]](#cite_note-279)[[260]](#cite_note-280) The [EU–UK Trade and Cooperation Agreement](/wiki/EU%E2%80%93UK_Trade_and_Cooperation_Agreement "EU–UK Trade and Cooperation Agreement") states that the UK and EU have to cooperate and negotiate with each other with 'full mutual respect and good faith', as defined by international law.[[261]](#cite_note-281) Under the [Windsor Framework](/wiki/Windsor_Framework "Windsor Framework"), [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland") matters requiring interpretation of EU law go to the [ECJ](/wiki/European_Court_of_Justice "European Court of Justice"), though the [Stormont Brake](/wiki/Stormont_Brake "Stormont Brake") can prevent new EU rules from taking effect.

## Economy

Main article: [Economy of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Economy_of_the_United_Kingdom "Economy of the United Kingdom")

Further information: [Economy of England](/wiki/Economy_of_England "Economy of England"), [Economy of Scotland](/wiki/Economy_of_Scotland "Economy of Scotland"), [Economy of Northern Ireland](/wiki/Economy_of_Northern_Ireland "Economy of Northern Ireland"), and [Economy of Wales](/wiki/Economy_of_Wales "Economy of Wales")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/Bank_junction_-_2020-09-20_%282%29.jpg/250px-Bank_junction_-_2020-09-20_%282%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bank_junction_-_2020-09-20_(2).jpg)

[City of London](/wiki/City_of_London "City of London") skyline from [London City Hall](/wiki/City_Hall,_London_(Southwark) "City Hall, London (Southwark)"). [London](/wiki/London "London") is Europe's largest financial centre.[[262]](#cite_note-282)

The United Kingdom has a highly developed [social market economy](/wiki/Social_market_economy "Social market economy").[[263]](#cite_note-283)[[264]](#cite_note-ftms-284) With an estimated nominal GDP of £2.765 trillion in 2024,[[265]](#cite_note-285) it is the [sixth-largest national economy](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(nominal) "List of countries by GDP (nominal)") in the world and the [second-largest](/wiki/List_of_sovereign_states_in_Europe_by_GDP_(nominal) "List of sovereign states in Europe by GDP (nominal)") in Europe. Its currency, the [pound sterling](/wiki/Pound_sterling "Pound sterling"), is the [fourth-most-traded currency](/wiki/Foreign_exchange_market#Most_traded_currencies_by_value "Foreign exchange market") in the [foreign exchange market](/wiki/Foreign_exchange_market "Foreign exchange market") and the world's fourth-largest [reserve currency](/wiki/Reserve_currency "Reserve currency"), after the [United States dollar](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar"), the [euro](/wiki/Euro "Euro") and the [yen](/wiki/Yen "Yen").[[266]](#cite_note-286) The pound sterling maintains its high nominal value through both its long history of stability and by never undergoing formal [redenomination](/wiki/Redenomination "Redenomination"). Since 2022 the UK has been both the world's [fourth-largest exporter](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_exports "List of countries by exports")[[267]](#cite_note-287) and [fourth-largest importer](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_imports "List of countries by imports")[[268]](#cite_note-288) of goods and services. Despite having one of the highest levels of [income inequality](/wiki/Income_inequality_in_the_United_Kingdom "Income inequality in the United Kingdom") in the [OECD](/wiki/OECD "OECD"),[[269]](#cite_note-289)[[270]](#cite_note-290) the UK has a very high [HDI](/wiki/Human_Development_Index "Human Development Index") ranking, including when [adjusted for inequality](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_inequality-adjusted_Human_Development_Index "List of countries by inequality-adjusted Human Development Index"). As of 2025 the [UK unemployment rate](/wiki/Unemployment_in_the_United_Kingdom "Unemployment in the United Kingdom") is 4.7%,[[271]](#cite_note-291) which is moderately low [by European standards](/wiki/List_of_sovereign_states_in_Europe_by_unemployment_rate "List of sovereign states in Europe by unemployment rate").

[HM Treasury](/wiki/HM_Treasury "HM Treasury"), led by the [Chancellor of the Exchequer](/wiki/Chancellor_of_the_Exchequer "Chancellor of the Exchequer"), is responsible for developing and executing the government's [public finance](/wiki/Public_finance "Public finance") and [economic policy](/wiki/Economic_policy "Economic policy"). The [Department for Business and Trade](/wiki/Department_for_Business_and_Trade "Department for Business and Trade") is responsible for business, international trade, and enterprise. The [Bank of England](/wiki/Bank_of_England "Bank of England") is the UK's [central bank](/wiki/Central_bank "Central bank") and responsible for issuing notes and coins in the pound sterling. Banks in Scotland and Northern Ireland retain the right to issue their own notes, subject to retaining enough Bank of England notes in reserve to cover their issue.

### Industries and services

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Bank-of-England.jpg/250px-Bank-of-England.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bank-of-England.jpg)

The [Bank of England](/wiki/Bank_of_England "Bank of England") is the [central bank](/wiki/Central_bank "Central bank") of the United Kingdom and the model on which most modern central banks have been based.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/British_Airways_Concorde_G-BOAC_03.jpg/250px-British_Airways_Concorde_G-BOAC_03.jpg)](/wiki/File:British_Airways_Concorde_G-BOAC_03.jpg)

[Concorde](/wiki/Concorde "Concorde") was a [supersonic](/wiki/Supersonic_speed "Supersonic speed") airliner that reduced transatlantic flight time from 8 hours to 3.5 hours.[[272]](#cite_note-292)

The [service sector](/wiki/Tertiary_sector_of_the_economy "Tertiary sector of the economy") made up around 80 per cent of the UK's [GVA](/wiki/Gross_value_added "Gross value added") in 2023.[[273]](#cite_note-293) As of 2023 it is the world's [second-largest exporter of services](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_service_exports "List of countries by service exports")[[274]](#cite_note-294) and in 2024 was the world's largest net exporter of [financial services](/wiki/Financial_services_in_the_United_Kingdom "Financial services in the United Kingdom").[[275]](#cite_note-295) [London](/wiki/London "London") is the world capital for foreign exchange trading, with a market share of 37.8 per cent in 2022 of the global turnover.[[276]](#cite_note-296) It is the largest urban economy in Europe[[277]](#cite_note-297) and, alongside [New York](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), the city in the world most integrated with the [global economy](/wiki/World_economy "World economy").[[278]](#cite_note-298) London is also one of the world's leading financial centres, ranking second in the 2025 [Global Financial Centres Index](/wiki/Global_Financial_Centres_Index "Global Financial Centres Index").[[279]](#cite_note-www.longfinance.net-299) [Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh "Edinburgh"), the UK's second-largest financial centre, ranks 29th in the world in the same index.[[279]](#cite_note-www.longfinance.net-299)

The [UK's manufacturing sector](/wiki/Manufacturing_in_the_United_Kingdom "Manufacturing in the United Kingdom") in 2024 was the world's [10th-largest](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_manufacturing_output "List of countries by manufacturing output") and Europe's fourth-largest by value output.[[280]](#cite_note-300) At the end of 2024 manufacturing in the United Kingdom accounted for 8 per cent of the workforce and 8.6 per cent of national economic output.[[281]](#cite_note-301) As reported in 2017 the [East Midlands](/wiki/East_Midlands_Manufacturing "East Midlands Manufacturing") and [West Midlands](/wiki/West_Midlands_Manufacturing "West Midlands Manufacturing") (at 12.6 and 11.8 per cent respectively) were the regions with the highest proportion of employees in manufacturing. [London's manufacturing sector](/wiki/London_manufacturing "London manufacturing") had the lowest at 2.8 per cent.[[282]](#cite_note-302)

The [country's tourism sector](/wiki/Tourism_in_the_United_Kingdom "Tourism in the United Kingdom") is important to the British economy;[[283]](#cite_note-303) The [creative industries](/wiki/Creative_industries "Creative industries") accounted for 5.9 per cent of the UK's GVA in 2019,[[284]](#cite_note-304) and contributed £111 billion to the UK economy in 2018.[[285]](#cite_note-305) [Lloyd's of London](/wiki/Lloyd%27s_of_London "Lloyd's of London"), located in London, is the world's largest [insurance](/wiki/Insurance "Insurance") and [reinsurance](/wiki/Reinsurance "Reinsurance") market.[[286]](#cite_note-306) [WPP plc](/wiki/WPP_plc "WPP plc") is one of the world's biggest advertising companies and also based in London.[[287]](#cite_note-307) The UK is one of Europe's leading retail markets and its largest [e-commerce](/wiki/E-commerce "E-commerce") market.[[288]](#cite_note-308) With [consumption expenditures](/wiki/Consumer_spending "Consumer spending") of over US$2 trillion in 2023, it has the [second-largest consumer market](/wiki/List_of_largest_consumer_markets "List of largest consumer markets") in Europe.[[289]](#cite_note-309) [John Lewis](/wiki/John_Lewis_Partnership "John Lewis Partnership") is the UK's largest employee-owned business.[[290]](#cite_note-310)

The [British automotive industry](/wiki/Automotive_industry_in_the_United_Kingdom "Automotive industry in the United Kingdom") generates £47 billion of exports (12 per cent of the UK's total exports of goods).[[291]](#cite_note-Auto_Output_2025-311) In 2024 the UK produced 779,584 passenger vehicles and 125,649 commercial vehicles; 8 out of 10 cars produced in the UK are exported overseas.[[291]](#cite_note-Auto_Output_2025-311) Britain is known for iconic cars like [Mini](/wiki/Mini_Hatch "Mini Hatch") and [Jaguar](/wiki/Jaguar_Cars "Jaguar Cars"),[[292]](#cite_note-312) as well as luxury cars such as [Rolls-Royce](/wiki/Rolls-Royce_Motor_Cars "Rolls-Royce Motor Cars"), [Bentley](/wiki/Bentley "Bentley") and [Range Rover](/wiki/Range_Rover "Range Rover"). The UK is a major centre for engine manufacturing: 1.59 million engines were produced in 2024.[[291]](#cite_note-Auto_Output_2025-311) It was the world's [third-largest exporter of engines](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_engine_exports "List of countries by engine exports") by value in 2023.[[293]](#cite_note-313) The [UK motorsport](/wiki/Motorsport_in_the_United_Kingdom "Motorsport in the United Kingdom") industry has an annual turnover of around £10 billion.[[294]](#cite_note-314) Seven of the eleven [Formula One](/wiki/Formula_One "Formula One") teams are based in the UK, with their technology being used in supercars and hypercars from [McLaren](/wiki/McLaren_Automotive "McLaren Automotive"), [Aston Martin](/wiki/Aston_Martin "Aston Martin") and [Lotus](/wiki/Lotus_Cars "Lotus Cars").[[u]](#cite_note-315) The [aerospace industry of the UK](/wiki/Aerospace_industry_in_the_United_Kingdom "Aerospace industry in the United Kingdom") is the second-largest in the world[[295]](#cite_note-316) and has an annual turnover of around £30 billion.[[296]](#cite_note-317) The [UK space industry](/wiki/British_space_programme "British space programme") was worth £17.5 billion in 2020/21 and employed around 48,800 people.[[297]](#cite_note-318)[[298]](#cite_note-319) The [UK Space Agency](/wiki/UK_Space_Agency "UK Space Agency") has stated in 2023 that it is investing £1.6 billion in space-related projects.[[299]](#cite_note-320)

The [British agriculture industry](/wiki/Agriculture_in_the_United_Kingdom "Agriculture in the United Kingdom") is intensive, highly mechanised and efficient by European standards, producing approximately 60 per cent of the country's overall food requirements and 73 per cent of its indigenous food needs, utilising around 0.9 per cent of the labour force (292,000 workers).[[300]](#cite_note-321) Around two-thirds of production is devoted to [livestock](/wiki/Livestock "Livestock") and one-third to [arable crops](/wiki/Arable_crops "Arable crops"). The UK retains a significant though much reduced fishing industry, with at least 49 per cent of UK fish sustainably caught in 2020.[[301]](#cite_note-322) The UK marine natural capital assets were estimated to have a value of £211 billion in 2021.[[302]](#cite_note-323) It is rich in a variety of other natural resources including coal, [petroleum](/wiki/Petroleum "Petroleum"), [natural gas](/wiki/Natural_gas "Natural gas"), tin, limestone, iron ore, salt, clay, chalk, [gypsum](/wiki/Gypsum "Gypsum"), lead, and silica and has an abundance of arable land.[[303]](#cite_note-324)

### Science and technology

Main articles: [Science and technology in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Science_and_technology_in_the_United_Kingdom "Science and technology in the United Kingdom") and [Telecommunications in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Telecommunications_in_the_United_Kingdom "Telecommunications in the United Kingdom")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Cambridge_Science_Park_Napp.jpg/250px-Cambridge_Science_Park_Napp.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cambridge_Science_Park_Napp.jpg)

The [Cambridge Cluster](/wiki/Cambridge_Cluster "Cambridge Cluster") is the most intensive research cluster for science and technology in the world.[[304]](#cite_note-325)

England and Scotland were leading centres of the [Scientific Revolution](/wiki/Scientific_Revolution "Scientific Revolution") from the 17th century.[[305]](#cite_note-326) The United Kingdom led the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution") from the 18th century, and has continued to produce scientists and [engineers](/wiki/List_of_British_engineers "List of British engineers") credited with [important advances](/wiki/List_of_British_innovations_and_discoveries "List of British innovations and discoveries").[[306]](#cite_note-327) Major theorists from the 17th and 18th centuries include [Isaac Newton](/wiki/Isaac_Newton "Isaac Newton"), whose [laws of motion](/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion "Newton's laws of motion") and illumination of [gravity](/wiki/Gravitation "Gravitation") have been seen as a keystone of modern science;[[307]](#cite_note-328) from the 19th century [Charles Darwin](/wiki/Charles_Darwin "Charles Darwin"), whose theory of [evolution](/wiki/Evolution "Evolution") by [natural selection](/wiki/Natural_selection "Natural selection") was fundamental to the development of modern biology, and [James Clerk Maxwell](/wiki/James_Clerk_Maxwell "James Clerk Maxwell"), who formulated classical [electromagnetic theory](/wiki/Electromagnetic_theory "Electromagnetic theory"); and more recently [Stephen Hawking](/wiki/Stephen_Hawking "Stephen Hawking"), who advanced major theories in the fields of [cosmology](/wiki/Cosmology "Cosmology"), [quantum gravity](/wiki/Quantum_gravity "Quantum gravity") and the investigation of [black holes](/wiki/Black_holes "Black holes").[[308]](#cite_note-329)

The [Department for Science, Innovation and Technology](/wiki/Department_for_Science,_Innovation_and_Technology "Department for Science, Innovation and Technology") (DSIT) is responsible for helping to develop and manage the UK's scientific, research, and technological outputs. Scientific research and development remains important in [British universities](/wiki/Universities_in_the_United_Kingdom "Universities in the United Kingdom"), with many establishing [science parks](/wiki/Science_park "Science park") to facilitate production and co-operation with industry.[[309]](#cite_note-330) In 2022, the UK produced [6.0 per cent of the world's scientific research papers](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_number_of_scientific_and_technical_journal_articles "List of countries by number of scientific and technical journal articles") and had a 8.8 per cent share of scientific citations, the fourth and third highest among G7 countries and Brazil, China, India, Russia, and South Korea. The UK ranked 1st among these countries for Field-Weighted Citation Impact.[[310]](#cite_note-331) Scientific journals produced in the UK include publications by the [Royal Society](/wiki/Royal_Society "Royal Society"), *[Nature](/wiki/Nature_(journal) "Nature (journal)")*, the *[British Medical Journal](/wiki/BMJ "BMJ")* and *[The Lancet](/wiki/The_Lancet "The Lancet")*.[[311]](#cite_note-332)

By 2024 the UK tech sector reached a value of US$1.2 trillion surpassing the combined values of the French and German sectors.[[312]](#cite_note-333) Cambridge was named the number one university in the world for producing successful technology founders.[[313]](#cite_note-334) The [UK's artificial intelligence industry](/wiki/Artificial_intelligence_industry_in_the_United_Kingdom "Artificial intelligence industry in the United Kingdom") is the largest in Europe by value[[314]](#cite_note-335) and the country ranked third globally in a 2024 report on artificial intelligence development by [Stanford University](/wiki/Stanford_University "Stanford University").[[315]](#cite_note-336) The UK ranked 6th in the 2025 [Global Innovation Index](/wiki/Global_Innovation_Index "Global Innovation Index").[[316]](#cite_note-337)[[317]](#cite_note-338)

### Transport

Main article: [Transport in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Transport_in_the_United_Kingdom "Transport in the United Kingdom")

Further information: [Transport in England](/wiki/Transport_in_England "Transport in England"), [Transport in Scotland](/wiki/Transport_in_Scotland "Transport in Scotland"), [Transport in Wales](/wiki/Transport_in_Wales "Transport in Wales"), and [Transport in Northern Ireland](/wiki/Transport_in_Northern_Ireland "Transport in Northern Ireland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/An_LNER_Azuma_train_on_the_East_Coast_Railway_Line%2C_geograph_6275180_by_Walter_Baxter.jpg/250px-An_LNER_Azuma_train_on_the_East_Coast_Railway_Line%2C_geograph_6275180_by_Walter_Baxter.jpg)](/wiki/File:An_LNER_Azuma_train_on_the_East_Coast_Railway_Line,_geograph_6275180_by_Walter_Baxter.jpg)

A high-speed [East Coast Main Line](/wiki/East_Coast_Main_Line "East Coast Main Line") train in [Northumberland](/wiki/Northumberland "Northumberland"), England

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/London_City_Airport_Sunset.jpg/250px-London_City_Airport_Sunset.jpg)](/wiki/File:London_City_Airport_Sunset.jpg)

A plane taking off from [London City Airport](/wiki/London_City_Airport "London City Airport"). [London's airports](/wiki/Airports_of_London "Airports of London") make it the city with the [busiest airport system](/wiki/List_of_busiest_city_airport_systems_by_passenger_traffic "List of busiest city airport systems by passenger traffic") in the world.

In the UK, all traffic [drives on the left](/wiki/Left-_and_right-hand_traffic "Left- and right-hand traffic"), with distance and speed being measured in miles, yards and miles per hour (mph). All vehicles manufactured for use in the UK have the steering wheel situated on the right side of the vehicle. A radial road network totals 29,145 miles (46,904 km) of main roads, 2,173 miles (3,497 km) of motorways and 213,750 miles (344,000 km) of paved roads.[[136]](#cite_note-factbook-154) The [M25](/wiki/M25_motorway "M25 motorway"), encircling London, is the largest and busiest bypass in the world.[[318]](#cite_note-339) In 2024 there were 41.7 million licensed vehicles in the UK.[[319]](#cite_note-340)

The UK has an extensive railway network of 10,072 miles (16,209 km). In Great Britain, the [British Rail](/wiki/British_Rail "British Rail") network [was privatised](/wiki/Privatisation_of_British_Rail "Privatisation of British Rail") between 1994 and 1997, followed by a rapid rise in passengers. [Great British Railways](/wiki/Great_British_Railways "Great British Railways") is a planned state-owned public body that will oversee [rail transport in Great Britain](/wiki/Rail_transport_in_Great_Britain "Rail transport in Great Britain"). The UK was ranked eighth amongst national European rail systems in the 2017 European Railway Performance Index assessing intensity of use, quality of service and safety.[[320]](#cite_note-341)

A train runs directly from London to [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris").[[321]](#cite_note-342) Called the [Eurostar](/wiki/Eurostar "Eurostar"), it travels through the [Channel Tunnel](/wiki/Channel_Tunnel "Channel Tunnel") under the [English Channel](/wiki/English_Channel "English Channel"). At 23.5 miles long, it is the world's longest undersea tunnel.[[322]](#cite_note-343) There is also a car service through the tunnel to France called [LeShuttle](/wiki/LeShuttle "LeShuttle"). The [Elizabeth line](/wiki/Elizabeth_line "Elizabeth line"), a rail link running between [East](/wiki/East_London "East London") and [West London](/wiki/West_London "West London"), was named in honour of [Queen Elizabeth II](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_II "Queen Elizabeth II") in 2016 and opened in 2022.[[323]](#cite_note-344)[[324]](#cite_note-345) Another major infrastructure project is [High Speed 2](/wiki/High_Speed_2 "High Speed 2") (HS2), a high-speed railway under construction since 2019. It will link London with [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham"), with the potential to extend further north and capable of speeds of up to 225 mph.[[325]](#cite_note-346)[[326]](#cite_note-347)

In 2023 there were 4 billion bus journeys in the UK, 1.8 billion of which were in London.[[327]](#cite_note-348) The red [double-decker](/wiki/Double-decker_bus "Double-decker bus") bus has entered popular culture as an internationally recognised icon of London and England.[[328]](#cite_note-349) The [London bus network](/wiki/London_Buses "London Buses") is extensive, with over 6,800 scheduled services every weekday carrying about 6 million passengers on over 700 different routes, making it one of the most extensive bus systems in the world and the largest in Europe.[[329]](#cite_note-350)

During 2024 [British airports](/wiki/List_of_airports_in_the_United_Kingdom_and_the_British_Crown_Dependencies "List of airports in the United Kingdom and the British Crown Dependencies") handled nearly 292.5 million passengers.[[330]](#cite_note-caastats-351) In that period the three largest airports were [London Heathrow Airport](/wiki/London_Heathrow_Airport "London Heathrow Airport") (83.9 million passengers), [Gatwick Airport](/wiki/Gatwick_Airport "Gatwick Airport") (43.2 million passengers) and [Manchester Airport](/wiki/Manchester_Airport "Manchester Airport") (30.8 million passengers).[[330]](#cite_note-caastats-351) London Heathrow Airport, located 15 miles (24 km) west of the capital, is the world's [second-busiest airport by international passenger traffic](/wiki/List_of_busiest_airports_by_passenger_traffic "List of busiest airports by passenger traffic") and has the most international passenger traffic of any airport in the world;[[331]](#cite_note-352) it is the hub for the UK flag carrier [British Airways](/wiki/British_Airways "British Airways"), as well as [Virgin Atlantic](/wiki/Virgin_Atlantic "Virgin Atlantic").[[332]](#cite_note-353) During 2023, 18.3 million passengers travelled internationally by rail and 18.1 million by sea.[[333]](#cite_note-354)

### Energy

Main article: [Energy in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Energy_in_the_United_Kingdom "Energy in the United Kingdom")

Further information: [Energy in England](/wiki/Energy_in_England "Energy in England"), [Energy in Scotland](/wiki/Energy_in_Scotland "Energy in Scotland"), [Energy in Wales](/wiki/Energy_in_Wales "Energy in Wales"), and [Energy in Northern Ireland](/wiki/Energy_in_Northern_Ireland "Energy in Northern Ireland")

In 2021 the UK was the world's [14th-largest consumer and 22nd-largest producer of energy](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_energy_consumption_and_production "List of countries by energy consumption and production").[[334]](#cite_note-United_Kingdom_Energy_Profile-355) It is home to [many large energy companies](/wiki/Big_Six_energy_suppliers "Big Six energy suppliers"), including two of the six major oil and gas companies – [BP](/wiki/BP "BP") and [Shell](/wiki/Shell_plc "Shell plc").[[335]](#cite_note-356)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Ardrossan%27s_fan_club._-_panoramio.jpg/250px-Ardrossan%27s_fan_club._-_panoramio.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ardrossan%27s_fan_club._-_panoramio.jpg)

Wind turbines overlooking [Ardrossan](/wiki/Ardrossan "Ardrossan") in Scotland. The UK is [a major producer of wind energy](/wiki/Wind_power_in_the_United_Kingdom "Wind power in the United Kingdom") in Europe.[[336]](#cite_note-357)

Renewable electricity sources provided 51 per cent of the electricity generated in the UK in 2024. [Wind power](/wiki/Wind_power_in_the_United_Kingdom "Wind power in the United Kingdom") was the largest source of electricity in 2024, generating 30 per cent of the UK's total electricity.[[337]](#cite_note-2024_Energy-358) The UK has the largest offshore wind farm in the world, which is located off the coast of [Yorkshire](/wiki/Yorkshire "Yorkshire").[[338]](#cite_note-359)

In 2023 the UK had nine nuclear reactors generating about 15 per cent of the UK's electricity.[[339]](#cite_note-coal-ref-360) There are two reactors under construction and more planned.[[340]](#cite_note-361)[[341]](#cite_note-362) In the late 1990s [nuclear power plants](/wiki/Nuclear_power_plants_in_the_United_Kingdom "Nuclear power plants in the United Kingdom") contributed around 25 per cent of the total annual electricity generation in the UK, but this has gradually declined as old plants have been shut down. The UK government is investing in [small modular reactors](/wiki/Small_modular_reactors "Small modular reactors") that operate via [nuclear fission](/wiki/Nuclear_fission "Nuclear fission"), as well as in research and development towards commercial [fusion reactors](/wiki/Fusion_reactors "Fusion reactors"). To that end the government entered into a partnership with the US in late 2023 to collaborate on fusion technology, with "a commercial grid-ready fusion reactor by 2040" stated as a goal.[[342]](#cite_note-363)

At the end of 2023 it was estimated that there were 1.1 billion [barrels of oil equivalent](/wiki/Barrels_of_oil_equivalent "Barrels of oil equivalent") of "proven" and "probable" [gas reserves](/wiki/Oil_and_gas_reserves_and_resource_quantification "Oil and gas reserves and resource quantification") and 2.3 billion boe of "proven" and "probable" [oil reserves](/wiki/North_Sea_oil "North Sea oil") offshore, reducing reliance on imports for energy security and transitioning to renewables.[[343]](#cite_note-364) Emissions from UK gas production are roughly four times lower than imported [liquefied natural gas](/wiki/Liquefied_natural_gas "Liquefied natural gas") (LNG), according to the [UK's oil and gas regulator](/wiki/North_Sea_Transition_Authority "North Sea Transition Authority").[[344]](#cite_note-365)

In September 2024 the last coal power station was closed, making [coal](/wiki/Coal "Coal") no longer a power source in the UK.[[337]](#cite_note-2024_Energy-358) The UK currently has no [fracking](/wiki/Fracking "Fracking") (hydraulic fracturing) for [shale gas](/wiki/Shale_gas "Shale gas") despite a large supply, due to environmental concerns.[[345]](#cite_note-366)

### Water supply and sanitation

Main article: [Water supply and sanitation in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Water_supply_and_sanitation_in_the_United_Kingdom "Water supply and sanitation in the United Kingdom")

Access to improved water supply and sanitation in the UK is universal. It is estimated that 96 per cent of households are connected to the sewer network.[[346]](#cite_note-367) According to the [Environment Agency](/wiki/Environment_Agency "Environment Agency"), total water abstraction for public water supply in the UK was 16,406 [megalitres](/wiki/Megalitre "Megalitre") per day in 2007.[[347]](#cite_note-368)

In England and Wales, water and sewerage services are provided by 10 private regional water and sewerage companies and 13 mostly smaller private "water only" companies. In Scotland, said services are provided by a single public company, [Scottish Water](/wiki/Scottish_Water "Scottish Water"). In Northern Ireland, they are also provided by a single public entity, [Northern Ireland Water](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Water "Northern Ireland Water").[[348]](#cite_note-369)

## Demographics

Main articles: [Demographics of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Demographics_of_the_United_Kingdom "Demographics of the United Kingdom") and [British people](/wiki/British_people "British people")

Further information: [Ceremonial counties of England](/wiki/Ceremonial_counties_of_England "Ceremonial counties of England"), [Subdivisions of Scotland](/wiki/Subdivisions_of_Scotland "Subdivisions of Scotland"), [Subdivisions of Wales](/wiki/Subdivisions_of_Wales "Subdivisions of Wales"), and [Counties of Northern Ireland](/wiki/Counties_of_Northern_Ireland "Counties of Northern Ireland")

In the [2021 census](/wiki/2021_United_Kingdom_census "2021 United Kingdom census") the population of the United Kingdom was 67,026,292.[[349]](#cite_note-2011census-370) It is the [fourth-largest](/wiki/List_of_European_countries_by_population "List of European countries by population") in Europe and the [22nd-largest](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_population "List of countries by population") in the world. In 2012 and 2013 births contributed the most to population growth, while in 2014 and 2015 net international migration contributed more.[[350]](#cite_note-pop2015-371) Between 2001 and 2011 the population increased at an average annual rate of 0.7 per cent.[[349]](#cite_note-2011census-370) The [2011 census](/wiki/2011_United_Kingdom_census "2011 United Kingdom census") also showed that, over the previous 100 years, the proportion of the population aged 0–14 fell from 31 to 18 per cent, and the proportion of people aged 65 and over rose from 5 to 16 per cent.[[349]](#cite_note-2011census-370) In 2018 the [median age](/wiki/Median_age "Median age") of the UK population was 41.7 years.[[136]](#cite_note-factbook-154) The 2021 census put Scotland's population at 5.48 million, Wales's at 3.1 million and Northern Ireland's at 1.9 million.[[349]](#cite_note-2011census-370)

England's population in 2021 was 56 million, representing some 84 per cent of the UK total.[[349]](#cite_note-2011census-370) England is one of the [most-densely-populated countries](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_dependencies_by_population_density "List of countries and dependencies by population density") in the world, with 434 people per square kilometre in mid-2021,[[349]](#cite_note-2011census-370) with a particular concentration in London and the south-east.[[352]](#cite_note-373) [London's wider metropolitan area](/wiki/London_metropolitan_area "London metropolitan area") is the largest in [Western Europe](/wiki/Western_Europe "Western Europe"), with a population of 14.9 million in 2024.[[353]](#cite_note-374)

In 2021 the [total fertility rate](/wiki/Total_fertility_rate "Total fertility rate") across the UK was 1.53 children born per woman,[[354]](#cite_note-375) which remains considerably below the [baby boom](/wiki/Mid-20th_century_baby_boom "Mid-20th century baby boom") peak of 2.95 children per woman in 1964,[[355]](#cite_note-376) or the high of 6.02 children born per woman in 1815[[356]](#cite_note-377) and below the replacement rate of 2.1. In 2011, 47.3 per cent of births in the UK were to unmarried women.[[357]](#cite_note-378) The [Office for National Statistics](/wiki/Office_for_National_Statistics "Office for National Statistics") reported in 2015 that amongst the UK population aged 16 and over, 1.7 per cent identify as gay, lesbian, or bisexual (2.0 per cent of males and 1.5 per cent of females); 4.5 per cent of respondents responded with "other", "I don't know", or did not respond.[[358]](#cite_note-379) The number of [transgender](/wiki/Transgender "Transgender") people in the UK was estimated to be between 65,000 and 300,000 by research between 2001 and 2008.[[359]](#cite_note-380)

### Ethnicity

Main article: [Ethnic groups in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_in_the_United_Kingdom "Ethnic groups in the United Kingdom")

Historically, indigenous British people were thought to be [descended from ethnic groups](/wiki/Genetic_history_of_the_British_Isles "Genetic history of the British Isles") that settled there before the 12th century: the [Celts](/wiki/Celts "Celts"), Romans, Anglo-Saxons, Norse and the [Normans](/wiki/Normans "Normans"). [Welsh people](/wiki/Welsh_people "Welsh people") could be the oldest ethnic group in the UK.[[363]](#cite_note-384) The UK has a history of non-white immigration with [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool "Liverpool") having the oldest black population in the country dating from at least the 1730s,[[364]](#cite_note-385) in addition to having the oldest [Chinese](/wiki/British_Chinese "British Chinese") community in Europe dating from the 19th century.[[365]](#cite_note-386)

In 2021, 83 per cent of the UK population identified themselves as white, meaning 17 per cent of the UK population identify themselves as of one of an [ethnic minority](/wiki/Minority_group "Minority group") group.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] Ethnic diversity varies significantly across the UK. Regionally, in 2021, 46.2 per cent of London's population was an ethnic minority, comparatively to Scotland, Wales, Northern Ireland and the South West and North East of England being less than 10 per cent.[[4]](#cite_note-2021_census_-_ethnicity_-_England_and_Wales-7)

### Language

Main article: [Languages of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Languages_of_the_United_Kingdom "Languages of the United Kingdom")

The [English language](/wiki/English_language "English language") is the *[de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto")* official and most widely spoken language in the United Kingdom.[[366]](#cite_note-387)[[367]](#cite_note-388) The UK promotes the language globally to build connections, understanding and trust between people in the UK and countries worldwide.[[368]](#cite_note-389)[[369]](#cite_note-390)

In the UK, the English language is spoken with distinctive characteristics that collectively form what is known as [British English](/wiki/British_English "British English"). The variety of dialects and accents is often noted, with nearby regions frequently having highly distinct variations. [Received Pronunciation](/wiki/Received_Pronunciation "Received Pronunciation") is traditionally associated with educated speakers in southern England.[[370]](#cite_note-391) The main national dialects are [Scottish English](/wiki/Scottish_English "Scottish English"), [Welsh English](/wiki/Welsh_English "Welsh English") and [Northern Irish English](/wiki/Northern_Irish_English "Northern Irish English"). Distinctive regional varieties include
[Brummie](/wiki/Brummie_dialect "Brummie dialect"), [Cockney](/wiki/Cockney "Cockney"), [Geordie](/wiki/Geordie "Geordie"), [Mancunian](/wiki/Manchester_dialect "Manchester dialect"), [Scouse](/wiki/Scouse "Scouse"), [West Country](/wiki/West_Country_English "West Country English"), [Yorkshire](/wiki/Yorkshire_dialect "Yorkshire dialect") and [MLE](/wiki/Multicultural_London_English "Multicultural London English") (Multicultural London English).[[371]](#cite_note-392)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/Bilingual_welcome_sign_Newry.jpg/250px-Bilingual_welcome_sign_Newry.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bilingual_welcome_sign_Newry.jpg)

[Bilingual sign](/wiki/Bilingual_sign "Bilingual sign") ([Irish](/wiki/Irish_language "Irish language") and [English](/wiki/English_language "English language")) in [Newry](/wiki/Newry "Newry"), Northern Ireland

Three indigenous [Celtic languages](/wiki/Celtic_languages "Celtic languages") are spoken in the UK: [Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_language "Welsh language"), [Irish](/wiki/Irish_language "Irish language") and [Scottish Gaelic](/wiki/Scottish_Gaelic "Scottish Gaelic"). [Cornish](/wiki/Cornish_language "Cornish language"), which became extinct as a first language in the late-18th century, is being revived and has a small group of second-language speakers.[[372]](#cite_note-393)[[2]](#cite_note-reglang-3) In the [2021 census](/wiki/2021_United_Kingdom_census "2021 United Kingdom census") the [Welsh-speaking population](/wiki/Welsh-speaking_population "Welsh-speaking population") of Wales aged three or older was 538,300 (17.8 per cent).[[373]](#cite_note-394) In addition it is estimated that about 200,000 Welsh-speakers live in England.[[374]](#cite_note-395) In the 2021 census in Northern Ireland 12.4 per cent of people had some [Irish language](/wiki/Irish_language_in_Northern_Ireland "Irish language in Northern Ireland") ability and 10.4 per cent had some facility in the [Ulster-Scots](/wiki/Ulster_Scots_dialect "Ulster Scots dialect") language.[[375]](#cite_note-396)

In 2001, over 92,000 people in Scotland, just under 2 per cent of the population, had some Gaelic language ability, including 72 per cent of those living in the [Outer Hebrides](/wiki/Outer_Hebrides "Outer Hebrides").[[376]](#cite_note-397) The number of children being taught either Welsh or Scottish Gaelic is increasing.[[377]](#cite_note-398) [Scots](/wiki/Scots_language "Scots language"), a language descended from early northern [Middle English](/wiki/Middle_English "Middle English"), has limited [recognition](/wiki/European_Charter_for_Regional_or_Minority_Languages "European Charter for Regional or Minority Languages") alongside its regional variant, [Ulster Scots](/wiki/Ulster_Scots_dialects "Ulster Scots dialects") in Northern Ireland, without specific commitments to protection and promotion.[[2]](#cite_note-reglang-3)[[378]](#cite_note-399) As of April 2020 there are around 151,000 users of [British Sign Language](/wiki/British_Sign_Language "British Sign Language") (BSL), a [sign language](/wiki/Sign_language "Sign language") used by deaf people, in the UK.[[379]](#cite_note-400)

In 2013, it was estimated that 95 per cent of the UK's population were [monolingual](/wiki/Monolingual "Monolingual") English speakers.[[380]](#cite_note-BBC_languages-401) In 2013, over 5 per cent of the population were estimated to speak languages brought to the UK as a result of immigration.[[380]](#cite_note-BBC_languages-401) [South Asian languages](/wiki/South_Asian_languages "South Asian languages") are the largest group, which includes [Punjabi](/wiki/Punjabi_language "Punjabi language"), [Urdu](/wiki/Urdu "Urdu"), [Bengali](/wiki/Bengali_language "Bengali language"), [Sylheti](/wiki/Sylheti_language "Sylheti language"), [Hindi](/wiki/Hindi "Hindi"), [Pahari-Pothwari](/wiki/Pahari-Pothwari "Pahari-Pothwari"), [Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_language "Tamil language") and [Gujarati](/wiki/Gujarati_language "Gujarati language").[[381]](#cite_note-402) In the 2011 census [Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language") was the second-largest language spoken in England, with 546,000 speakers.[[382]](#cite_note-403) In 2019 some three-quarters of a million people spoke little or no English.[[383]](#cite_note-404)

### Religion

Main articles: [Religion in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Religion_in_the_United_Kingdom "Religion in the United Kingdom") and [Freedom of religion in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Freedom_of_religion_in_the_United_Kingdom "Freedom of religion in the United Kingdom")

Religion in the United Kingdom (2022 census):[[384]](#cite_note-405)

1. Christianity (46.5%)
2. No religion (37.8%)
3. Islam (5.97%)
4. Hinduism (1.59%)
5. Sikhism (0.79%)
6. Buddhism (0.43%)
7. Judaism (0.41%)
8. Other religion (0.58%)
9. Not stated (5.91%)

[Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") has dominated religious life in the United Kingdom for [more than 1,400 years](/wiki/History_of_Christianity_in_Britain "History of Christianity in Britain").[[385]](#cite_note-406) Although a majority of citizens still identify with Christianity in surveys, regular church attendance has fallen dramatically since the middle of the 20th century,[[386]](#cite_note-407) while immigration and demographic change have contributed to the growth of Islam and other faiths.[[387]](#cite_note-408) This has led some commentators to describe the UK as a multi-faith,[[388]](#cite_note-409) [secularised](/wiki/Secularised "Secularised"),[[389]](#cite_note-410) or [post-Christian](/wiki/Post-Christian "Post-Christian") society.[[390]](#cite_note-411)

In the 2021 census 46.5 per cent of respondents reported that they were Christians, with the next largest faiths being [Islam](/wiki/Islam_in_the_United_Kingdom "Islam in the United Kingdom") (5.9 per cent), [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism_in_the_United_Kingdom "Hinduism in the United Kingdom") (1.6 per cent), [Sikhism](/wiki/Sikhism_in_the_United_Kingdom "Sikhism in the United Kingdom") (0.8 per cent), [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism_in_the_United_Kingdom "Buddhism in the United Kingdom") (0.4 per cent), [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism_in_the_United_Kingdom "Judaism in the United Kingdom") (0.4 per cent), and all other religions (0.6 per cent). Of the respondents, 38 per cent stated that they had [no religion](/wiki/Irreligion_in_the_United_Kingdom "Irreligion in the United Kingdom") and a further 6 per cent stated no religious preference.[[7]](#cite_note-2021_census_-_religion_-_England_and_Wales-10) A [Tearfund](/wiki/Tearfund "Tearfund") survey in 2007 showed that one Briton in ten attends church weekly.[[391]](#cite_note-412) Between the 2001 and 2011 censuses there was a 12 per cent decrease in those who identified as Christian, while the percentage reporting no religious affiliation doubled. This contrasted with growth in the other main religious group categories, with the number of [Muslims](/wiki/Muslims "Muslims") increasing the most to about 5 per cent.[[392]](#cite_note-413) The Muslim population has increased from 1.6 million in 2001 to 2.7 million in 2011, making it the second-largest religious group in the UK.[[393]](#cite_note-414)

The [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") is the [established church](/wiki/State_religion "State religion") in England.[[394]](#cite_note-415) It retains [representation](/wiki/Lords_Spiritual "Lords Spiritual") in the UK Parliament, and the British monarch is its [Supreme Governor](/wiki/Supreme_Governor_of_the_Church_of_England "Supreme Governor of the Church of England").[[395]](#cite_note-416) In [Scotland](/wiki/Religion_in_Scotland "Religion in Scotland") the [Church of Scotland](/wiki/Church_of_Scotland "Church of Scotland") is the [national church](/wiki/National_church "National church"). It is not subject to state control, and the British monarch is an ordinary member, required to swear an oath to "maintain and preserve the Protestant Religion and [Presbyterian Church Government](/wiki/Presbyterian_polity "Presbyterian polity")" upon his or her accession.[[396]](#cite_note-417)[[2]](#cite_note-reglang-3)[[397]](#cite_note-418) The [Church in Wales](/wiki/Church_in_Wales "Church in Wales") was disestablished in 1920 and, because the [Church of Ireland](/wiki/Church_of_Ireland "Church of Ireland") was disestablished in 1870 before the [partition of Ireland](/wiki/Partition_of_Ireland "Partition of Ireland"), there is no established church in Northern Ireland.[[398]](#cite_note-419) Although there are no UK-wide data in the 2001 census on adherence to individual Christian denominations, it has been estimated that 62 per cent of Christians are Anglican, 13.5 per cent Catholic, 6 per cent Presbyterian, and 3.4 per cent Methodist, with smaller numbers of other denominations.[[399]](#cite_note-420)

### Migration

Main articles: [Historical immigration to Great Britain](/wiki/Historical_immigration_to_Great_Britain "Historical immigration to Great Britain") and [Modern immigration to the United Kingdom](/wiki/Modern_immigration_to_the_United_Kingdom "Modern immigration to the United Kingdom")

See also: [Foreign-born population of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Foreign-born_population_of_the_United_Kingdom "Foreign-born population of the United Kingdom")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/United_Kingdom_foreign_born_population_by_country_of_birth.png/500px-United_Kingdom_foreign_born_population_by_country_of_birth.png)](/wiki/File:United_Kingdom_foreign_born_population_by_country_of_birth.png)

Estimated foreign-born population by country of birth from April 2007 to March 2008

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/British_expats_countrymap.svg/500px-British_expats_countrymap.svg.png)](/wiki/File:British_expats_countrymap.svg)
