UK

Immigration is contributing to a rising population in the United Kingdom,[[400]](#cite_note-421)[[401]](#cite_note-bbc-23-05-2024-422) with arrivals and UK-born children of migrants accounting for about half of the population increase between 1991 and 2001. According to statistics released in 2015, 27 per cent of UK live births in 2014 were to mothers born outside the UK.[[402]](#cite_note-423)

In 2013 approximately 208,000 foreign nationals were naturalised as British citizens, the highest number since 1962. This figure fell to around 125,800 in 2014. Between 2009 and 2013 the average number of British citizenships granted annually was 195,800. The most common origins of those naturalised in 2024 were Pakistani, Indian, [Nigerian](/wiki/Nigeria "Nigeria"), [Filipino](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"), [Bangladeshi](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh"), Italian, Turkish, Romanian and Iranian. The number of grants of settlement, which confer [permanent residence](/wiki/Permanent_residency "Permanent residency") in the UK but not [citizenship](/wiki/British_citizenship "British citizenship"),[[403]](#cite_note-424) was approximately 154,700 in 2013, higher than the previous two years.[[404]](#cite_note-Blinder2015-425) Long-term net migration (the number of people immigrating minus the number emigrating) reached a record of 860,000 in 2023, with immigration at 1.326 million and emigration at 466,000. In comparison, in 2024 net migration was estimated to be 431,000 with immigration at 948,000 and emigration at 517,000.[[405]](#cite_note-426)

[Emigration](/wiki/British_diaspora "British diaspora") was an important feature of British society in the 19th century. Between 1815 and 1930, around 11.4 million people emigrated from Britain and 7.3 million from Ireland.[[406]](#cite_note-427)[[407]](#cite_note-richards-2018-428) Estimates show that by the end of the 20th century, some 300 million people of British and Irish descent were permanently settled around the globe.[[407]](#cite_note-richards-2018-428) In 2006 at least 5.5 million UK-born people lived abroad,[[408]](#cite_note-BritsAbroad-429)[[409]](#cite_note-430) mainly in Australia, Spain, the United States and Canada.[[408]](#cite_note-BritsAbroad-429)[[410]](#cite_note-431)

### Education

Main articles: [Education in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Education_in_the_United_Kingdom "Education in the United Kingdom") and [Universities in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Universities_in_the_United_Kingdom "Universities in the United Kingdom")

Further information: [Education in England](/wiki/Education_in_England "Education in England"), [Education in Scotland](/wiki/Education_in_Scotland "Education in Scotland"), [Education in Wales](/wiki/Education_in_Wales "Education in Wales"), and [Education in Northern Ireland](/wiki/Education_in_Northern_Ireland "Education in Northern Ireland")

Education in the United Kingdom is a [devolved](/wiki/Devolution "Devolution") matter, with each country having a separate education system. About 38 per cent of the United Kingdom population has a university or [college degree](/wiki/College_degree "College degree"), the highest percentage in Europe and one of the highest in the world.[[411]](#cite_note-432) The UK is home to [many universities](/wiki/List_of_universities_in_the_United_Kingdom "List of universities in the United Kingdom"), including the [University of Oxford](/wiki/University_of_Oxford "University of Oxford") and [University of Cambridge](/wiki/University_of_Cambridge "University of Cambridge"), which often achieve first place on global rankings.[[412]](#cite_note-433)[[413]](#cite_note-434)

University education has varied [tuition fees](/wiki/Student_loans_and_grants_in_the_United_Kingdom "Student loans and grants in the United Kingdom") in different regions of the UK. England and Wales have a fixed maximum annual fee for all UK citizens, contingent on attaining a certain level of income. Only those who reach a certain salary threshold pay this fee through [general taxation](/wiki/Taxation_in_the_United_Kingdom "Taxation in the United Kingdom").[[414]](#cite_note-435) Northern Ireland and Scotland have a reduced maximum fee or no fee for citizens where it is their home region. Some NHS courses have bursaries which pay the fee and in 2017 it was stated that each doctor gets subsidised by £230,000 during their training.[[415]](#cite_note-436)[[416]](#cite_note-437)

In 2022 the [Programme for International Student Assessment](/wiki/Programme_for_International_Student_Assessment "Programme for International Student Assessment") (PISA), coordinated by the [OECD](/wiki/OECD "OECD"), ranked the overall knowledge and skills of British 15-year-olds as 14th in the world in reading, mathematics and science. The average British student scored 494, above the OECD average of 478.[[417]](#cite_note-438)

### Health

Main articles: [Health in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Health_in_the_United_Kingdom "Health in the United Kingdom") and [Healthcare in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Healthcare_in_the_United_Kingdom "Healthcare in the United Kingdom")

Further information: [Healthcare in England](/wiki/Healthcare_in_England "Healthcare in England"), [Healthcare in Scotland](/wiki/Healthcare_in_Scotland "Healthcare in Scotland"), [Healthcare in Wales](/wiki/Healthcare_in_Wales "Healthcare in Wales"), and [Healthcare in Northern Ireland](/wiki/Healthcare_in_Northern_Ireland "Healthcare in Northern Ireland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/QEUH.jpg/250px-QEUH.jpg)](/wiki/File:QEUH.jpg)

[NHS Scotland](/wiki/NHS_Scotland "NHS Scotland")'s [Queen Elizabeth University Hospital](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_University_Hospital "Queen Elizabeth University Hospital") in Glasgow, the largest hospital campus in Europe

The modern system of [universal](/wiki/Universal_service "Universal service") [publicly funded healthcare](/wiki/Publicly_funded_health_care "Publicly funded health care") in the United Kingdom has its origins in the creation of the [National Health Service](/wiki/National_Health_Service "National Health Service") (NHS) in 1949 is the primary healthcare provider in the United Kingdom. The widespread popularity of the NHS has led to it being described as a "national religion".[[418]](#cite_note-439)[[419]](#cite_note-440) Healthcare in the United Kingdom is a [devolved matter](/wiki/Devolution "Devolution") and each country has its own system of universal publicly funded healthcare ([NHS England](/wiki/NHS_England "NHS England"), [NHS Scotland](/wiki/NHS_Scotland "NHS Scotland"), [Health and Social Care (Northern Ireland)](/wiki/Health_and_Social_Care_(Northern_Ireland) "Health and Social Care (Northern Ireland)") and [NHS Wales](/wiki/NHS_Wales "NHS Wales")), although private healthcare is also available. Public healthcare is provided to all [UK permanent residents](/wiki/British_nationality_law "British nationality law") and is mostly free at the point of need, being paid for from [general taxation](/wiki/Taxation_in_the_United_Kingdom "Taxation in the United Kingdom"). The [World Health Organization](/wiki/World_Health_Organization "World Health Organization"), in 2000, ranked the provision of healthcare in the United Kingdom as fifteenth-best in Europe and eighteenth in the world.[[420]](#cite_note-441)

Since 1979 expenditure on healthcare has increased significantly.[[421]](#cite_note-442) The 2018 [OECD](/wiki/OECD "OECD") data, which incorporates in health a chunk of what in the UK is classified as social care, has the UK spending £3,121 per person.[[422]](#cite_note-443) In 2017 the UK spent £2,989 per person on healthcare, near the median for members of the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development.[[423]](#cite_note-444)

Regulatory bodies are organised on a UK-wide basis such as the [General Medical Council](/wiki/General_Medical_Council "General Medical Council"), the [Nursing and Midwifery Council](/wiki/Nursing_and_Midwifery_Council "Nursing and Midwifery Council") and non-governmental-based, such as the [Royal Colleges](/wiki/Medical_royal_college "Medical royal college"). Political and operational responsibility for healthcare lies with four national [governments](/wiki/Governments "Governments"); healthcare in England is the responsibility of the UK Government; healthcare in Northern Ireland is the responsibility of the Northern Ireland Executive; healthcare in Scotland is the responsibility of the Scottish Government; and healthcare in Wales is the responsibility of the Welsh Government. Each National Health Service has different policies and priorities.[[424]](#cite_note-445)

## Culture

Main article: [Culture of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Culture_of_the_United_Kingdom "Culture of the United Kingdom")

The culture of the United Kingdom is influenced by the nation's island status, its history, and being a [political union](/wiki/Political_union "Political union") of four countries with each preserving distinctive traditions, customs and symbolism. British influence can be observed in the language, culture and [legal systems](/wiki/Common_law "Common law") of many of [its former colonies](/wiki/List_of_countries_that_have_gained_independence_from_the_United_Kingdom "List of countries that have gained independence from the United Kingdom"), in particular the United States, Australia, Canada, New Zealand and Ireland, a common culture known as the Anglosphere.[[425]](#cite_note-446)[[426]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFerguson2004307-447) Most of its former colonies are Commonwealth countries, bonded by a shared British inheritance.[[427]](#cite_note-448)[[428]](#cite_note-449)[[429]](#cite_note-450) A global survey in 2024 ranked the UK third in the 'Most Influential Countries' rankings, behind the US and China.[[430]](#cite_note-451)

### Literature and drama

Main articles: [British literature](/wiki/British_literature "British literature") and [Theatre of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Theatre_of_the_United_Kingdom "Theatre of the United Kingdom")

Further information: [Literature of England](/wiki/Literature_of_England "Literature of England"), [Scottish literature](/wiki/Scottish_literature "Scottish literature"), [Welsh literature](/wiki/Welsh_literature "Welsh literature"), and [Literature of Northern Ireland](/wiki/Literature_of_Northern_Ireland "Literature of Northern Ireland")

[![Robert Burns](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/PG_1063Burns_Naysmith.jpg/250px-PG_1063Burns_Naysmith.jpg)](/wiki/File:PG_1063Burns_Naysmith.jpg)

[Robert Burns](/wiki/Robert_Burns "Robert Burns") (1759–1796)

[![William Shakespeare](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/William_Shakespeare_by_John_Taylor%2C_edited.jpg/250px-William_Shakespeare_by_John_Taylor%2C_edited.jpg)](/wiki/File:William_Shakespeare_by_John_Taylor,_edited.jpg)

[William Shakespeare](/wiki/William_Shakespeare "William Shakespeare") (1564–1616)

Burns and Shakespeare are considered the [national poets](/wiki/National_poet "National poet") of Scotland and England respectively.

British literature includes that associated with the United Kingdom, the [Isle of Man](/wiki/Isle_of_Man "Isle of Man") and the [Channel Islands](/wiki/Channel_Islands "Channel Islands"). Most British literature is in English. In 2022, 669 million physical books were sold in the UK, the most ever.[[431]](#cite_note-452) Britain is renowned for [children's literature](/wiki/Literature_for_children "Literature for children"); writers include [Daniel Defoe](/wiki/Daniel_Defoe "Daniel Defoe"), [Rudyard Kipling](/wiki/Rudyard_Kipling "Rudyard Kipling"), [Lewis Carroll](/wiki/Lewis_Carroll "Lewis Carroll") and [Beatrix Potter](/wiki/Beatrix_Potter "Beatrix Potter"), who also illustrated her own books. Other writers include [A. A. Milne](/wiki/A._A._Milne "A. A. Milne"), [Enid Blyton](/wiki/Enid_Blyton "Enid Blyton"), [J. R. R. Tolkien](/wiki/J._R._R._Tolkien "J. R. R. Tolkien"), [Roald Dahl](/wiki/Roald_Dahl "Roald Dahl"), [Terry Pratchett](/wiki/Terry_Pratchett "Terry Pratchett") and [J. K. Rowling](/wiki/J._K._Rowling "J. K. Rowling"), who wrote *[Harry Potter](/wiki/Harry_Potter "Harry Potter")*, the [best-selling book series](/wiki/List_of_best-selling_book_series "List of best-selling book series") of all time.[[432]](#cite_note-453)

The playwright and poet [William Shakespeare](/wiki/William_Shakespeare "William Shakespeare") is generally regarded as the greatest dramatist ever and the [national poet](/wiki/National_poet "National poet") of England.[[433]](#cite_note-454) Theatre of United Kingdom plays an important part in British culture and has had a vibrant tradition of [theatre](/wiki/Theatre "Theatre") since the Renaissance with roots going back to the Roman occupation. Other important figures in the literature of England are [Geoffrey Chaucer](/wiki/Geoffrey_Chaucer "Geoffrey Chaucer"), known for *[The Canterbury Tales](/wiki/The_Canterbury_Tales "The Canterbury Tales")*, the poet [William Wordsworth](/wiki/William_Wordsworth "William Wordsworth"), and other [Romantic poets](/wiki/Romantic_Poetry "Romantic Poetry"), also the novelists [Charles Dickens](/wiki/Charles_Dickens "Charles Dickens"), [H. G. Wells](/wiki/H._G._Wells "H. G. Wells"), [George Orwell](/wiki/George_Orwell "George Orwell"), [Aldous Huxley](/wiki/Aldous_Huxley "Aldous Huxley") and [Ian Fleming](/wiki/Ian_Fleming "Ian Fleming"). The 20th-century English crime writer [Agatha Christie](/wiki/Agatha_Christie "Agatha Christie") is the [best-selling novelist](/wiki/List_of_best-selling_fiction_authors "List of best-selling fiction authors") in history.[[434]](#cite_note-455) Twelve of the top 25 of 100 novels by British writers chosen by a BBC poll of global critics were written by women; these included works by [George Eliot](/wiki/George_Eliot "George Eliot"), [Virginia Woolf](/wiki/Virginia_Woolf "Virginia Woolf"), [Charlotte Brontë](/wiki/Charlotte_Bront%C3%AB "Charlotte Brontë"), [Emily Brontë](/wiki/Emily_Bront%C3%AB "Emily Brontë"), [Mary Shelley](/wiki/Mary_Shelley "Mary Shelley"), [Jane Austen](/wiki/Jane_Austen "Jane Austen"), [Doris Lessing](/wiki/Doris_Lessing "Doris Lessing") and [Zadie Smith](/wiki/Zadie_Smith "Zadie Smith").[[435]](#cite_note-456)

Scottish literature includes [Arthur Conan Doyle](/wiki/Arthur_Conan_Doyle "Arthur Conan Doyle") (the creator of [Sherlock Holmes](/wiki/Sherlock_Holmes "Sherlock Holmes")), [Sir Walter Scott](/wiki/Walter_Scott "Walter Scott"), [J. M. Barrie](/wiki/J._M._Barrie "J. M. Barrie"), [Robert Louis Stevenson](/wiki/Robert_Louis_Stevenson "Robert Louis Stevenson") (whose novel *[Treasure Island](/wiki/Treasure_Island "Treasure Island")* strongly influenced the portrayal of [pirates in the arts and popular culture](/wiki/Pirates_in_the_arts_and_popular_culture "Pirates in the arts and popular culture")), and the poet [Robert Burns](/wiki/Robert_Burns "Robert Burns"), who is considered the national poet of Scotland. More recently [Hugh MacDiarmid](/wiki/Hugh_MacDiarmid "Hugh MacDiarmid") and [Neil M. Gunn](/wiki/Neil_M._Gunn "Neil M. Gunn") contributed to the [Scottish Renaissance](/wiki/Scottish_Renaissance "Scottish Renaissance"), with grimmer works from [Ian Rankin](/wiki/Ian_Rankin "Ian Rankin") and [Iain Banks](/wiki/Iain_Banks "Iain Banks"). Edinburgh was UNESCO's first worldwide [City of Literature](/wiki/City_of_Literature "City of Literature").[[436]](#cite_note-457)

Welsh literature includes Britain's oldest known poem, *[Y Gododdin](/wiki/Y_Gododdin "Y Gododdin")*, most likely composed in the late 6th century. It was written in [Cumbric](/wiki/Cumbric_language "Cumbric language") or [Old Welsh](/wiki/Old_Welsh "Old Welsh") and contains the earliest known reference to [King Arthur](/wiki/King_Arthur "King Arthur").[[437]](#cite_note-458) The [Arthurian legend](/wiki/Arthurian_legend "Arthurian legend") was further developed by [Geoffrey of Monmouth](/wiki/Geoffrey_of_Monmouth "Geoffrey of Monmouth").[[438]](#cite_note-459) [Dafydd ap Gwilym](/wiki/Dafydd_ap_Gwilym "Dafydd ap Gwilym") (*fl.* 1320–1370) is widely regarded as one of the greatest European poets of his age.[[439]](#cite_note-460) [Daniel Owen](/wiki/Daniel_Owen "Daniel Owen") is credited as the first Welsh-language novelist, publishing *[Rhys Lewis](/wiki/Rhys_Lewis_(novel) "Rhys Lewis (novel)")* in 1885. The best-known of the [Anglo-Welsh poets](/wiki/Anglo-Welsh_poetry "Anglo-Welsh poetry") are [Dylan Thomas](/wiki/Dylan_Thomas "Dylan Thomas") and [R. S. Thomas](/wiki/R._S._Thomas "R. S. Thomas"), the latter nominated for the [Nobel Prize in Literature](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Literature "Nobel Prize in Literature") in 1996. Leading Welsh novelists of the twentieth century include [Richard Llewellyn](/wiki/Richard_Llewellyn "Richard Llewellyn") and [Kate Roberts](/wiki/Kate_Roberts_(author) "Kate Roberts (author)").[[440]](#cite_note-461)[[441]](#cite_note-462)

Northern Ireland's most popular writer is [C. S. Lewis](/wiki/C._S._Lewis "C. S. Lewis"), who was born in Belfast and wrote *[The Chronicles of Narnia](/wiki/The_Chronicles_of_Narnia "The Chronicles of Narnia")*.[[442]](#cite_note-463) Irish writers, living at a time when all of Ireland was part of the United Kingdom, include [Oscar Wilde](/wiki/Oscar_Wilde "Oscar Wilde"),[[443]](#cite_note-464) [Bram Stoker](/wiki/Bram_Stoker "Bram Stoker") (who wrote *[Dracula](/wiki/Dracula "Dracula")*)[[444]](#cite_note-465) and [George Bernard Shaw](/wiki/George_Bernard_Shaw "George Bernard Shaw").[[445]](#cite_note-466) There have been many authors whose origins were from outside the United Kingdom but who moved to the UK, including [Joseph Conrad](/wiki/Joseph_Conrad "Joseph Conrad"),[[446]](#cite_note-467) [T. S. Eliot](/wiki/T._S._Eliot "T. S. Eliot"),[[447]](#cite_note-468) [Kazuo Ishiguro](/wiki/Kazuo_Ishiguro "Kazuo Ishiguro"),[[448]](#cite_note-469) Sir [Salman Rushdie](/wiki/Salman_Rushdie "Salman Rushdie")[[449]](#cite_note-470) and [Ezra Pound](/wiki/Ezra_Pound "Ezra Pound").[[450]](#cite_note-471)

### Philosophy

Main article: [British philosophy](/wiki/British_philosophy "British philosophy")

The United Kingdom is famous for "[British Empiricism](/wiki/British_Empiricism "British Empiricism")", a branch of the philosophy that states that only knowledge verified by experience is valid, and 'Scottish Philosophy', sometimes referred to as the '[Scottish School of Common Sense](/wiki/Scottish_School_of_Common_Sense "Scottish School of Common Sense")'.[[451]](#cite_note-472) The most famous philosophers of British Empiricism are [John Locke](/wiki/John_Locke "John Locke"), [George Berkeley](/wiki/George_Berkeley "George Berkeley")[[v]](#cite_note-473) and [David Hume](/wiki/David_Hume "David Hume"); while [Dugald Stewart](/wiki/Dugald_Stewart "Dugald Stewart"), [Thomas Reid](/wiki/Thomas_Reid "Thomas Reid") and [William Hamilton](/wiki/Sir_William_Hamilton,_9th_Baronet "Sir William Hamilton, 9th Baronet") were major exponents of the Scottish "common sense" school. Two Britons are also notable for the ethical theory of [utilitarianism](/wiki/Utilitarianism "Utilitarianism"), a moral philosophy first used by [Jeremy Bentham](/wiki/Jeremy_Bentham "Jeremy Bentham") and later by [John Stuart Mill](/wiki/John_Stuart_Mill "John Stuart Mill") in his short work *[Utilitarianism](/wiki/Utilitarianism_(book) "Utilitarianism (book)")*.[[452]](#cite_note-474)

### Media

Main articles: [Media of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Media_of_the_United_Kingdom "Media of the United Kingdom") and [Censorship in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Censorship_in_the_United_Kingdom "Censorship in the United Kingdom")

Further information: [Media of England](/wiki/Media_of_England "Media of England"), [Media of Scotland](/wiki/Media_of_Scotland "Media of Scotland"), [Media of Wales](/wiki/Media_of_Wales "Media of Wales"), and [Media of Northern Ireland](/wiki/Media_of_Northern_Ireland "Media of Northern Ireland")

The [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC"), founded in 1922, is the United Kingdom's publicly funded radio, television and Internet broadcasting corporation, and is the oldest and largest broadcaster in the world.[[453]](#cite_note-MediaNewsline-475)[[454]](#cite_note-ProspectMag-476)[[455]](#cite_note-AboutBBC-477) It operates television and radio stations across the UK and abroad and its domestic services are funded by the [television licence](/wiki/Television_licensing_in_the_United_Kingdom "Television licensing in the United Kingdom").[[456]](#cite_note-478) The [BBC World Service](/wiki/BBC_World_Service "BBC World Service") is an [international broadcaster](/wiki/International_broadcasting "International broadcasting") owned and operated by the BBC, and the world's largest.[[457]](#cite_note-479) It broadcasts radio news, speech and discussions in more than 40 languages.[[458]](#cite_note-480)

Other major players in the UK media include [ITV](/wiki/ITV_plc "ITV plc"), which operates 11 of the 15 regional television broadcasters that make up the [ITV Network](/wiki/ITV_(TV_network) "ITV (TV network)"),[[459]](#cite_note-481) and [Sky](/wiki/Sky_UK "Sky UK").[[460]](#cite_note-482) Newspapers produced in the United Kingdom include the *[Daily Mail](/wiki/Daily_Mail "Daily Mail")*, *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")*, *[The Telegraph](/wiki/The_Daily_Telegraph "The Daily Telegraph")*, *[The Times](/wiki/The_Times "The Times")*, and the *[Financial Times](/wiki/Financial_Times "Financial Times")*.[[461]](#cite_note-483) Magazines and journals published in the United Kingdom that have achieved worldwide circulation include *[The Spectator](/wiki/The_Spectator "The Spectator")*, *[The Economist](/wiki/The_Economist "The Economist")*, *[New Statesman](/wiki/New_Statesman "New Statesman")* and *[Radio Times](/wiki/Radio_Times "Radio Times")*.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/Media_City_Footbridge_and_BBC_Offices_%28geograph_2685261%29.jpg/250px-Media_City_Footbridge_and_BBC_Offices_%28geograph_2685261%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Media_City_Footbridge_and_BBC_Offices_(geograph_2685261).jpg)

[MediaCityUK](/wiki/MediaCityUK "MediaCityUK") in [Salford](/wiki/Salford "Salford"), [Greater Manchester](/wiki/Greater_Manchester "Greater Manchester"), is one of the largest media production facilities in Europe.

London dominates the media sector in the UK: national newspapers and television and radio are largely based there, although [MediaCityUK](/wiki/MediaCityUK "MediaCityUK") in Manchester is also a significant national media centre. Edinburgh and Glasgow, and Cardiff, are important centres of newspaper and broadcasting production in Scotland and Wales, respectively.[[462]](#cite_note-484) The UK publishing sector, including books, directories and databases, journals, magazines and business media, newspapers and news agencies, has a combined turnover of around £20 billion and employs 167,000 people.[[463]](#cite_note-485) In 2015 the UK published 2,710 book titles per million inhabitants, more than any other country, with much of this exported to other [Anglophone](/wiki/Anglophone "Anglophone") countries.[[464]](#cite_note-486)

In 2010, 82.5 per cent of the UK population were Internet users, the [highest proportion](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_number_of_Internet_users "List of countries by number of Internet users") amongst the 20 countries with the largest total number of users in that year.[[465]](#cite_note-487) The [British video game industry](/wiki/Video_games_in_the_United_Kingdom "Video games in the United Kingdom") is the largest in Europe, and since 2022 the UK has the [largest video game market](/wiki/List_of_video_games_markets_by_country "List of video games markets by country") in Europe by sales, surpassing [Germany](/wiki/Video_games_in_Germany "Video games in Germany").[[466]](#cite_note-488) It is the world's third-largest producer of video games after [Japan](/wiki/Video_games_in_Japan "Video games in Japan") and the [United States](/wiki/Video_games_in_the_United_States "Video games in the United States").[[467]](#cite_note-489)

### Visual art

Main articles: [Art of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Art_of_the_United_Kingdom "Art of the United Kingdom") and [Architecture of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Architecture_of_the_United_Kingdom "Architecture of the United Kingdom")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/Angel_of_the_North_-_6150534524.jpg/250px-Angel_of_the_North_-_6150534524.jpg)](/wiki/File:Angel_of_the_North_-_6150534524.jpg)

The [Angel of the North](/wiki/Angel_of_the_North "Angel of the North") sculpture by [Antony Gormley](/wiki/Antony_Gormley "Antony Gormley") has become a symbol of [Northern England](/wiki/Northern_England "Northern England").

Major British artists include the [Romantic](/wiki/Romanticism "Romanticism") artists [William Blake](/wiki/William_Blake "William Blake"), [John Constable](/wiki/John_Constable "John Constable"), [Samuel Palmer](/wiki/Samuel_Palmer "Samuel Palmer"), and [J. M. W. Turner](/wiki/J._M._W._Turner "J. M. W. Turner"); the [portrait](/wiki/Portrait "Portrait") painters [Sir Joshua Reynolds](/wiki/Joshua_Reynolds "Joshua Reynolds") and [Lucian Freud](/wiki/Lucian_Freud "Lucian Freud"); the landscape artists [Thomas Gainsborough](/wiki/Thomas_Gainsborough "Thomas Gainsborough") and [L. S. Lowry](/wiki/L._S._Lowry "L. S. Lowry"); the pioneer of the [Arts and Crafts movement](/wiki/Arts_and_Crafts_movement "Arts and Crafts movement") [William Morris](/wiki/William_Morris "William Morris"); the figurative painter [Francis Bacon](/wiki/Francis_Bacon_(artist) "Francis Bacon (artist)"); the [Pop artists](/wiki/Pop_artist "Pop artist") [Peter Blake](/wiki/Peter_Blake_(artist) "Peter Blake (artist)"), [Richard Hamilton](/wiki/Richard_Hamilton_(artist) "Richard Hamilton (artist)") and [David Hockney](/wiki/David_Hockney "David Hockney"); the pioneers of [conceptual art](/wiki/Conceptual_art "Conceptual art") movement [Art & Language](/wiki/Art_%26_Language "Art & Language");[[468]](#cite_note-490) the collaborative duo [Gilbert and George](/wiki/Gilbert_and_George "Gilbert and George"); the [abstract](/wiki/Abstract_art "Abstract art") artist [Howard Hodgkin](/wiki/Howard_Hodgkin "Howard Hodgkin"); and the sculptors [Antony Gormley](/wiki/Antony_Gormley "Antony Gormley"), [Anish Kapoor](/wiki/Anish_Kapoor "Anish Kapoor") and [Henry Moore](/wiki/Henry_Moore "Henry Moore"). During the late 1980s and 1990s the [Saatchi Gallery](/wiki/Saatchi_Gallery "Saatchi Gallery") in London helped to bring to public attention a group of multi-genre artists who would become known as the "[Young British Artists](/wiki/Young_British_Artists "Young British Artists")": [Damien Hirst](/wiki/Damien_Hirst "Damien Hirst"), [Chris Ofili](/wiki/Chris_Ofili "Chris Ofili"), [Rachel Whiteread](/wiki/Rachel_Whiteread "Rachel Whiteread"), [Tracey Emin](/wiki/Tracey_Emin "Tracey Emin"), [Mark Wallinger](/wiki/Mark_Wallinger "Mark Wallinger"), [Steve McQueen](/wiki/Steve_McQueen_(director) "Steve McQueen (director)"), [Sam Taylor-Wood](/wiki/Sam_Taylor-Wood "Sam Taylor-Wood") and the [Chapman Brothers](/wiki/Jake_and_Dinos_Chapman "Jake and Dinos Chapman") are amongst the better-known members of this loosely affiliated movement.

The [Royal Academy](/wiki/Royal_Academy "Royal Academy") in London is a key organisation for the promotion of the visual arts in the United Kingdom. Major schools of art in the UK include: the six-school [University of the Arts London](/wiki/University_of_the_Arts_London "University of the Arts London"), which includes the [Central Saint Martins College of Art and Design](/wiki/Central_Saint_Martins_College_of_Art_and_Design "Central Saint Martins College of Art and Design") and [Chelsea College of Art and Design](/wiki/Chelsea_College_of_Art_and_Design "Chelsea College of Art and Design"); [Goldsmiths, University of London](/wiki/Goldsmiths,_University_of_London "Goldsmiths, University of London"); the [Slade School of Fine Art](/wiki/Slade_School_of_Fine_Art "Slade School of Fine Art") (part of [University College London](/wiki/University_College_London "University College London")); the [Glasgow School of Art](/wiki/Glasgow_School_of_Art "Glasgow School of Art"); the [Royal College of Art](/wiki/Royal_College_of_Art "Royal College of Art"); and [The Ruskin School of Drawing and Fine Art](/wiki/The_Ruskin_School_of_Drawing_and_Fine_Art "The Ruskin School of Drawing and Fine Art") (part of the University of Oxford). The [Courtauld Institute of Art](/wiki/Courtauld_Institute_of_Art "Courtauld Institute of Art") is a leading centre for the teaching of the [history of art](/wiki/History_of_art "History of art"). Important art galleries in the United Kingdom include the [National Gallery](/wiki/National_Gallery "National Gallery"), [National Portrait Gallery](/wiki/National_Portrait_Gallery_(London) "National Portrait Gallery (London)"), [Tate Britain](/wiki/Tate_Britain "Tate Britain"), and [Tate Modern](/wiki/Tate_Modern "Tate Modern") (the most-visited modern art gallery in the world, with around 4.7 million visitors per year).[[469]](#cite_note-491)

### Music

Main article: [Music of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Music_of_the_United_Kingdom "Music of the United Kingdom")

Further information: [Music of England](/wiki/Music_of_England_(disambiguation) "Music of England (disambiguation)"), [Music of Scotland](/wiki/Music_of_Scotland "Music of Scotland"), [Music of Wales](/wiki/Music_of_Wales "Music of Wales"), and [Music of Northern Ireland](/wiki/Music_of_Northern_Ireland "Music of Northern Ireland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Royal_Albert_Hall%2C_BBC_Proms_2017.jpg/250px-Royal_Albert_Hall%2C_BBC_Proms_2017.jpg)](/wiki/File:Royal_Albert_Hall,_BBC_Proms_2017.jpg)

[The Proms](/wiki/The_Proms "The Proms") is a classical music festival, most closely associated with [Henry Wood](/wiki/Henry_Wood "Henry Wood") and held at the [Royal Albert Hall](/wiki/Royal_Albert_Hall "Royal Albert Hall") in London, which regularly plays music by [Edward Elgar](/wiki/Edward_Elgar "Edward Elgar") and other British composers.

Various styles of music have become popular in the UK, including the [folk music](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music") of [England](/wiki/Folk_music_of_England "Folk music of England"), [Scotland](/wiki/Folk_music_of_Scotland "Folk music of Scotland"), [Wales](/wiki/Music_of_Wales#Folk_music "Music of Wales") and [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Folk_music_of_Northern_Ireland "Folk music of Northern Ireland"). English folk features melodic ballads with strong lyrics and music for country dancing often using accordion and fiddles.[[470]](#cite_note-492) Scottish folk features bagpipes and fiddles playing traditional dance tunes with bouncy tempos.[[471]](#cite_note-493) Welsh folk has harps and vocal harmonies often sung in Welsh.[[472]](#cite_note-494) Northern Irish folk blends fiddles with flutes merging Scottish and Irish influences.[[473]](#cite_note-495)

Historically, there has been [Renaissance music](/wiki/Renaissance_music "Renaissance music") from the [Tudor period](/wiki/Tudor_period "Tudor period"), with [masses](/wiki/Mass_(music) "Mass (music)"), [madrigals](/wiki/Madrigal "Madrigal") and [lute](/wiki/Lute "Lute") music by [Thomas Tallis](/wiki/Thomas_Tallis "Thomas Tallis"), [John Taverner](/wiki/John_Taverner "John Taverner"), [William Byrd](/wiki/William_Byrd "William Byrd"), [Orlando Gibbons](/wiki/Orlando_Gibbons "Orlando Gibbons") and [John Dowland](/wiki/John_Dowland "John Dowland"). After the [Stuart Restoration](/wiki/Stuart_Restoration "Stuart Restoration"), an English tradition of dramatic [masques](/wiki/Masque "Masque"), anthems and airs became established, led by [Henry Purcell](/wiki/Henry_Purcell "Henry Purcell"), followed by [Thomas Arne](/wiki/Thomas_Arne "Thomas Arne") and others. [George Frideric Handel](/wiki/George_Frideric_Handel "George Frideric Handel") composed the anthem *[Zadok the Priest](/wiki/Zadok_the_Priest "Zadok the Priest")* for the [coronation of George II](/wiki/Coronation_of_George_II "Coronation of George II"); it became the traditional ceremonial music for anointing all future monarchs. Handel's many oratorios, such as his famous *[Messiah](/wiki/Messiah_(Handel) "Messiah (Handel)")*, were written in English.[[474]](#cite_note-496)

In the second half of the 19th century, [Arthur Sullivan](/wiki/Arthur_Sullivan "Arthur Sullivan") and his librettist [W. S. Gilbert](/wiki/W._S._Gilbert "W. S. Gilbert") wrote their popular [Savoy operas](/wiki/Savoy_opera "Savoy opera"), and [Edward Elgar](/wiki/Edward_Elgar "Edward Elgar") composed a wide range of music. Increasingly, composers became inspired by the English countryside and its [folk music](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music"), notably [Gustav Holst](/wiki/Gustav_Holst "Gustav Holst"), [Ralph Vaughan Williams](/wiki/Ralph_Vaughan_Williams "Ralph Vaughan Williams") and [Benjamin Britten](/wiki/Benjamin_Britten "Benjamin Britten"), a pioneer of modern British opera. Amongst the many post-war composers, some of the most notable have made their own personal choice of musical identity: [Peter Maxwell Davies](/wiki/Peter_Maxwell_Davies "Peter Maxwell Davies") (Orkney), [Harrison Birtwistle](/wiki/Harrison_Birtwistle "Harrison Birtwistle") (mythological), and [John Tavener](/wiki/John_Tavener "John Tavener") (religious).[[475]](#cite_note-497) Recent classical singers include [Alfie Boe](/wiki/Alfie_Boe "Alfie Boe"), [Bryn Terfel](/wiki/Bryn_Terfel "Bryn Terfel"), [Katherine Jenkins](/wiki/Katherine_Jenkins "Katherine Jenkins"), [Michael Ball](/wiki/Michael_Ball "Michael Ball"), [Roderick Williams](/wiki/Roderick_Williams "Roderick Williams"), [Russell Watson](/wiki/Russell_Watson "Russell Watson") and [Sarah Brightman](/wiki/Sarah_Brightman "Sarah Brightman"), while [Nicola Benedetti](/wiki/Nicola_Benedetti "Nicola Benedetti") and [Nigel Kennedy](/wiki/Nigel_Kennedy "Nigel Kennedy") are renowned for their violin ability.[[476]](#cite_note-498)

According to *[The New Grove Dictionary of Music and Musicians](/wiki/The_New_Grove_Dictionary_of_Music_and_Musicians "The New Grove Dictionary of Music and Musicians")* the term "[pop music](/wiki/Pop_music "Pop music")" originated in Britain in the mid-1950s to describe [rock and roll](/wiki/Rock_and_roll "Rock and roll")'s fusion with the "new youth music".[[477]](#cite_note-499) *The Oxford Dictionary of Music* states that artists such as [the Beatles](/wiki/The_Beatles "The Beatles") and [the Rolling Stones](/wiki/The_Rolling_Stones "The Rolling Stones") drove pop music to the forefront of popular music in the early 1960s.[[478]](#cite_note-500) Birmingham became known as the birthplace of [heavy metal](/wiki/Heavy_metal_music "Heavy metal music"), with the band [Black Sabbath](/wiki/Black_Sabbath "Black Sabbath") starting there in the 1960s.[[479]](#cite_note-501) In the following years, Britain took part in the development of [rock music](/wiki/Rock_music "Rock music"), with British acts pioneering [hard rock](/wiki/Hard_rock "Hard rock"),[[480]](#cite_note-502) [raga rock](/wiki/Raga_rock "Raga rock"), heavy metal,[[481]](#cite_note-503) [space rock](/wiki/Space_rock "Space rock"), [glam rock](/wiki/Glam_rock "Glam rock"),[[482]](#cite_note-504) [Gothic rock](/wiki/Gothic_rock "Gothic rock"),[[483]](#cite_note-505) [psychedelic rock](/wiki/Psychedelic_rock "Psychedelic rock")[[484]](#cite_note-506) and [punk rock](/wiki/Punk_rock "Punk rock").[[485]](#cite_note-507) British acts also developed [neo soul](/wiki/Neo_soul "Neo soul") and created [dubstep](/wiki/Dubstep "Dubstep").[[486]](#cite_note-508) The UK has produced some of the most prominent English-speaking [rappers](/wiki/Rappers "Rappers") along with the United States, including [Stormzy](/wiki/Stormzy "Stormzy"), [Kano](/wiki/Kano_(rapper) "Kano (rapper)"), [Yxng Bane](/wiki/Yxng_Bane "Yxng Bane"), [Ramz](/wiki/Ramz_(rapper) "Ramz (rapper)"), [Little Simz](/wiki/Little_Simz "Little Simz") and [Skepta](/wiki/Skepta "Skepta").[[487]](#cite_note-509)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/The_SSE_Hydro_arena_-_geograph.org.uk_-_6725739.jpg/250px-The_SSE_Hydro_arena_-_geograph.org.uk_-_6725739.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_SSE_Hydro_arena_-_geograph.org.uk_-_6725739.jpg)

The [OVO Hydro](/wiki/OVO_Hydro "OVO Hydro") in [Glasgow](/wiki/Glasgow "Glasgow"), Scotland, a 14,500-capacity arena consistently ranked within the top ten busiest venues worldwide[[488]](#cite_note-510)

The Beatles have international sales of over 1 billion units and are the [biggest-selling band](/wiki/List_of_best-selling_music_artists "List of best-selling music artists"), in addition to being widely regarded as the [most influential band in the history of popular music](/wiki/Cultural_impact_of_the_Beatles "Cultural impact of the Beatles").[[489]](#cite_note-Beatles_sales-511)[[490]](#cite_note-McCartney-512)[[491]](#cite_note-Guinness-513)[[492]](#cite_note-514) Other prominent British contributors to popular music over the last 50 years include the Rolling Stones, [Pink Floyd](/wiki/Pink_Floyd "Pink Floyd"), [Queen](/wiki/Queen_(band) "Queen (band)"), [Led Zeppelin](/wiki/Led_Zeppelin "Led Zeppelin"), the [Bee Gees](/wiki/Bee_Gees "Bee Gees") and [Elton John](/wiki/Elton_John "Elton John"), all of whom have worldwide record sales of 200 million or more.[[493]](#cite_note-515) The [Brit Awards](/wiki/Brit_Awards "Brit Awards") are the [BPI](/wiki/British_Phonographic_Industry "British Phonographic Industry")'s annual music awards, and some of the British recipients of the [Outstanding Contribution to Music](/wiki/Brit_Award_for_Outstanding_Contribution_to_Music "Brit Award for Outstanding Contribution to Music") award include [the Who](/wiki/The_Who "The Who"), [David Bowie](/wiki/David_Bowie "David Bowie"), [Eric Clapton](/wiki/Eric_Clapton "Eric Clapton"), [Rod Stewart](/wiki/Rod_Stewart "Rod Stewart"), [the Police](/wiki/The_Police "The Police") and [Fleetwood Mac](/wiki/Fleetwood_Mac "Fleetwood Mac") (who are a British-American band).[[494]](#cite_note-516) More recent UK music acts that have had international success include [George Michael](/wiki/George_Michael "George Michael"), [Oasis](/wiki/Oasis_(band) "Oasis (band)"), [Spice Girls](/wiki/Spice_Girls "Spice Girls"), [Radiohead](/wiki/Radiohead "Radiohead"), [Coldplay](/wiki/Coldplay "Coldplay"), [Arctic Monkeys](/wiki/Arctic_Monkeys "Arctic Monkeys"), [Robbie Williams](/wiki/Robbie_Williams "Robbie Williams"), [Amy Winehouse](/wiki/Amy_Winehouse "Amy Winehouse"), [Susan Boyle](/wiki/Susan_Boyle "Susan Boyle"), [Adele](/wiki/Adele "Adele"), [Ed Sheeran](/wiki/Ed_Sheeran "Ed Sheeran"), [Lewis Capaldi](/wiki/Lewis_Capaldi "Lewis Capaldi"), [One Direction](/wiki/One_Direction "One Direction"), [Harry Styles](/wiki/Harry_Styles "Harry Styles") and [Dua Lipa](/wiki/Dua_Lipa "Dua Lipa").[[495]](#cite_note-517) As of 2024 the UK is the world's [third-largest music market](/wiki/List_of_largest_music_markets "List of largest music markets") behind the US and Japan,[[496]](#cite_note-518) and in 2021 was the second-largest exporter of music behind the US.[[497]](#cite_note-519)

Many British cities are known for their music. Acts from Liverpool have had 54 UK chart number 1 hit singles, more per capita than any other city worldwide.[[498]](#cite_note-520) Glasgow's contribution was recognised in 2008 when it was named a [City of Music](/wiki/City_of_Music_(UNESCO) "City of Music (UNESCO)") by [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO").[[499]](#cite_note-521) Manchester played a role in the spread of dance music such as [acid house](/wiki/Acid_house "Acid house"), and from the mid-1990s, [Britpop](/wiki/Britpop "Britpop"). London and Bristol are closely associated with the origins of [electronic music](/wiki/Electronic_music "Electronic music") sub-genres such as [drum and bass](/wiki/Drum_and_bass "Drum and bass") and [trip hop](/wiki/Trip_hop "Trip hop").[[500]](#cite_note-522)

UK dance music traces its roots back to the black British [Sound System Culture](/wiki/Sound_system_(Jamaican) "Sound system (Jamaican)") and the [New Age Traveller](/wiki/New_Age_travellers "New Age travellers") movement of the 60s and 70s,[[501]](#cite_note-523) it also has influences from the [Chicago House](/wiki/House_music "House music") and [Detroit Techno](/wiki/Detroit_techno "Detroit techno") scenes. In the late 80's, dance music exploded with [Rave](/wiki/Rave "Rave") culture mainly [Acid House](/wiki/Acid_House "Acid House") tracks which were made mainstream with novelty records (such as Smart E's [Sesame's Treet](/wiki/Sesame%27s_Treet "Sesame's Treet") and the Prodigy's [Charly](/wiki/Charly_(song) "Charly (song)"))[[502]](#cite_note-524) and the [Balearic sound](/wiki/Balearic_beat "Balearic beat") brought back from the Ibiza club scene. This led on to genres such as [UK Garage](/wiki/UK_Garage "UK Garage"), [Speed Garage](/wiki/Speed_Garage "Speed Garage"), [Drum and bass](/wiki/Drum_and_bass "Drum and bass"), [Jungle](/wiki/Jungle_music "Jungle music"), [Trance](/wiki/Trance_music "Trance music"), and [Dubstep](/wiki/Dubstep "Dubstep"). Influential UK dance acts past and present include [808 State](/wiki/808_State "808 State"), [Orbital](/wiki/Orbital_(band) "Orbital (band)"), [the Prodigy](/wiki/The_Prodigy "The Prodigy"), [Underworld](/wiki/Underworld_(band) "Underworld (band)"), [Roni Size](/wiki/Roni_Size "Roni Size"), [Leftfield](/wiki/Leftfield "Leftfield"), [Massive Attack](/wiki/Massive_Attack "Massive Attack"), [Groove Armada](/wiki/Groove_Armada "Groove Armada"), [Fatboy Slim](/wiki/Fatboy_Slim "Fatboy Slim"), [Faithless](/wiki/Faithless "Faithless"), [Basement Jaxx](/wiki/Basement_Jaxx "Basement Jaxx"), [Chemical Brothers](/wiki/Chemical_Brothers "Chemical Brothers"), [Sub Focus](/wiki/Sub_Focus "Sub Focus"), [Chase & Status](/wiki/Chase_%26_Status "Chase & Status"), [Disclosure](/wiki/Disclosure_(band) "Disclosure (band)"), [Calvin Harris](/wiki/Calvin_Harris "Calvin Harris"), and [Fred Again](/wiki/Fred_Again "Fred Again").[[503]](#cite_note-525) Other influential UK DJs include [Judge Jules](/wiki/Judge_Jules "Judge Jules"), [Pete Tong](/wiki/Pete_Tong "Pete Tong"), [Carl Cox](/wiki/Carl_Cox "Carl Cox"), [Paul Oakenfold](/wiki/Paul_Oakenfold "Paul Oakenfold"), [John Digweed](/wiki/John_Digweed "John Digweed") and [Sasha](/wiki/Sasha_(dj) "Sasha (dj)").[[504]](#cite_note-526)

### Cinema

Main articles: [Cinema of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Cinema_of_the_United_Kingdom "Cinema of the United Kingdom") and [Independent cinema in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Independent_cinema_in_the_United_Kingdom "Independent cinema in the United Kingdom")

The United Kingdom has had a considerable influence on the history of the cinema. The British directors [Alfred Hitchcock](/wiki/Alfred_Hitchcock "Alfred Hitchcock"), whose film *[Vertigo](/wiki/Vertigo_(film) "Vertigo (film)")* is considered by some critics as the [best film of all time](/wiki/List_of_films_considered_the_best "List of films considered the best"),[[505]](#cite_note-527) and [David Lean](/wiki/David_Lean "David Lean"), who directed *[Lawrence of Arabia](/wiki/Lawrence_of_Arabia_(film) "Lawrence of Arabia (film)")*, are amongst the most critically acclaimed directors ever.[[506]](#cite_note-528) Recent popular directors include [Christopher Nolan](/wiki/Christopher_Nolan "Christopher Nolan"), [Sam Mendes](/wiki/Sam_Mendes "Sam Mendes"), [Steve McQueen](/wiki/Steve_McQueen_(director) "Steve McQueen (director)"), [Richard Curtis](/wiki/Richard_Curtis "Richard Curtis"), [Danny Boyle](/wiki/Danny_Boyle "Danny Boyle"), [Tony Scott](/wiki/Tony_Scott "Tony Scott") and [Ridley Scott](/wiki/Ridley_Scott "Ridley Scott").[[507]](#cite_note-529)[[508]](#cite_note-530)[[509]](#cite_note-531)[[510]](#cite_note-532) Many British actors have achieved international fame and critical success. Some of the most commercially successful films have been produced in the United Kingdom, including two of the [highest-grossing film franchises](/wiki/List_of_highest-grossing_film_series "List of highest-grossing film series") (*[Harry Potter](/wiki/Harry_Potter_(film_series) "Harry Potter (film series)")* and *[James Bond](/wiki/James_Bond_(film_series) "James Bond (film series)")*).[[511]](#cite_note-533)

2019 was a particularly good year for British films which grossed around £10.3 billion globally, accounting for 28.7 per cent of global box office revenue.[[512]](#cite_note-534) UK box-office takings totalled £1.25 billion in 2019, with around 176 million admissions.[[513]](#cite_note-535) In 2023 UK film and television studio stage space stood at 6.9 million sq ft, with 1 million sq ft added in the past year with more in development.[[514]](#cite_note-536) The annual [BAFTA Film Awards](/wiki/British_Academy_Film_Awards "British Academy Film Awards") are hosted by the [British Academy of Film and Television Arts](/wiki/British_Academy_of_Film_and_Television_Arts "British Academy of Film and Television Arts").[[515]](#cite_note-537)

### Cuisine

Main article: [British cuisine](/wiki/British_cuisine "British cuisine")

Further information: [English cuisine](/wiki/English_cuisine "English cuisine"), [Scottish cuisine](/wiki/Scottish_cuisine "Scottish cuisine"), [Welsh cuisine](/wiki/Welsh_cuisine "Welsh cuisine"), and [Northern Irish cuisine](/wiki/Northern_Irish_cuisine "Northern Irish cuisine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Fish%2C_chips_and_mushy_peas.jpg/250px-Fish%2C_chips_and_mushy_peas.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fish,_chips_and_mushy_peas.jpg)

[Fish and chips](/wiki/Fish_and_chips "Fish and chips"), a traditional British dish, served with lemon, tartar sauce and mushy peas

British cuisine developed from influences reflective of its land, settlements, [arrivals of new settlers and immigrants](/wiki/Immigration_to_the_United_Kingdom "Immigration to the United Kingdom"), trade and colonialism. The food of England has historically been characterised by simplicity of approach and a reliance on the high quality of natural produce.[[516]](#cite_note-538) The traditional [Sunday roast](/wiki/Sunday_roast "Sunday roast") is one example, featuring a [roast joint](/wiki/Roasting "Roasting") (usually of beef), lamb, chicken, or pork, often [free range](/wiki/Free_range "Free range") (and generally grass-fed, in the case of beef). Roasts are served with either roasted or boiled vegetables, [Yorkshire pudding](/wiki/Yorkshire_pudding "Yorkshire pudding") and [gravy](/wiki/Gravy "Gravy"). Other traditional meals include [meat pies](/wiki/Meat_pie "Meat pie") and [stews](/wiki/Stew "Stew"). A poll by [YouGov](/wiki/YouGov "YouGov") in 2019 rated classic British food, with more than 80 per cent liking the Sunday roast, Yorkshire pudding, [fish and chips](/wiki/Fish_and_chips "Fish and chips"), [crumpets](/wiki/Crumpet "Crumpet") and the [full English breakfast](/wiki/Full_English_breakfast "Full English breakfast").[[517]](#cite_note-539)

The UK is home to a large selection of [fine dining](/wiki/Fine_dining "Fine dining"). The nation hosts multiple restaurant guides such as *[The Good Food Guide](/wiki/The_Good_Food_Guide "The Good Food Guide")* and *[Harden's](/wiki/Harden%27s "Harden's")*. In 2025 there were 197 restaurants with a [Michelin Star](/wiki/Michelin_Star "Michelin Star"); [ten of which have received a three-star rating](/wiki/List_of_Michelin_3-star_restaurants_in_the_United_Kingdom "List of Michelin 3-star restaurants in the United Kingdom").[[518]](#cite_note-540) Sweet foods are common within British cuisine, and there is a long list of [British desserts](/wiki/List_of_British_desserts "List of British desserts"). Afternoon tea is a light afternoon meal served with tea in tea rooms and hotels around the United Kingdom, with the tradition dating back to around 1840.[[519]](#cite_note-541) A poll from July 2024 revealed that 3 per cent of the UK population follow a [vegan](/wiki/Vegan "Vegan") diet, 6 per cent are [vegetarian](/wiki/Vegetarian "Vegetarian"), and 13 per cent identify as [flexitarian](/wiki/Flexitarian "Flexitarian") (following a mainly vegetarian diet).[[520]](#cite_note-542) The British Empire facilitated knowledge of [Indian cuisine](/wiki/Indian_cuisine "Indian cuisine") with its "strong, penetrating spices and herbs". British cuisine has absorbed the cultural influence of those who have settled in Britain, producing hybrid dishes, such as [chicken tikka masala](/wiki/Chicken_tikka_masala "Chicken tikka masala").[[521]](#cite_note-543) The British have embraced world cuisine and regularly eat recipes or fast food from other European countries, the Caribbean and Asia.

The UK has many [gastropubs](/wiki/Gastropub "Gastropub") and is the birthplace of many alcoholic drinks including several beer styles such as [pale ale](/wiki/Pale_ale "Pale ale"), [India pale ale](/wiki/India_pale_ale "India pale ale"), [bitter](/wiki/Bitter_(beer) "Bitter (beer)"), [brown ale](/wiki/Brown_ale "Brown ale"), [porter](/wiki/Porter_(beer) "Porter (beer)"), and [stout](/wiki/Stout "Stout"). The number of [craft beers](/wiki/Craft_beer "Craft beer") and microbreweries has expanded rapidly in the last two decades.[[522]](#cite_note-544) Other popular alcoholic drinks produced in the UK include [Scotch whisky](/wiki/Scotch_whisky "Scotch whisky"), [English wine](/wiki/Wine_from_the_United_Kingdom "Wine from the United Kingdom"), [gin](/wiki/Gin "Gin"), [perry](/wiki/Perry "Perry") and [cider](/wiki/Cider "Cider").

### Sport

Main articles: [Sport in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Sport_in_the_United_Kingdom "Sport in the United Kingdom") and [Great Britain at the Olympics](/wiki/Great_Britain_at_the_Olympics "Great Britain at the Olympics")

Further information: [Sport in England](/wiki/Sport_in_England "Sport in England"), [Sport in Scotland](/wiki/Sport_in_Scotland "Sport in Scotland"), [Sport in Wales](/wiki/Sport_in_Wales "Sport in Wales"), and [Sport in Northern Ireland](/wiki/Sport_in_Northern_Ireland "Sport in Northern Ireland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/City_vs_United_FA_Cup_final_2023.jpg/250px-City_vs_United_FA_Cup_final_2023.jpg)](/wiki/File:City_vs_United_FA_Cup_final_2023.jpg)

The [2023 FA Cup final](/wiki/2023_FA_Cup_final "2023 FA Cup final") at [Wembley Stadium](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium "Wembley Stadium") between [Manchester City](/wiki/Manchester_City "Manchester City") and [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United "Manchester United")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/Old_18th_tee_lr.jpg/250px-Old_18th_tee_lr.jpg)](/wiki/File:Old_18th_tee_lr.jpg)

Golf originated from the [Old Course at St Andrews](/wiki/Old_Course_at_St_Andrews "Old Course at St Andrews") in Scotland.

[Association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football"), [tennis](/wiki/Tennis "Tennis"), [table tennis](/wiki/Table_tennis "Table tennis"), [badminton](/wiki/Badminton "Badminton"), [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union"), [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league"), [rugby sevens](/wiki/Rugby_sevens "Rugby sevens"), [golf](/wiki/Golf "Golf"), [boxing](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing"), [netball](/wiki/Netball "Netball"), [water polo](/wiki/Water_polo "Water polo"), [field hockey](/wiki/Field_hockey "Field hockey"), [billiards](/wiki/English_billiards "English billiards"), [darts](/wiki/Darts "Darts"), [rowing](/wiki/Rowing_(sport) "Rowing (sport)"), [rounders](/wiki/Rounders "Rounders") and [cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket") originated or were substantially developed in the UK, with the rules and codes of many modern sports invented and codified in the Victorian era.[[w]](#cite_note-547)

A poll in 2003 found that football is the most popular sport in the UK.[[525]](#cite_note-sports_poll-548) England is recognised by [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA") as the birthplace of club football, and the [Football Association](/wiki/Football_Association "Football Association") is the oldest of its kind, with the [rules of football](/wiki/Laws_of_the_Game_(association_football) "Laws of the Game (association football)") first drafted in 1863 by [Ebenezer Cobb Morley](/wiki/Ebenezer_Cobb_Morley "Ebenezer Cobb Morley").[[526]](#cite_note-549) Each of the [Home Nations](/wiki/Home_Nations "Home Nations") (England, Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland) has its own football association, national team and [league system](/wiki/League_system "League system"), and each is individually a governing member of the [International Football Association Board](/wiki/International_Football_Association_Board "International Football Association Board") alongside FIFA. The English top division, the [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League"), is the most watched football league in the world.[[527]](#cite_note-550) The first international football match was contested by [England](/wiki/England_national_football_team "England national football team") and [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland_national_football_team "Scotland national football team") on 30 November 1872.[[528]](#cite_note-551) England, Scotland, [Wales](/wiki/Wales_national_football_team "Wales national football team") and [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_national_football_team "Northern Ireland national football team") usually compete as separate countries in international competitions.[[529]](#cite_note-552)

In 2003 rugby union was ranked the second-most-popular sport in the UK.[[525]](#cite_note-sports_poll-548) The sport was created in [Rugby School](/wiki/Rugby_School "Rugby School"), Warwickshire, and the [first rugby international](/wiki/1871_England_versus_Scotland_rugby_union_match "1871 England versus Scotland rugby union match") took place on 27 March 1871 between [England](/wiki/England_national_rugby_union_team "England national rugby union team") and [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland_national_rugby_union_team "Scotland national rugby union team").[[530]](#cite_note-553)[[531]](#cite_note-554) England, Scotland, [Wales](/wiki/Wales_national_rugby_union_team "Wales national rugby union team"), [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland_national_rugby_union_team "Ireland national rugby union team"), [France](/wiki/France_national_rugby_union_team "France national rugby union team") and [Italy](/wiki/Italy_national_rugby_union_team "Italy national rugby union team") compete in the [Six Nations Championship](/wiki/Six_Nations_Championship "Six Nations Championship"), the premier international rugby union tournament in the northern hemisphere. [Sports governing bodies](/wiki/Sports_governing_bodies "Sports governing bodies") in [England](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_England "Rugby union in England"), [Scotland](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Scotland "Rugby union in Scotland"), [Wales](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Wales "Rugby union in Wales") and [Ireland](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Ireland "Rugby union in Ireland") organise and regulate the game separately.[[532]](#cite_note-555) Every four years the Home Nations make a combined team known as the [British and Irish Lions](/wiki/British_and_Irish_Lions "British and Irish Lions") which tours Australia, New Zealand and South Africa.

The United Kingdom hosted the [Summer Olympic Games](/wiki/Summer_Olympic_Games "Summer Olympic Games") in [1908](/wiki/1908_Summer_Olympics "1908 Summer Olympics"), [1948](/wiki/1948_Summer_Olympics "1948 Summer Olympics") and [2012](/wiki/2012_Summer_Olympics "2012 Summer Olympics"), with London acting as the host city on all three occasions. Birmingham hosted the [2022 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/2022_Commonwealth_Games "2022 Commonwealth Games"), the seventh time a country of the United Kingdom hosted the [Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games "Commonwealth Games") (England, Scotland and Wales have each hosted the Commonwealth Games at least once).[[533]](#cite_note-556)

### Symbols

Main article: [Symbols of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Symbols_of_the_United_Kingdom,_the_Channel_Islands_and_the_Isle_of_Man "Symbols of the United Kingdom, the Channel Islands and the Isle of Man")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Queen_Elizabeth_II_Platinum_Jubilee_2022_-_Platinum_Pageant_%2852123378222%29.jpg/250px-Queen_Elizabeth_II_Platinum_Jubilee_2022_-_Platinum_Pageant_%2852123378222%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Queen_Elizabeth_II_Platinum_Jubilee_2022_-_Platinum_Pageant_(52123378222).jpg)

Union Jack flags on [The Mall](/wiki/The_Mall,_London "The Mall, London"), London

The [flag of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Flag_of_the_United_Kingdom "Flag of the United Kingdom") is the [Union Flag](/wiki/Union_Flag "Union Flag"), which is also referred to as the Union Jack.[[534]](#cite_note-557) It was created in 1606 by the superimposition of the [flag of England](/wiki/Flag_of_England "Flag of England"), representing [Saint George](/wiki/Saint_George "Saint George"), on the [flag of Scotland](/wiki/Flag_of_Scotland "Flag of Scotland"), representing [Saint Andrew](/wiki/Saint_Andrew "Saint Andrew"), and was updated in 1801 with the addition of [Saint Patrick's Flag](/wiki/Saint_Patrick%27s_Flag "Saint Patrick's Flag").[[535]](#cite_note-558) Wales is not represented in the Union Flag, as Wales had been conquered and [annexed](/wiki/Annexed "Annexed") to England prior to the formation of the United Kingdom. The possibility of redesigning the Union Flag to include representation of Wales was discussed in 2007.[[536]](#cite_note-559) The [national anthem](/wiki/National_anthem "National anthem") of the United Kingdom is "[God Save the King](/wiki/God_Save_the_King "God Save the King")", with "King" replaced with "Queen" in the lyrics whenever the monarch is a woman.

[Britannia](/wiki/Britannia "Britannia") is a [national personification](/wiki/National_personification "National personification") of the United Kingdom, originating from [Roman Britain](/wiki/Roman_Britain "Roman Britain").[[537]](#cite_note-560) Beside [The Lion and the Unicorn](/wiki/The_Lion_and_the_Unicorn "The Lion and the Unicorn") and the [dragon](/wiki/Welsh_Dragon "Welsh Dragon") of heraldry, the [bulldog](/wiki/Bulldog "Bulldog") is an iconic animal and commonly represented with the Union Flag.[[538]](#cite_note-561) A rare personification is a character originating in the 18th century, [John Bull](/wiki/John_Bull "John Bull").[[539]](#cite_note-562)

[England](/wiki/National_symbols_of_England "National symbols of England"), [Wales](/wiki/National_symbols_of_Wales "National symbols of Wales") and [Scotland](/wiki/National_symbols_of_Scotland "National symbols of Scotland") each have their own national symbols, including their national flags. [Northern Ireland](/wiki/National_symbols_of_Ireland,_the_Republic_of_Ireland_and_Northern_Ireland "National symbols of Ireland, the Republic of Ireland and Northern Ireland") also has symbols, many of which are shared with the [Republic of Ireland](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland "Republic of Ireland").

## Notes

1. **[^](#cite_ref-2)** "God Save the King" is the [national anthem](/wiki/National_anthem "National anthem") by custom, not statute, and there is no authorised version. Typically only the first verse is usually sung, although the second verse is also often sung as well at state and public events.[[1]](#cite_note-1) The words *King, he, him, his*, used at present, are replaced by *Queen, she, her* when the monarch is female.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-5)** Scots, Ulster Scots, Welsh, Cornish, Scottish Gaelic and Irish are classed as [regional](/wiki/Regional_language "Regional language") or [minority](/wiki/Minority_language "Minority language") languages under the [Council of Europe](/wiki/Council_of_Europe "Council of Europe")'s [European Charter for Regional or Minority Languages](/wiki/European_Charter_for_Regional_or_Minority_Languages "European Charter for Regional or Minority Languages").[[2]](#cite_note-reglang-3) These include defined obligations to promote those languages.[[3]](#cite_note-4) See also [Languages of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Languages_of_the_United_Kingdom "Languages of the United Kingdom"). Welsh has limited *[de jure](/wiki/De_jure "De jure")* official status in Wales, as well as in the provision of national government services provided for Wales.
3. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-Census2021/22_6-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-Census2021/22_6-1) [***c***](#cite_ref-Census2021/22_6-2) Scotland held its census a year later after England, Wales and Northern Ireland due to the COVID-19 pandemic. As a result, the data shown is from two separate years.
4. **[^](#cite_ref-15)** Although the United Kingdom has traditionally been seen as a [unitary state](/wiki/Unitary_state "Unitary state"), an alternative description of the UK as a "union state", put forward by, amongst others, [Vernon Bogdanor](/wiki/Vernon_Bogdanor "Vernon Bogdanor"),[[9]](#cite_note-12) has become increasingly influential since the adoption of devolution in the 1990s.[[10]](#cite_note-13) A union state is considered to differ from a unitary state in that while it maintains a central authority it also recognises the authority of historic rights and infrastructures of its component parts.[[11]](#cite_note-14)
5. **[^](#cite_ref-ONSLandArea_16-0)** [ONS](/wiki/Office_for_National_Statistics "Office for National Statistics") Standard Area Measurement, "area to mean high water excluding inland water". Excludes the [Crown Dependencies](/wiki/Crown_Dependencies "Crown Dependencies") and [British Overseas Territories](/wiki/British_Overseas_Territories "British Overseas Territories").
6. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-ONSArea_17-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-ONSArea_17-1) [***c***](#cite_ref-ONSArea_17-2) [ONS](/wiki/Office_for_National_Statistics "Office for National Statistics") Standard Area Measurement, 'area to mean high water'. Excludes the [Crown Dependencies](/wiki/Crown_Dependencies "Crown Dependencies") and [British Overseas Territories](/wiki/British_Overseas_Territories "British Overseas Territories").
7. **[^](#cite_ref-26)** Some of the devolved countries, Crown Dependencies and British Overseas Territories issue their own sterling banknotes or currencies, or use another nation's currency. See [List of British currencies](/wiki/List_of_British_currencies "List of British currencies").
8. **[^](#cite_ref-27)** Also observed by the [Crown Dependencies](/wiki/Crown_Dependencies "Crown Dependencies"). For further information, see [Time in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Time_in_the_United_Kingdom "Time in the United Kingdom").
9. **[^](#cite_ref-29)** The UK Government uses the [ISO 8601](/wiki/ISO_8601 "ISO 8601") format, yyyy-mm-dd, for machine-readable dates and times.[[20]](#cite_note-28) See [Date and time notation in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Date_and_time_notation_in_the_United_Kingdom "Date and time notation in the United Kingdom").
10. **[^](#cite_ref-30)** Excludes most overseas territories. See [Telephone numbers in the United Kingdom#Telephone numbers in Overseas Territories](/wiki/Telephone_numbers_in_the_United_Kingdom#Telephone_numbers_in_Overseas_Territories "Telephone numbers in the United Kingdom").
11. **[^](#cite_ref-31)** The [.gb](/wiki/.gb ".gb") domain is also reserved for the UK, but has been little used.
12. **[^](#cite_ref-32)** Except two overseas territories: [Gibraltar](/wiki/Gibraltar "Gibraltar") and the [British Indian Ocean Territory](/wiki/British_Indian_Ocean_Territory "British Indian Ocean Territory").
13. **[^](#cite_ref-36)** Usage is mixed. *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")*[[21]](#cite_note-33) and *[The Telegraph](/wiki/The_Daily_Telegraph "The Daily Telegraph")*[[22]](#cite_note-34) use *Britain* as a synonym for the United Kingdom. The British [Cabinet Office](/wiki/Cabinet_Office "Cabinet Office")'s [Government Digital Service](/wiki/Government_Digital_Service "Government Digital Service") style guide[[23]](#cite_note-35) for use on [gov.uk](/wiki/Gov.uk "Gov.uk") recommends: "Use UK and United Kingdom in preference to Britain and British (UK business, UK foreign policy, ambassador and high commissioner). But British embassy, not UK embassy."
14. **[^](#cite_ref-37)** The [Isle of Man](/wiki/Isle_of_Man "Isle of Man"), [Guernsey](/wiki/Bailiwick_of_Guernsey "Bailiwick of Guernsey") and [Jersey](/wiki/Jersey "Jersey") are [Crown Dependencies](/wiki/Crown_Dependencies "Crown Dependencies") and not part of the UK.
15. **[^](#cite_ref-39)** The United Kingdom does not have a codified constitution but an unwritten one formed of Acts of Parliament, court judgments, traditions, and conventions.[[24]](#cite_note-38)
16. **[^](#cite_ref-40)** Compare to section 1 of both of the 1800 [Acts of Union](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_(1800) "Acts of Union (1800)") which reads: "the kingdoms of Great Britain and Ireland shall ... be united into one kingdom, by the name of the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland".
17. **[^](#cite_ref-56)** "UK" embassies became "British" embassies in 1961.[[39]](#cite_note-55)
18. **[^](#cite_ref-63)** Historically, the term *British* was used to refer to members and institutions within the British Empire and later Commonwealth and was not limited to the geographical British Isles. The UK Government adopted the term for its exclusive use only in 1961, but in recognition of its wider usage first sought the prior consent of Australia, Canada and New Zealand.[[44]](#cite_note-61)[[45]](#cite_note-62)
19. **[^](#cite_ref-186)** British [sovereignty](/wiki/Sovereignty "Sovereignty") derives from the Crown, a [corporation sole](/wiki/Corporation_sole "Corporation sole") occupied by the monarch. It is therefore by and through the monarch that Parliament exercises supreme legislative authority over both the executive and judiciary. Distinguished Professor of [Public Law](/wiki/Public_Law "Public Law") Maurice Sunkin opined the Crown symbolically occupies "...what in other places would be a core element of a written constitution."[[166]](#cite_note-184) As a result of this state of constitutional affairs, the monarch is formally referred to as "[the Sovereign](/wiki/Sovereign "Sovereign")" in legislation.[[167]](#cite_note-185)
20. **[^](#cite_ref-194)** For instance, the monarch alone appoints the prime minister and confers [state honours](/wiki/Orders,_decorations,_and_medals_of_the_United_Kingdom "Orders, decorations, and medals of the United Kingdom") in the personal gift of the Crown. When necessary, the monarch may also refuse a [dissolution](/wiki/Dissolution_of_Parliament "Dissolution of Parliament") or [prorogation of Parliament](/wiki/Prorogation_in_the_United_Kingdom "Prorogation in the United Kingdom"), withhold [royal assent](/wiki/Royal_assent "Royal assent") to [primary legislation](/wiki/Act_of_Parliament_(UK) "Act of Parliament (UK)"), and prevent illegal use of the [British Armed Forces](/wiki/British_Armed_Forces "British Armed Forces"), amongst other reserve powers.[[174]](#cite_note-193)
21. **[^](#cite_ref-315)** Car brands here are classed as British based on several of the following criteria: historical heritage, cultural significance, design and engineering base, manufacturing location, headquarters location, UK registered company (even with overseas investors).
22. **[^](#cite_ref-473)** Berkeley is in fact Irish but was called a 'British empiricist' due to the territory of what is now known as the [Republic of Ireland](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland "Republic of Ireland") being in the UK at the time.
23. **[^](#cite_ref-547)** In 2012, the president of the IOC, [Jacques Rogge](/wiki/Jacques_Rogge "Jacques Rogge"), stated, "This great, sports-loving country is widely recognised as the birthplace of modern sport. It was here that the concepts of sportsmanship and fair play were first codified into clear rules and regulations. It was here that sport was included as an educational tool in the school curriculum."[[523]](#cite_note-545)[[524]](#cite_note-546)

For example, the Scottish Parliament is empowered to enact primary legislation on all matters, save those in relation to which competence is explicitly denied ... but this power to legislate on what may be termed "devolved matters" is concurrent with the Westminster Parliament's general power to legislate for Scotland on any matter at all, including devolved matters ... "The UK Internal Market Act gives ministers sweeping powers to enforce mutual recognition and non-discrimination across the four jurisdictions. Existing differences and some social and health matters are exempted but these are much less extensive than the exemptions permitted under the EU Internal Market provisions. Only after an amendment in the House of Lords, the Bill was amended to provide a weak and non-binding consent mechanism for amendments (equivalent to the Sewel Convention) to the list of exemptions. The result is that, while the devolved governments retain regulatory competences, these are undermined by the fact that goods and services originating in, or imported into, England can be marketed anywhere."
